- Distinguish clearly between literature-backed elements and your proposed adaptation.
- Do not include equipment or materials not supported by the BOM unless you put them under "Missing Capability / Assumption".
- If the BOM is insufficient, say so explicitly.
- Cite source papers by exact title from the retrieved cards.

available_bom.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric laser power levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a single “power-gradient” NiTi DED coupon that contains five adjacent stripes (or stacked bands) deposited at five laser power levels spanning the validated stable window. Maintain constant scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, and shielding gas flow. Section each stripe, mount, grind/polish, and image cross-sections by SEM to quantify porosity (area fraction and pore morphology). Output is porosity vs. laser power and identification of lack-of-fusion vs. keyhole-like pores as power changes.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps (DED build under argon, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, SEM imaging) are directly supported by the available materials and equipment.
  - No heat treatment, advanced metrology, or non-SEM analytics are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: 
    - Use of argon shielding during DED of NiTi.
    - Existence of a validated stable operating window for laser power.
    - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Single-build, multi-power “gradient” coupon: print multiple side-by-side stripes (or layer-wise bands) at different laser powers within one build to reduce build-to-build variability and conserve powder, while enabling direct comparison under identical environmental and thermal boundary conditions.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power at five levels evenly spaced within the validated stable window reported in the literature (e.g., P1 < P2 < P3 < P4 < P5; all inside the stable window).
  - Hold constant:
    - Scan speed (e.g., one fixed value typical for NiTi in DED)
    - Hatch spacing (constant value)
    - Layer thickness (constant value)
    - Shielding gas flow/composition (argon, fixed flow rate)
    - Powder feed rate (fixed)
    - Build path (same stripe width/length; identical infill strategy)
  - Replication: At least two replicate stripes per power level within the same coupon, or print two identical coupons.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections from mid-length of each stripe/band.
  - Quantify:
    - Porosity area fraction (% area) per stripe using SEM image thresholding or grid-based point counting.
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circular diameter) and pore morphology classification (lack-of-fusion irregular pores vs. near-spherical keyhole-like pores) by visual/shape metrics.
  - Plot porosity metrics vs. laser power; identify the power range minimizing porosity and the onset of keyhole-like porosity.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Exceeding the upper end of the stable laser power can cause excessive keyholing, spatter, or composition shifts (Ni evaporation) leading to erratic porosity or build defects that prevent completing the gradient coupon. Mitigation: keep all powers within the validated stable window and verify melt-track stability on a sacrificial substrate before the main build.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the SEM workstation provides basic area measurement/thresholding tools; if not, manual grid point counting will be used. No other missing capabilities identified.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

available_bom.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric scan speed levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
A two-part DED study on NiTi where scan speed is swept within the established stable window to quantify (a) single-track width from top-view optical micrographs and (b) multi-layer wall build height by calipers. For each scan speed, deposit: (1) a 40–50 mm single track (1 layer) for width measurement, and (2) a 10-layer, 40–50 mm single-bead wall with fixed Z-increment for height measurement. Hold laser power, powder feed rate, spot size, shielding gas flow, and standoff distance constant across all runs.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All fabrication and metrology steps use the DED machine, optical microscope, and digital calipers listed. Argon gas provides shielding. No additional instruments are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From “paper_003_chunk_01”: 
- Operate within a validated scan-speed window for stable NiTi DED.
- Use top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
- Separate geometries tailored to available metrology: single-layer beads for accurate top-view width, multi-layer walls to amplify height for caliper resolution.
- Report height-per-layer versus scan speed as a deposition efficiency proxy, and correlate with linear energy density P/(v·spot) computed from fixed power and spot with varying scan speed.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine
- Optical microscope
- Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder
- Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Primary sweep: scan speed (select 5–7 evenly spaced values within the validated stable window used in your NiTi DED setup).
- Held constant: laser power, powder feed rate, spot size, standoff, argon flow, layer Z-increment (for walls), track length, inter-layer dwell.

- Measurements / Outputs:
- Single-track width (mean and std) from top-view optical images at multiple positions along each track.
- 10-layer wall height via calipers at three positions; compute average height and height-per-layer.
- Qualitative stability indicators (bead continuity, balling, spatter).
- Derived: linear energy density for each condition; correlations: width vs speed, height-per-layer vs speed, and width vs height-per-layer.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
- Single-bead height is below caliper resolution; mitigated by using 10-layer walls.
- Out-of-window speeds may yield instability (balling or lack of fusion); mitigated by staying within the validated window and aborting runs that show severe instability.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Assumes availability of compatible flat substrate coupons (e.g., NiTi or steel) sized for the DED machine; not listed in BOM. If unavailable, the experiment cannot proceed as described.
- Assumes the DED machine can fix

available_bom.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how solution treatment temperature affects retained austenite fraction in DED NiTi coupons.

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_004_chunk_01
DOI: 

DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how solution treatment temperature affects retained austenite fraction in DED NiTi coupons.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric solution treatment temperatures.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

evidence.text: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build identical DED NiTi coupons, then perform vacuum-furnace solution treatments at a set of temperatures, holding time constant and using a standardized argon backfill cool, followed by XRD-based quantification of retained austenite fraction versus solution treatment temperature.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - Coupons can be fabricated from NiTi powder using the DED machine under argon shielding.
  - Solution treatments can be performed in the available vacuum furnace with controlled temperature setpoints.
  - Argon gas can be used to backfill and standardize cooling rate after the hold.
  - XRD is available to quantify retained austenite fraction after heat treatment.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_004_chunk_01”: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Apply a controlled series of solution treatment temperatures (e.g., 750, 800, 850, 900, 950 C) with identical hold time (e.g., 30 minutes) and identical argon backfill cool conditions to isolate the effect of temperature alone on retained austenite fraction in DED coupons built with a single fixed parameter set.
  - Maintain constant DED build parameters and coupon geometry to minimize composition/texture variability, focusing the study strictly on post-build solution temperature.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Vacuum furnace capable of controlled setpoint holds and argon backfill cooling
  - XRD instrument

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Solution treatment temperature: target 5 levels, e.g., 750, 800, 850, 900, 950 C
  - Fixed parameters (held constant across all conditions):
    - DED build: laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, shielding argon flow, and coupon size (e.g., 10 × 10 × 5 mm); select values that reliably produce dense coupons on your machine.
    - Heat treatment hold time: 30 minutes at temperature
    - Furnace atmosphere: high vacuum to heat, argon backfill to a fixed pressure (e.g., 1 atm) at end of hold
    - Cooling protocol: initiate argon backfill immediately upon completion of the hold; allow cooling to <200 C under argon before removal

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - XRD phase quantification of retained austenite fraction for each temperature condition
    - Collect scans over the NiTi B2 and B19′ peak regions with identical instrument settings for all samples
    - Use integrated peak intensity ratios or whole-pattern fitting to estimate austenite fraction; report relative changes across temperatures
  - Secondary XRD outputs: peak positions/strain indicators to check for lattice parameter shifts consistent with composition or residual stress changes

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Nickel loss during DED or oxidation during heat treatment could alter composition and confound the temperature effect on austenite fraction. Mitigation: use conservative DED energy density and robust argon shielding; heat treat under high vacuum with minimal time at peak; apply identical conditions to all samples.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the vacuum furnace can backfill with argon promptly and reproducibly to standardize cooling rate.
  - Assumes access to XRD quantitative analysis (e.g., peak fitting or Rietveld) software/workflow; if not available, use consistent integrated intensity ratios for relative comparisons.
  - Assumes adequate furnace thermometry and uniformity (±5 C) at setpoint.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_004_chunk_01

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.

task.evidence_summary: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

evidence.text: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a series of 10 × 10 × 5 mm NiTi pads by DED while sweeping hatch spacing, then perform cross-sectional Vickers microhardness mapping to quantify how hatch spacing influences hardness distribution through the build.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps use only the provided DED machine, argon shielding, NiTi powder, and standard metallography plus a microhardness tester. Cross-sectional polishing and hardness mapping are explicitly supported by the listed tools.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From "paper_005_chunk_01": Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window.
  - From "paper_005_chunk_01": Polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Apply a controlled, monotonic sweep of hatch spacing spanning high-overlap to near-no-overlap conditions in otherwise fixed process settings, and perform dense hardness grids to capture both average hardness and spatial gradients (top-to-bottom and edge-to-center). Include replicate builds per spacing to separate process variability from spacing effects.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Microhardness tester
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Standard grinding papers and polishing media (as included with the kit)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Hatch spacing (µm): 80, 120, 160, 200 µm
  - Hold constant across all pads:
    - Laser power: 250 W (example constant within a stable window)
    - Scan speed: 600 mm/min
    - Layer thickness: 50 µm
    - Spot size: fixed by machine optics
    - Shielding gas: Argon, constant flow per machine recommendation
    - Scan strategy: Bidirectional raster, 90° rotation between layers
    - Substrate and preheat: Use standard machine substrate, no change between builds
  - Replicates: n = 2 pads per hatch spacing to assess repeatability

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Prepare one cross-section per pad (cut normal to scan direction, mid-pad).
  - Mount, grind, and polish to a mirror finish (no etch required).
  - Vickers microhardness mapping:
    - Load: 200 gf (or the nearest available low-load on the tester), 10 s dwell
    - Grid: 10 × 8 indents, 200 µm spacing, covering from top surface to base and from edge to center
    - Exclude indents that intersect pores or edges; re-place nearby if needed
  - Outputs:
    - Mean and standard deviation of hardness per pad
    - Hardness profiles vs build height (top, mid, bottom rows)
    - Edge-to-center hardness gradients
    - ANOVA/regression of hardness vs hatch spacing
    - Qualitative notes on surface integrity and any visible lack-of-fusion in polished cross-sections observed under the microhardness scope

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Very large hatch spacing (e.g., 200 µm) may induce lack-of-fusion, causing scattered hardness or invalid indents due to porosity. Mitigation: stay within spacing values that the machine can reliably fuse for NiTi; if an condition produces extensive voids, document and exclude from statistical averaging while reporting as process limit.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED machine can stably hold the listed power, speed, and hatch spacing and provide adequate argon shielding.
  - Assumes the microhardness tester supports low-load Vickers (around 100–500 gf) and is calibrated.
  - No etchants are listed; the plan relies on polished (unetched) hardness mapping as allowed by the literature card.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01

The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric laser power levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Build a single “power-gradient” NiTi DED wall coupon in argon where laser power is stepped every 5 layers across 7 levels (from slightly below, within, and slightly above the validated stable operating window), keeping all other parameters constant. Section the wall at each power zone, mount, grind/polish, and use SEM cross-sections to quantify porosity fraction, pore size distribution, and pore morphology versus laser power.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All required steps—DED deposition under argon shielding, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM cross-section imaging—are explicitly supported by the listed equipment and materials.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
• Argon shielding during DED of NiTi and operating within a validated stable laser power window.  
• Post-build sample preparation (sectioning, mounting, grinding, polishing) for SEM cross-sections to quantify porosity.  
Source: “paper_002_chunk_01”

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Implement a single-build “stepped-power gradient” coupon to minimize build-to-build variability: deposit a straight wall (~30 mm long, ~5–8 mm tall) where laser power is incremented after every 5 layers across 7 preset levels. This allows direct, same-coupon comparison of porosity versus power, reducing confounding factors (powder lot, ambient changes).

- Needed Equipment:
• DED machine  
• SEM  
• Precision saw  
• Mounting press  
• Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
• NiTi powder  
• Argon gas  
• Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
• Primary sweep: Laser power at 7 levels (e.g., P1 < window low, P2–P6 within window, P7 > window high; exact values chosen based on machine capability and the validated window reported in the literature).  
• Held constant to isolate power effect: scan speed, hatch spacing (if using thin wall with overlapping tracks), layer thickness, powder feed rate, spot size, argon flow rate/nozzle standoff, and substrate preheat (if available, keep off or fixed).

- Measurements / Outputs:
• From SEM cross-sections taken at the mid-length of each power zone:
  - Porosity area fraction (%) via image thresholding
  - Pore size distribution and morphology (rounded/keyhole vs irregular/lack-of-fusion)
  - Melt pool width and penetration depth indicators from etched contrast (if visible after polish)
• Plot porosity metrics versus laser power; annotate observed transition from lack-of-fusion (low power) to keyhole porosity (high power), if present.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
At powers above the stable window, excessive keyholing or distortion may collapse the wall or create unusable sections; at too-low powers, lack-of-fusion may prevent build continuity. Mitigation: start with a pilot on three power levels to confirm buildability, then execute the full 7-level gradient.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
• Assumes access to basic image analysis software for porosity quantification from SEM micrographs.  
• No in-situ melt pool monitoring or oxygen sensor; assume adequate argon shielding per standard DED practice.

- Source Papers Used:
• paper_002_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric laser power levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build single-bead, multi-layer (SBML) NiTi walls using DED under argon shielding while sweeping laser power within the validated stable window, then section, mount, grind/polish, and perform SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity (area fraction, pore size distribution, and pore morphology). Keep all parameters except laser power constant.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  The BOM includes NiTi powder, argon gas for shielding, and a DED machine to fabricate SBML walls. Post-build sectioning, mounting, grinding, polishing, and SEM are all covered by the listed equipment and consumables (mounting resin, precision saw, mounting press, grinding/polishing kit, SEM). No additional metrology beyond SEM imaging is required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: Use argon shielding; operate within a validated stable laser power window; prepare cross-sections by sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing; quantify porosity via SEM cross-section imaging.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Use an SBML geometry (single track stacked into a wall) to isolate the effect of laser power on lack-of-fusion vs keyhole porosity without the confounding of hatch spacing. This reduces variables to power while keeping scan speed, layer height, and track overlap fixed (overlap = 0 because it’s a single bead).
  - Include a “power ladder” variant as an optional add-on: a single tall SBML wall where power is stepped every N layers within the same coupon to minimize between-part variability. This can complement the primary design of separate SBML coupons at fixed powers.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine (with argon shielding capability)
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Laser power: choose three levels within the validated stable window reported in the literature (e.g., Low, Mid, High corresponding to approximately 60%, 80%, 100% of the center of that stable window). Exact wattage setpoints should be chosen based on the machine’s validated window.
  - Hold constant across all builds:
    - Scan speed (fixed)
    - Powder feed rate (fixed)
    - Layer height (fixed)
    - Shielding gas flow rate (argon; fixed)
    - Stand-off distance / focus (fixed)
    - Track width target (via constant optical focus; monitor but do not vary)
  - Geometry:
    - SBML wall: ~20 mm length, ~6–8 mm height, single-bead per layer, straight toolpath, unidirectional passes, same start/stop for each layer.
    - Replicates: n = 3 per power level (total 9 coupons). Optional “power ladder” wall: power stepped every ~10 layers.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-section imaging at 3 through-thickness positions (bottom/mid/top) for each coupon.
  - Porosity metrics from SEM:
    - Area fraction porosity by manual stereological point counting or field thresholding on SEM images.
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter) via manual annotation or simple thresholding; at least 10 fields per section.
    - Pore morphology classification (rounded/keyhole-like vs irregular lack-of-fusion) by aspect ratio and location relative to bead boundaries (identify bead boundaries from etch/contrast or melt pool lines).
  - Derived outputs:
    - Porosity area fraction vs laser power.
    - Fraction of keyhole-like pores vs lack-of-fusion vs power.
    - Within-part gradient (bottom/mid/top) to check thermal history effects.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Without strict control of oxygen in the build chamber, oxidation could alter wetting and porosity independent of laser power. Also, insufficient substrate preheat or poor substrate bonding can cause lack-of-fusion artifacts not attributable to power. SBML geometry reduces, but does not eliminate, thermal history variations with height.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a suitable build substrate plate (e.g., NiTi or compatible alloy plate) and means to fixture it in the DED machine; this is not explicitly listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes basic image analysis can be done from SEM micrographs (manual stereology/annotation); no specialized image analysis software is listed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

Use 5 laser power levels spanning the validated stable operating window, with 3 replicate tracks per level on a common substrate. Cross-section each track at mid-length, mount, grind/polish, and quantify porosity via SEM image analysis (area fraction, pore size distribution, and pore morphology).

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps (DED build with argon shielding; sectioning with precision saw; mounting in resin; grinding/polishing; SEM imaging) are supported by the listed equipment and materials.
  - The literature card indicates a validated stable operating window and established metallographic workflow for porosity quantification in DED NiTi.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: use of argon shielding during DED of NiTi; operation within a validated stable laser power window; sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing for SEM cross-sectional porosity quantification.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - A controlled, replicate-based single-track matrix specifically isolating laser power as the sole variable, with:
    - Five discrete power setpoints within the validated stable window (e.g., evenly spaced), all other parameters fixed (scan speed, powder feed rate, standoff, spot size, shielding flow).
    - Spatial porosity profiling across melt pool depth (subdivide each cross-section into top/mid/bottom regions) to reveal power-dependent transition in lack-of-fusion versus keyhole-type porosity tendencies within the stable window.
    - Co-measurement of melt pool geometry (depth/width/aspect ratio) from the same SEM cross-sections to correlate pore metrics with energy input signatures.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Independent variable: Laser power (five levels within the validated stable window reported for DED NiTi).
  - Held constant: scan speed, powder feed rate, hatch spacing (N/A for single tracks), layer count (single layer), standoff/defocus, spot size, argon flow rate, substrate preheat (if any, held constant), and track length.
  - Replication: 3 tracks per power level; mid-length cross-section per track.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections at mid-track:
    - Porosity area fraction (per cross-section and averaged over replicates).
    - Pore size distribution and morphology (e.g., circularity) to distinguish gas/Keyhole-like vs. lack-of-fusion signatures.
    - Spatial porosity profile (top/middle/bottom of melt pool).
    - Melt pool geometry (depth, width, aspect ratio).
  - Output plots:
    - Porosity area fraction vs. laser power.
    - Melt pool aspect ratio vs. laser power.
    - Representative pore size histograms at low/mid/high power.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At the upper end of the power window, Ni evaporation and incipient keyholing could alter chemistry and inflate porosity, or cause excessive spatter leading to track instability. At the low end, lack-of-fusion may dominate if true energy input is insufficient due to local variability in powder delivery. Either case can obscure a clean monotonic trend.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Substrate plate: Assume availability of a compatible flat substrate (e.g., NiTi or tool steel) for depositing single tracks; not listed in the BOM.
  - Image analysis: Assume access to basic image analysis on the SEM console or a standard lab PC to threshold pores and compute area fractions; software not listed in the BOM.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric laser power levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a single thin-wall “power-gradient” DED NiTi specimen composed of four sequential 10-mm wall segments, each deposited at a different laser power level within the validated stable operating window. Fabricate three identical walls (biological replicates). Cross-section each segment, mount, grind/polish, and acquire SEM cross-sections to quantify porosity (area fraction, pore size/morphology). Compare porosity metrics versus laser power.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps use available materials (NiTi powder, argon) and equipment (DED machine, precision saw, mounting press, grinding/polishing kit, SEM).
  - The literature card supports argon shielding, a validated stable power window, and SEM-based cross-sectional porosity quantification.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding during DED of NiTi.
  - Operating within a validated stable laser power window to ensure buildability.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing for SEM cross-section porosity quantification.
  (From: paper_002_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Implement a single-build “power-gradient” thin wall where consecutive segments are deposited at different laser powers. This within-part gradient reduces run-to-run powder and environmental variability and ensures identical thermal boundary conditions across power levels. Randomize the order of power levels across the three replicate walls to mitigate position effects.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit
  - SEM

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (shielding)
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power at four levels within the validated stable window (e.g., P1 < P2 < P3 < P4; choose absolute values according to the machine’s validated window per paper_002_chunk_01).
  - Held constant: scan speed, powder feed rate, layer height, hatch spacing (if multi-track per layer), argon flow rate, and standoff distance. Keep interlayer dwell consistent across segments.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections for each segment:
    - Porosity area fraction (estimate via thresholding/point counting on SEM images).
    - Pore size distribution and morphology classification (lack-of-fusion vs keyhole-like, spherical vs irregular).
    - Melt pool depth/width from cross-sections to correlate energy input to fusion characteristics.
  - Report mean ± standard deviation across three replicate walls for each power level. Perform simple trend analysis of porosity vs laser power.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - If the lowest or highest power approaches the edge of the stable window, segments may exhibit lack-of-fusion (too low) or keyhole instability/excess evaporation (too high), causing severe defects or build stoppage. Polishing-induced pore pull-out could bias porosity estimates.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the SEM or available computing environment allows basic image thresholding/area measurements. If not available, porosity will be estimated by manual point counting on printed micrographs, which increases uncertainty.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric laser power levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Power-bracketed DED build of NiTi coupons under argon shielding, followed by cross‑section SEM to quantify 2D porosity area fraction and pore size distributions as a function of laser power. Build five identical small coupons while sweeping laser power across a validated stable operating window; keep all other process parameters fixed. Section each coupon, mount in resin, grind/polish, and image multiple fields per coupon in SEM for manual point-count porosity and pore size binning.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All required steps—DED fabrication under argon, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM imaging—are supported by the listed materials and equipment. Porosity quantification can be performed from SEM cross‑sections without additional software by manual grid point-counting and ruler-based pore sizing.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding in DED NiTi and operating within a validated stable laser power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing to prepare cross‑sections for SEM imaging to quantify porosity.
  (Both from: Title: paper_002_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - A bracketed power sweep centered on the validated stable window, expressed as relative setpoints (e.g., 80%, 90%, 100%, 110%, 120% of the window’s nominal center), with all other parameters constant, to map the transition from lack‑of‑fusion to keyhole‑type porosity.
  - Manual, equipment‑only porosity quantification using point counting and size binning on SEM images to avoid reliance on non‑BOM image analysis tools.
  - Triplicate fields per depth tier (top/middle/bottom) in each coupon to assess within‑build variability of porosity.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Abrasive papers and polishing cloths/suspensions as included with the grinding and polishing kit

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power at five levels around the validated stable window:
    - P1 = 80% of nominal center
    - P2 = 90%
    - P3 = 100% (center)
    - P4 = 110%
    - P5 = 120%
  - Hold constant across all builds:
    - Scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, powder feed rate, stand‑off distance, and argon flow (use the literature‑validated or lab‑standard values)
    - Build geometry: e.g., 10 mm × 10 mm × 5 mm block or a 20‑mm‑long, 3‑mm‑thick wall (choose one and keep constant)

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross‑sections: capture at least 9 fields per coupon (3 near top/middle/bottom; magnifications ~200×–500× with scale bars)
  - Porosity metrics from SEM:
    - 2D area fraction by manual point counting on an overlaid grid
    - Pore number density (count per mm^2)
    - Pore size distribution via equivalent circle diameter measured against scale bar, binned (e.g., <10 µm, 10–30 µm, >30 µm)
    - Qualitative pore morphology notes (rounded/keyhole vs lack‑of‑fusion irregular pores)
  - Output plots:
    - Porosity area fraction vs relative laser power
    - Pore size distribution histograms per power level
    - Morphology prevalence vs power (qualitative bar chart)

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At the lowest power level, deposition may be unstable with excessive lack‑of‑fusion, yielding unusable coupons. Mitigation: confirm track stability at each setpoint with a brief single‑layer test pass before full build; if instability is observed, adjust the lowest power upward while keeping the five‑level spacing.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED machine allows precise laser power setpoints and argon shielding as indicated in the literature. If 3D porosity characterization were desired, micro‑CT would be needed, which is not in the BOM; this plan relies solely on 2D cross‑section SEM.

- Source Papers Used:
  - Title: paper_002_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Power-sweep DED builds of NiTi to map porosity vs laser power, using cross-section SEM to quantify pore area fraction and morphology. Two sub-series:
  A) Fixed scan speed and feed: vary laser power across the machine’s validated stable window.
  B) Constant linear energy input: vary laser power while inversely adjusting scan speed to keep P/v constant, to isolate peak-power effects from energy density.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps (DED building under argon, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, SEM imaging) are supported by the listed equipment and materials. Porosity can be quantified from SEM cross-sections without extra consumables.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding during DED of NiTi and operating within a validated stable laser-power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, polishing, and SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  These precedents are from: paper_002_chunk_01.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  In addition to a conventional fixed-speed power sweep (Series A), include a matched linear-energy series (Series B) that holds P/v constant while changing the absolute power. This contrasts porosity driven by total energy input versus phenomena tied to instantaneous power (e.g., keyhole vs lack-of-fusion), using only available tools.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Laser power (primary factor): choose 5 levels spanning the machine’s validated stable range from paper_002_chunk_01 (e.g., P1 < P2 < P3 < P4 < P5).
  - Series A (fixed-speed): hold scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, powder feed rate, and argon flow constant; vary only power.
  - Series B (constant linear energy): for each target P, set scan speed v so that P/v = constant (match mid-level P3/v3 from Series A); keep other parameters as in Series A.
  - Common controls: identical substrate material and surface prep, identical build geometry (e.g., 10 × 10 × 5 mm pads or equivalent minimal coupons), identical inter-layer dwell and argon shielding settings.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections at mid-build height and near top surface.
  - Quantify:
    - Porosity area fraction via point-count stereology on printed micrographs with a uniform grid.
    - Pore size distribution and morphology metrics (equivalent diameter; circularity to distinguish lack-of-fusion vs keyhole pores).
    - Qualitative pore location (intra-track, inter-track, inter-layer).
  - Output: porosity vs power curves for Series A and Series B; pore morphology maps indicative of lack-of-fusion (irregular, elongated) vs keyhole (rounded).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At low power (or high scan speed), lack-of-fusion may prevent stable builds; at high power, keyholing and excessive evaporation can spike porosity or alter track stability. Mitigation: begin with single-layer pads and short beads to confirm melt pool stability at each setting before building full pads.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED machine can set and log laser power and scan speed precisely and maintain argon shielding as per paper_002_chunk_01.
  - 3D porosity (micro-CT) is not available; results rely on 2D stereology from SEM cross-sections.
  - Image analysis will be done by manual stereology and simple measurements on SEM micrographs; no dedicated image-analysis software is assumed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric laser power levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Build a NiTi DED “power map” coupon containing multiple square tiles, each deposited at a different laser power within the machine’s validated stable window, while holding all other parameters constant. Cross-section the coupon, mount, grind, and polish for SEM imaging to quantify porosity (area fraction, size distribution, and morphology) versus laser power.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps use only NiTi powder, argon shielding, the DED system for fabrication, and standard metallographic prep (saw, mounting press, grinding/polishing) followed by SEM imaging for porosity quantification, exactly as outlined in the literature precedent.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
• Use of argon shielding during DED NiTi and adherence to a validated stable operating window for laser power.  
• Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing followed by SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.  
Source: “paper_002_chunk_01”

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Implement an in-situ “power map” within a single coupon: a 3×3 grid of 9 adjacent 6×6 mm tiles, each deposited with a distinct laser power level (evenly spaced across the validated window), built in a randomized order to mitigate thermal history and time-drift effects. This single-build layout improves comparability across power levels and reduces powder/time consumption compared with separate builds.

- Needed Equipment:
• DED machine  
• Precision saw  
• Mounting press  
• Grinding and polishing kit  
• SEM

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
• NiTi powder  
• Argon gas  
• Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
Primary sweep:
• Laser power: select 5–9 levels spanning the machine’s validated stable window for NiTi (e.g., evenly spaced).  
Held constant (per machine’s recommended settings for NiTi, fixed for all tiles):
• Scan speed  
• Powder feed rate  
• Layer thickness  
• Hatch spacing and scan strategy (e.g., bidirectional with 90° rotation per layer)  
• Argon shielding flow rate  
• Spot size/focus

- Measurements / Outputs:
• SEM cross-sections from mid-thickness of each tile (and optionally near-top and near-bottom) at fixed magnifications.  
• Quantify: area porosity (%) via image thresholding; pore size distribution; pore circularity/aspect ratio to infer lack-of-fusion (irregular, inter-track) vs keyhole (near-circular, intrabead) tendencies.  
• Plot porosity metrics versus laser power; test for a potential U-shaped relation (hypothesis: low power → lack-of-fusion; high power → keyhole).  
• Report mean ± standard deviation using ≥10 fields of view per tile for statistical confidence.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
• Powers outside the true stable window could cause excessive spatter or keyholing, distorting trends or damaging the build.  
• Charging artifacts in SEM if the mounted surface is insufficiently grounded, complicating pore segmentation.  
• Thermal cross-talk between adjacent tiles affecting local porosity if tiles are too close.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
• Assumes the SEM can image mounted, uncoated NiTi cross-sections without a conductive coating (via good grounding or variable-pressure mode). If not, a conductive coating step would be needed (not listed in BOM).  
• Assumes the DED machine allows precise per-tile power control and maintains constant spot size/focus across the build.

- Source Papers Used:
• “paper_002_chunk_01”

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric laser power levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Laser-power sweep in DED-processed NiTi to quantify porosity via SEM cross-sections. Build a matrix of small multi-layer wall coupons at five laser power levels spanning below, within, and above the validated stable window (e.g., 180, 200, 220, 240, 260 W), keeping all other parameters constant (scan speed, powder feed, layer height, hatch/track spacing, argon flow). For an internal control, add a second subset where scan speed is co-varied to hold nominal volumetric energy density constant at the mid-power reference. Section, mount, polish, and image cross-sections by SEM to measure porosity area fraction and pore size/morphology.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps use available items: DED for builds under argon, precision saw for sectioning, mounting press and resin, grinding/polishing kit, and SEM for cross-sectional imaging.
  - NiTi powder and argon gas are available; no additional consumables are required beyond mounting resin.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding during DED of NiTi and operation within a validated stable laser power window.
  - Post-build metallographic preparation (sectioning, mounting, grinding, polishing) and SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  Source: Title: paper_002_chunk_01

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Two-mode comparison to isolate laser power effects:
    1) Constant scan speed subset to reveal the direct effect of laser power on porosity.
    2) Constant nominal volumetric energy density subset (co-vary scan speed with power) to test whether porosity trends are governed primarily by energy density or by power-specific thermal/plasma dynamics.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power at 180, 200, 220, 240, 260 W (adjust the exact values to fit the machine’s validated stable window; include one level slightly below and one slightly above the window only if machine safety interlocks and process stability allow).
  - Held constant (subset 1): scan speed (e.g., 600 mm/min), powder feed rate, layer height (e.g., 200 µm), track spacing (e.g., 400 µm), argon flow.
  - Co-varied (subset 2): adjust scan speed to maintain constant nominal volumetric energy density equal to the 220 W reference condition.
  - Coupon geometry: multi-layer wall or small block (e.g., 10 mm × 10 mm × 5 mm) with identical toolpaths across conditions.
  - Replicates: n = 3 per condition to capture variability.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections at mid-height of each coupon; acquire stitched images at low magnification for global porosity and higher magnification for pore morphology.
  - Quantify: porosity area fraction (%), pore size distribution, pore circularity/aspect ratio, and spatial distribution (e.g., near-track vs inter-track).
  - Optional within-coupon gradient: additional cross-section near the top layer to assess heat accumulation effects.
  - Output: porosity vs laser power curves for constant-speed subset and for constant-energy-density subset; compare trends to evaluate whether power-specific effects exist beyond energy density.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Excessive power may cause keyholing and spatter-induced lack-of-fusion elsewhere, skewing porosity; too low power may lead to pervasive lack-of-fusion. Both can produce unusable coupons. Mitigation: pilot single-track tests at each power to verify bead stability before building full coupons and stay within/near the validated window.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes access to basic image analysis tools (e.g., SEM software thresholding or equivalent) to calculate porosity area fraction. If unavailable, plan for manual point counting or consistent thresholding using SEM’s native measurement features.
  - No composition analysis (e.g., EDS) is listed; any Ni evaporation or phase changes cannot be directly verified in this study.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

Compare porosity metrics versus laser power to identify onset of lack-of-fusion versus keyhole regimes.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps—DED deposition under argon, mechanical sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM cross-sectional imaging—are supported by the provided equipment and materials.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding during DED of NiTi and operation within a validated stable laser power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing NiTi coupons for SEM cross-sectional imaging to quantify porosity.
  (From: paper_002_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Systematic, evenly spaced power sweep within the validated window with all other parameters fixed to isolate power-specific porosity mechanisms.
  - Dual-diagnostic via pore morphology classification on SEM (rounded, keyhole-type vs irregular, lack-of-fusion) to map a qualitative transition boundary across the power sweep without requiring additional analytics.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power P1–P5, evenly spaced across the machine’s validated stable operating window (as referenced in paper_002_chunk_01).
  - Held constant (report and fix these before the run):
    - Scan speed
    - Powder feed rate
    - Layer thickness
    - Track spacing/overlap (for walls: track-to-track offset)
    - Laser spot size/focus
    - Argon flow rate and shielding configuration
    - Build strategy: single-wall, multi-layer with constant number of layers (e.g., 10–15) to reach ~5–8 mm wall height

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections (at least 3 fields of view per coupon, same magnification and area) for:
    - Porosity area fraction (%)
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circular diameter)
    - Pore morphology classification (rounded/keyhole vs irregular/lack-of-fusion) and spatial location (near-track center vs inter-track boundaries)
  - Derived outputs:
    - Porosity vs laser power trend
    - Estimated transition power between lack-of-fusion-dominated and keyhole-dominated regimes (via morphology shift)
    - Within-condition variability (duplicate coupons at one mid-level power for repeatability)

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At higher power, excessive evaporation and keyholing may increase porosity; at lower power, lack-of-fusion between tracks can dominate. Either can produce unusable walls or severe distortion that complicates sectioning. Mitigation: stay strictly within the validated power window, verify melt stability on short test beads, and maintain consistent argon shielding.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes access to basic image analysis on the SEM console or a general-purpose computer for threshold-based porosity quantification; if unavailable, manual stereology will increase time and uncertainty.
  - No in-situ oxygen monitoring is listed; assume the DED system’s standard argon shielding achieves sufficiently low oxygen to avoid oxidation artifacts during processing.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Fabricate a single “staircase-power” DED NiTi wall composed of sequential 10 mm-long segments, each deposited at a different laser power within the validated stable window, while holding scan speed, hatch/track overlap (single-track wall), powder feed rate, and argon shielding constant. Section the wall to obtain cross-sections from each power segment, mount, grind, and polish for SEM imaging. Quantify porosity area fraction and pore morphology versus laser power.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps—DED deposition under argon, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM cross-section imaging—are supported by the listed equipment and materials. No additional instrumentation is required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: use of argon shielding during DED NiTi, operation within a validated stable laser power window, and porosity quantification via SEM on mounted, ground, and polished cross-sections.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  Implement a single-specimen “staircase-power” build (contiguous segments at incremented laser powers) to reduce run-to-run variability and conserve powder/time while mapping porosity vs. power in one wall. Add two replicate walls to assess repeatability.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Grinding/polishing media and cloths (from kit)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power at 5–7 evenly spaced levels within the validated stable window reported in paper_002_chunk_01.
  - Held constant: scan speed, powder feed rate, layer height (or single-layer bead height for a wall), track width strategy (single-track wall), argon flow/shielding configuration, and focus/spot size as per the stable window.
  - Replicates: fabricate two additional identical staircase walls.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sectional imaging of each power segment (≥3 fields of view per segment, mid-height of the wall).
  - Quantitative porosity metrics: area fraction porosity per segment; qualitative pore morphology classification (lack-of-fusion vs. keyhole-like rounded pores).
  - Derived relationship: porosity vs. laser power; estimate of process window regions where porosity increases (e.g., at too low power—lack-of-fusion; at too high power—keyhole-like pores).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  At higher laser powers, Ni evaporation in NiTi could alter composition and melt pool behavior, confounding the attribution of porosity solely to power. Also, thermal accumulation along the continuous wall could bias later segments; include short cool-down pauses between segments and consistent segment lengths to mitigate.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED machine can program segment-wise laser power changes and maintain constant scan speed/powder feed.
  - No in-situ monitoring or composition analysis is available; interpretation relies on ex-situ SEM only.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Systematically vary laser power within the validated stable operating window to quantify its effect on porosity in DED-built NiTi bulk coupons, using argon shielding. Build 10 x 10 x 5 mm coupons at three laser power levels (Low, Mid, High within the validated window), holding scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, powder feed rate, and spot size constant. Prepare metallographic cross-sections and use SEM imaging to measure porosity area fraction, pore size distribution, and pore morphology.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps use available NiTi powder, argon shielding on the DED machine, and standard metallographic prep followed by SEM imaging, as supported by the BOM and literature precedent.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding in DED NiTi.
  - Operating within a validated stable laser power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing NiTi coupons for SEM cross-sectional porosity quantification.
  (All from: paper_002_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Power triad with fixed linear energy input context: choose three discrete laser powers spanning the validated window (e.g., Low, Mid, High), while keeping all other parameters constant to isolate the power variable’s influence on porosity.
  - Add single-track “witness” lines at each power on the same build plate to measure melt pool depth/width in cross-section, enabling linkage between penetration mode and bulk porosity without extra equipment.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine (with argon shielding)
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit
  - SEM

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Grinding papers (e.g., SiC) and polishing media (e.g., diamond suspensions/alumina) from the kit

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Laser power: three levels within the validated stable operating window (denote as P_low, P_mid, P_high).
  - Hold constant for all builds:
    - Scan speed (v)
    - Hatch spacing (h)
    - Layer thickness (t)
    - Powder feed rate (ṁ)
    - Laser spot size (d)
    - Argon shielding flow rate
  - Optional control features:
    - Include single-tracks at each power (same v) adjacent to bulk coupons.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - From bulk coupons (cross-sections at mid-height and near top surface):
    - Porosity area fraction (%) via thresholded SEM images
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter) and morphology (sphericity) to differentiate lack-of-fusion vs keyhole-type pores
  - From single-tracks:
    - Melt pool width and depth to indicate conduction vs keyhole regime
  - Report relationships: porosity metrics vs laser power; correlate melt pool geometry vs porosity trend.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Excessive power may cause keyholing and spatter, inflating porosity; too low power may cause lack-of-fusion porosity. Ni evaporation at higher powers could locally shift composition and affect melt behavior. Mitigation: remain within the validated power window; verify stable shield gas flow; use consistent powder feed.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Image analysis: assume basic thresholding/measurement can be done from SEM-acquired images (either with SEM’s onboard tools or manual stereological point counting on printed micrographs). No micro-CT is available; analysis is limited to 2D stereology.
  - Oxygen monitoring of the build chamber is not explicitly listed; assume the DED machine’s argon shielding is adequate per standard operation.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  - Build a single thin-wall (e.g., 60 mm long, 3 mm thick, ~10 mm tall) NiTi structure by DED under continuous argon shielding while deliberately stepping the laser power along the wall length in defined segments within the validated stable operating window. Keep all other parameters constant (scan speed, powder feed, hatch spacing, layer height, gas flow). After building, section the wall perpendicular to its length to obtain cross-sections from each power segment, mount, grind, and polish, then use SEM to quantify porosity (area fraction and pore size distribution). Correlate porosity metrics with laser power.
  - Example segment plan: 6 segments × 10 mm each at increasing laser power (e.g., P1 < P2 < ... < P6). Include 2 replicate walls to assess repeatability.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps (DED under argon, sectioning with precision saw, mounting, grinding/polishing, SEM cross-section imaging) are explicitly supported by the listed equipment and materials.
  - The literature card indicates a validated stable operating window for laser power and that SEM cross-sections can quantify porosity.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From "paper_002_chunk_01":
    - Use of argon shielding in DED NiTi.
    - Operating within a validated stable laser power window.
    - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing coupons for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Implement an intra-coupon laser power step-wedge along a single thin-wall build to generate multiple power conditions in one substrate and thermal history, minimizing variability from run-to-run changes in powder delivery or environment and reducing build time and material use compared to separate coupons.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine (with programmable laser power setpoints for segment-wise stepping).
  - SEM (with basic imaging and measurement tools).
  - Precision saw.
  - Mounting press.
  - Grinding and polishing kit.

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder.
  - Argon gas.
  - Mounting resin.
  - Standard grinding papers and polishing suspensions/cloths (assumed part of the kit).

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power, 6 levels within the validated stable window (e.g., P = {P1, P2, P3, P4, P5, P6}, spaced to span low to high but still stable).
  - Held constant: scan speed (e.g., mm/s), powder feed rate (g/min), hatch spacing (for multi-track wall), layer height, shielding gas flow rate, spot size/defocus, inter-layer dwell.
  - Replication: build 2 identical step-wedge walls to quantify repeatability.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections from the mid-length of each power segment:
    - Porosity area fraction (%), measured via thresholded image analysis or SEM measurement tools on polished sections.
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter) and morphology classification (rounded keyhole vs. irregular lack-of-fusion) by visual criteria.
  - Plot porosity metrics vs. laser power; identify thresholds where porosity mode transitions (e.g., lack-of-fusion at low power to keyhole at high power).
  - Optional qualitative notes on melt track continuity and lack-of-fusion indicators between tracks/layers.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At high power, keyholing and spatter may increase porosity and cause geometry distortion, complicating consistent wall thickness and making section locations nonuniform. At low power, lack-of-fusion can prevent segment completion.

  - Assumes the DED machine can implement segment-wise laser power changes within a single build and logs parameters for traceability.
  - Assumes the SEM has basic measurement/thresholding tools sufficient for porosity area fraction estimation; if not, manual stereology on SEM images would be used.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a set of simple DED NiTi pad coupons while sweeping laser power across a validated stable operating window, hold all other parameters constant, then quantify porosity from SEM cross-sections. Use 4 laser power levels (e.g., 70%, 85%, 100%, 115% of the machine’s recommended NiTi setpoint within the stable window), produce 3-layer 10×10 mm pads at each power with duplicate builds, section mid-pad, mount, grind/polish, and measure porosity area fraction and pore morphology from SEM images.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps use available NiTi powder, argon shielding, DED machine, and standard metallographic prep (precision saw, mounting press, grinding/polishing kit) followed by SEM imaging.
  - No additional analytics beyond SEM imaging are required to quantify porosity in cross-section.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: 
    - Use argon shielding and operate within a validated stable laser-power window for DED NiTi.
    - Section, mount, grind, and polish samples for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Apply a percentage-based power sweep anchored to the machine’s recommended NiTi setpoint, ensuring all chosen powers lie within the literature-backed “stable operating window.” This normalizes the study across machines while maintaining feasibility.
  - Use small, 3-layer pads to capture layer-to-layer fusion effects on porosity with minimal build time and material usage, enabling duplicates at each power for statistical confidence.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power at 4 levels within the validated stable window (e.g., 70%, 85%, 100%, 115% of the OEM NiTi power setpoint).
  - Hold constant:
    - Scan speed (use the machine’s recommended NiTi value)
    - Powder feed rate (recommended NiTi value)
    - Layer height (e.g., nominal for chosen nozzle; keep constant)
    - Hatch spacing (for a 10×10 mm pad, use consistent overlap; if single-track raster, keep track pitch fixed)
    - Shielding gas flow (argon, per DED best practice)
    - Inter-layer dwell (e.g., 60–120 s) to avoid excessive heat buildup across powers

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-section porosity metrics:
    - Porosity area fraction (image thresholding of polished cross-sections)
    - Pore size distribution and morphology classification (lack-of-fusion elongated pores vs keyhole spherical pores)
  - Melt pool geometry indicators (depth/width) from etched contrast or polished contrast, if visible
  - Replicates: 2 pads per power level; section each pad at its center; analyze at least 3 fields of view per section to compute mean ± SD of porosity

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At higher powers near the top of the “stable window,” risk of keyholing and spatter-induced gas entrapment may increase, potentially overwhelming porosity differences. Mitigation: confirm that the highest power remains within the stable window; allow inter-layer cooling; ensure consistent argon shielding.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes access to basic SEM image analysis (e.g., built-in SEM software or open-source tools) for threshold-based porosity quantification. If unavailable, manual grid-based stereology can be used but will increase analysis time.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Systematically vary laser power in a DED process for NiTi to quantify porosity from SEM cross-sections of small build pads. Fabricate 10 mm × 10 mm × 5 mm pads at four laser powers within the validated stable window, holding scan speed, hatch spacing, powder feed, layer thickness, and argon shielding constant. Produce 3 replicates per power. Section each pad through the center, mount, grind, and polish to mirror finish. Acquire SEM mosaics across the full cross-section and quantify areal porosity fraction and pore morphology. Build-height effects are probed by imaging near-substrate, mid-height, and top regions in the same cross-section.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps (DED fabrication under argon, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM imaging) are explicitly supported by the available equipment and materials. No external characterization tools beyond SEM are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  From “paper_002_chunk_01”: 
  - Use of argon shielding during DED of NiTi.
  - Operating within a validated stable laser power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing samples for SEM cross-sections to quantify porosity.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Within each coupon, quantify porosity as a function of build height (bottom/middle/top fields) to separate start-up/transient porosity from steady-state porosity at each power.
  - Keep all non-power parameters fixed to directly isolate the laser power effect, while using multiple replicates to establish statistical confidence.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit
  - SEM

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power (four levels within the validated stable window; for example, P1 < P2 < P3 < P4 evenly spanning the allowed range).
  - Held constant across all builds: scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, powder feed rate, shielding gas configuration/flow setting used in prior validated operation, and build path (raster pattern and rotation).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sectional mosaics per coupon at three heights (near substrate, mid-height, near top).
  - Areal porosity fraction (%) via image-based measurement on SEM-acquired images (thresholding/tracing using SEM’s measurement tools).
  - Pore size distribution (equivalent circular diameter bins) and morphology notes (rounded gas pores vs lack-of-fusion/keyhole-shaped).
  - Porosity vs laser power curves, with within-coupon height-dependent trends and between-replicate variability.
  - Qualitative build observations (spatter, balling, keyhole signatures on top surfaces).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Selecting power points too close to the edges of the stable window could cause lack of bonding at low power or keyholing/excessive evaporation at high power, confounding porosity trends or preventing complete pad fabrication. Mitigation: pilot single-track or single-layer trials at each power to confirm melt pool stability before fabricating full pads.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the SEM has basic image capture and measurement tools sufficient for manual thresholding/tracing of pores. If not available, an external image analysis tool would be needed (not in current BOM).

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

Title: paper_002_chunk_01

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  - Build five NiTi DED coupons while sweeping laser power across a stable operating window (relative levels: 60%, 75%, 90%, 105%, 120% of the lab’s validated “nominal” power), holding all other parameters constant (scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, powder feed, argon shielding).
  - Section each coupon perpendicular to the build direction at mid-length, mount in resin, grind/polish to a mirror finish, and acquire SEM cross-section images at multiple fields of view (top/mid/bottom of build region).
  - Quantify areal porosity fraction by manual grid point-counting on SEM images and classify pore morphology (irregular lack-of-fusion vs round/keyhole-like) to construct porosity vs laser power trends and pore-type maps.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps (DED fabrication under argon, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, SEM imaging) are supported by the listed equipment and materials.
  - Porosity quantification can be performed via manual stereology on SEM images without additional software or instruments.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: Use argon shielding during DED NiTi, operate within a validated stable laser-power window, and quantify porosity via SEM cross-sections after sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - A controlled relative-power sweep around the lab’s nominal setting to intentionally traverse expected lack-of-fusion (low power), dense (near nominal), and potential keyhole (high power) regimes, while maintaining all other parameters fixed. This pairs manual stereology-based porosity quantification with pore morphology classification to discriminate mechanism-driven porosity changes attributable to laser power.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Laser power: 5 levels at 60%, 75%, 90%, 105%, and 120% of the site’s validated nominal power (from the stable window cited in the literature card).
  - Hold constant: scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, powder feed rate, shielding gas flow, and track/layer strategy.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - From SEM cross-sections (as-polished):
    - Areal porosity fraction via manual grid point counting (≥10 fields per coupon distributed across top/middle/bottom).
