response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  - Print a set of small DED NiTi pad coupons (e.g., 10 mm × 10 mm × ~3 mm) while sweeping laser power across a validated stable operating window, holding all other process parameters constant. Prepare cross-sections and quantify porosity via SEM image analysis to establish the relationship between laser power and porosity (area fraction and pore morphology).
  - Design: five power levels within the stable window (e.g., P1–P5 evenly spaced), with two replicate pads per level. Build pads in a randomized spatial order on the same substrate to minimize location/thermal-history bias. Section each pad at mid-thickness, mount, grind/polish, and image three fields of view per pad for porosity quantification.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - DED machine and argon are available to fabricate NiTi pads under argon shielding.
  - Precision saw, mounting press, and grinding/polishing kit enable cross-section prep.
  - SEM enables porosity quantification from cross-sections as reported in the literature card.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding during DED of NiTi.
  - Operating within a validated stable laser power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  - Source for these precedents: paper_002_chunk_01.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Randomized, multi-coupon build on a single substrate with two replicates per power level to control for run-to-run variability and location effects, enabling a more robust power–porosity curve from one build.
  - Pore morphology classification from SEM (e.g., lack-of-fusion elongated pores vs. keyhole spherical pores) to infer transition regimes within the power window, using only imaging-based metrics (size distribution, circularity, aspect ratio).

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine (with programmable laser power setpoints).
  - SEM.
  - Precision saw.
  - Mounting press.
  - Grinding and polishing kit.

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder.
  - Argon gas.
  - Mounting resin.
  - SiC papers and polishing media/colloidal silica (as part of the grinding and polishing kit’s consumables).

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power P at five levels within the validated stable window (e.g., P1 < P2 < P3 < P4 < P5).
  - Held constant: scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, powder feed rate, laser spot size, layer count (~3 mm height), and argon flow/shielding setup. Use facility-standard values from prior validated recipes to isolate the effect of power.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM-based porosity metrics per pad:
    - Porosity area fraction (thresholded image analysis on multiple fields of view).
    - Pore size distribution and number density.
    - Pore shape metrics (circularity, aspect ratio) to classify pores as lack-of-fusion-like vs. keyhole-like.
  - Power–porosity curve with mean and standard deviation across replicates.
  - Identification of power ranges associated with increased keyhole-like or lack-of-fusion-like porosity.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - If chosen power levels stray outside the stable window, excessive keyholing or lack of fusion may prevent successful pad fabrication or skew porosity. Mitigation: pre-verify the five power setpoints with single-track trials or rely on previously validated window edges; include a center-point power known to be process-stable.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a suitable DED substrate plate and fixturing compatible with NiTi processing (e.g., NiTi or a commonly used metal substrate) and standard shop safety/handling supplies; these are not listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the DED machine can change laser power between pads within one job file.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric laser power levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a matrix of single-layer DED NiTi pads while sweeping only laser power within the validated stable operating window, then quantify porosity from SEM cross-sections. On a single substrate, deposit 3 replicate pads for each of 5 laser power levels (e.g., 180, 200, 220, 240, 260 W), holding scan speed, hatch spacing, powder feed, spot size, and argon flow constant. Section each pad through the center, mount, grind/polish, and image at SEM to measure porosity area fraction and pore size distribution via manual image analysis. Compare porosity metrics versus laser power.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All required steps—DED deposition under argon, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM cross-section imaging—are supported by the listed equipment and materials.
  - Porosity can be quantified from SEM images without specialized software by manual thresholding/point-counting.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: Use argon shielding and operate within a validated stable laser power window for DED NiTi; section, mount, grind, and polish samples; use SEM cross-sections to quantify porosity.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Spatially randomized pad matrix on a single substrate to minimize inter-build variability and thermal history drift.
  - Include two “ramped-power” single tracks (power stepped every 5 mm across the track) to rapidly identify onset of lack-of-fusion and keyholing signatures before committing to full pad replication.
  - Depth-resolved porosity: segment SEM cross-sections into near-surface, mid-thickness, and near-substrate regions to see if power influences pore distribution through the layer.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Laser power (primary sweep): target 5 levels within the validated stable window (example: 180, 200, 220, 240, 260 W).
  - Hold constant:
    - Scan speed (example: 600 mm/min)
    - Hatch spacing (example: 0.6 mm for 10×10 mm pads, 50% overlap)
    - Powder feed rate (example: 6 g/min)
    - Layer thickness (single layer, example: 200 µm)
    - Spot size/focal offset (fixed)
    - Argon shielding flow (per machine recommendation for inert atmosphere)
  - Build layout:
    - 10×10×0.2 mm pads, 3 replicates per power, randomized positions
    - Two single-bead “ramped-power” tracks (e.g., 160→280 W in 20 W steps) for qualitative screening

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - From SEM cross-sections of each pad (central section):
    - Porosity area fraction (%) via manual thresholding/point counting
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter) and morphology (spherical vs. irregular)
    - Depth-resolved porosity (top/middle/bottom thirds of the layer)
    - Melt pool width and penetration into substrate (from bead geometry)
  - Derived:
    - Porosity vs. laser power curve; identification of regimes consistent with lack-of-fusion (low power) and keyholing (high power)
    - Replicate variability at each power

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At low power: lack-of-fusion causing high porosity and discontinuous tracks.
  - At high power: keyholing and potential Ni evaporation changing local composition and inducing porosity.
  - Mitigations: stay within the literature’s validated power window; use ramped-power scouting to trim the final power range; ensure adequate argon flow to limit oxidation.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible DED substrate plate (e.g., NiTi or stainless) and fixturing; the substrate is not listed in the BOM. If unavailable, the experiment cannot proceed and a suitable substrate must be procured.
  - Assumes the SEM allows image export; porosity quantification will be done manually on captured images without external software.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a single multi-segment DED NiTi wall in argon where each 10 mm-long segment is deposited at a different laser power within the validated stable power window, while keeping scan speed, hatch spacing, powder feed rate, and layer height constant. - Borrowed Literature Precedents:

  - There exists a validated stable operating window for laser power in DED NiTi.
  - Porosity can be quantified via SEM imaging of mounted, ground, and polished cross-sections.
  These precedents are from: paper_002_chunk_01.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  Implement a power-graded, single-build coupon (adjacent segments at different laser powers) to eliminate inter-build variability and reduce powder usage. Insert short dwell times between segments to minimize thermal carryover so that each segment better represents steady-state behavior at its assigned power.

  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:

  - Cleaning solvents/wipes for sample prep (if available; otherwise see Missing Capability / Assumption)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power at 5 levels spanning the validated stable window (e.g., P1 < P2 < P3 < P4 < P5 within that window).
  - Held constant: scan speed, powder feed rate, hatch spacing, layer height, shielding gas flow, stand-off distance, and spot size.
  - Build design: One wall, 5 adjacent segments, each ~10 mm length × ~3–5 mm height, with a 20–30 s dwell between segments.
  - Replicates: At least 2 identical walls to assess repeatability.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-section imaging at a consistent magnification for each segment.
  - Quantitative porosity metrics per segment:
    - Porosity area fraction (%)
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter histogram)
    - Pore morphology indicators (rounded/isolated vs lack-of-fusion elongated pores)
  - Optional spatial analysis: Near-surface vs mid-height porosity comparison within each segment to detect keyhole vs lack-of-fusion regimes.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - High laser power may induce keyholing and spatter entrapment; low power may cause lack-of-fusion. Both extremes can destabilize deposition, confounding steady-state porosity trends. Thermal coupling between adjacent segments could bias results.
  Mitigations:
    - Choose power levels strictly within the validated stable window.
    - Use short inter-segment dwells and begin each segment with a short lead-in track discarded from analysis.
    - Maintain consistent argon shielding and powder flow.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible substrate plate for DED (e.g., NiTi or steel) and fixturing, which is not listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes access to basic image analysis software (e.g., threshold-based area fraction on the SEM workstation) to quantify porosity.
  - If etchants are not available, analysis proceeds on polished, unetched surfaces; pore contrast should still be sufficient for thresholding.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Systematically vary laser power in a DED NiTi build to quantify how porosity (area fraction and pore morphology) changes, using SEM cross-sections of polished pads built under argon shielding with all other process parameters held constant.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps—DED deposition under argon, sectioning with a precision saw, mounting in resin, grinding/polishing, and SEM imaging—are directly supported by the listed materials and equipment.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding during DED NiTi processing.
  - Operating within a validated stable laser power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing followed by SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  Source: paper_002_chunk_01

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - A controlled laser power sweep (e.g., 5 levels within the stable window) at fixed scan speed, powder feed, layer height, and track overlap to isolate the effect of power alone.
  - Build identical multi-track, single-layer pads to minimize thermal history variation and speed throughput, enabling replication (n=3 per power) for basic statistics.
  - Classify pore morphologies (rounded vs. irregular/entrapped lack-of-fusion) via SEM image shape metrics to infer transition from lack-of-fusion to keyhole regimes as power increases.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Laser power: 5 discrete levels within the stable window cited in the literature (example: 180, 200, 220, 240, 260 W; exact values chosen to stay inside the validated range).
  - Held constant across all builds: scan speed, powder feed rate, hatch spacing/track overlap (e.g., 40–50%), layer height (single layer), shielding gas flow, stand-off distance, and spot size if adjustable.
  - Build geometry: 10 × 10 mm multi-track pads, single layer (e.g., 8–10 adjacent tracks) on the same substrate to reduce variability.
  - Replicates: 3 pads per power level.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections taken at pad mid-length:
    - Prepare 2 cross-sections per pad (center and edge region) to capture track overlap effects.
  - Quantitative porosity metrics from SEM images:
    - Area fraction porosity (%), mean pore size, pore size distribution.
    - Shape descriptors (circularity, aspect ratio) to distinguish rounded/keyhole-like vs. irregular/lack-of-fusion pores.
  - Plot porosity (and proportion of rounded pores) versus laser power to identify thresholds/trends.
  - Qualitative notes on surface/track continuity and visible lack-of-fusion between tracks at lower powers.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At low power, lack-of-fusion may cause discontinuous tracks that are hard to section consistently; at high power near the upper limit, excessive vaporization or spatter may destabilize the melt pool. Mitigation: pre-test a single pad at each extreme to confirm track continuity and adjust the chosen power range to remain within the stable window referenced in the literature.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the SEM provides basic image capture and measurement tools sufficient for manual thresholding/area fraction estimation; if not, manual point counting on printed micrographs will be used.
  - Assumes the DED machine parameters other than laser power (scan speed, feed rate, etc.) are user-controllable and can be held constant as required.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Fabricate a single “stepped-power” DED NiTi wall and companion bulk cubes where laser power is the only swept parameter within the validated stable window. Then section, mount, polish, and SEM-image cross-sections to quantify porosity (area fraction, pore size distribution, and pore morphology) as a function of laser power.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All required processing (DED with argon shielding) and post-processing (sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, SEM) are present in the BOM.
  - Porosity can be quantified from SEM cross-sections, consistent with the literature precedent.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: Use argon shielding during DED; operate within a validated stable laser power window; section, mount, grind, and polish samples; quantify porosity via SEM cross-sections.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - A single “stepped-power” wall in which laser power is changed in discrete segments (e.g., five adjacent 10 mm segments built back-to-back at different power levels) to minimize run-to-run variability and powder lot effects, complemented by two small bulk cubes (lowest and highest power) to compare wall vs bulk porosity mechanisms.
  - Within each segment/cube, take multiple cross-sections at different heights to separate start-up vs steady-state porosity effects.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power (e.g., 150, 200, 250, 300, 350 W), all within the validated stable operating window referenced in the literature.
  - Held constant across all builds: scan speed (e.g., 600 mm/min), powder feed rate, hatch spacing (for cubes), layer thickness, track overlap, shielding gas flow, and stand-off distance.
  - Geometries:
    - Stepped-power wall: 5 segments × ~10 mm length each, height ~10 mm, thickness ~2–3 mm.
    - Bulk cubes at lowest and highest power: ~10 × 10 × 10 mm.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-section imaging at:
    - For wall: mid-length of each power segment, at 3 heights (bottom/mid/top).
    - For cubes: mid-plane and top-plane cross-sections.
  - Quantify:
    - Porosity area fraction (%) via image thresholding.
    - Pore size distribution and number density.
    - Pore morphology classification (lack-of-fusion irregular pores vs keyhole near-spherical pores) and depth distribution relative to melt pool boundaries (qualitative).
  - Output: Power–porosity response curve; identification of regimes where porosity transitions from lack-of-fusion (low power) to keyhole-induced (high power).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - High power may cause keyholing and composition shift (Ni evaporation), confounding porosity attribution; mitigate by staying within the validated window and using argon shielding.
  - Thermal history differences across adjacent wall segments may cause heat accumulation at later segments; mitigate with cool-down pauses between segments and alternating build order if replicates are run.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes access to basic image analysis software to threshold SEM images and compute porosity metrics (e.g., open-source). If unavailable, porosity quantification would be limited to qualitative assessment.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Study porosity vs. laser power in DED-fabricated NiTi by building small coupons at three power levels spanning the validated stable operating window, then sectioning and SEM-imaging polished cross-sections to quantify porosity area fraction and pore size distribution.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps (DED build under argon, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, SEM imaging) are directly supported by the listed materials and equipment.
  - Porosity quantification from SEM cross-sections is explicitly aligned with the available SEM and sample prep tools.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: Use argon shielding during DED of NiTi; operate within a validated stable laser power window; prepare metallographic cross-sections (section, mount, grind, polish) and use SEM imaging to quantify porosity.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Systematically sample three laser power setpoints within the validated window (low/mid/high) while holding all other parameters constant, and perform depth-resolved porosity mapping (top, mid-height, bottom regions of the build) to separate lack-of-fusion versus gas/keyhole pore regimes by morphology in SEM.
  - Include fixed interlayer dwell time to control heat accumulation so that observed porosity trends are primarily attributable to laser power rather than thermal pile-up.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Laser power: Three levels within the lab’s validated stable window for NiTi DED (e.g., P_low ≈ lower tercile of window, P_mid ≈ midpoint, P_high ≈ upper tercile). Exact values to be selected from the machine’s established NiTi window referenced in-house.
  - Hold constant:
    - Scan speed, powder feed rate, spot size, hatch spacing, layer height, and argon flow rate (use the lab’s standard NiTi recipe).
    - Geometry: Build 10 × 10 × 5 mm coupons; n = 3 coupons per power level (total 9 builds).
    - Path strategy: Unidirectional raster with 90° rotation between layers.
    - Interlayer dwell: Fixed (e.g., 30–60 s) to limit heat accumulation across all builds.
  - Environment: Argon shielding as per standard DED practice for NiTi.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Sample preparation: Section each coupon at mid-length to expose a full build-height cross-section; mount in resin; grind/polish to mirror finish.
  - SEM imaging:
    - Acquire tiled images at consistent magnifications (e.g., 200× for area fraction, 1000× for morphology) across three depth zones (bottom, mid-height, top) with ≥5 fields per zone.
  - Quantification:
    - Porosity area fraction (%) via image thresholding on SEM images (use SEM/software tools available at the instrument; manual thresholding acceptable).
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter) and count density.
    - Morphological classification (rounded gas/keyhole pores vs irregular lack-of-fusion) by aspect ratio and contour.
  - Output: Porosity metrics vs. laser power, with depth-resolved comparison and replicate statistics (mean ± SD).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating too close to the upper edge of the power window may induce keyholing and excessive porosity; too low may cause lack-of-fusion. Mitigation: choose setpoints comfortably within the validated window; verify track stability on a short test line before building full coupons; maintain consistent powder feed and argon shielding to reduce spatter/oxidation artifacts.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - 3D porosity (volumetric) via micro-CT is not available; the study relies on 2D SEM cross-sections as per the literature precedent.
  - Assumes the lab already has a validated stable laser power window and standard NiTi DED recipe (scan speed, feed rate, etc.) as referenced in the literature card; specific numeric values are selected from in-house settings.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Study the effect of laser power on porosity in DED-built NiTi by fabricating a matrix of small bulk coupons at five laser power levels within a validated stable operating window, holding all other process parameters constant, then quantifying cross-sectional porosity via SEM image analysis.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All required steps—DED fabrication under argon shielding, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM imaging—are supported by the listed equipment and materials. No external metrology beyond SEM is required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding during DED of NiTi.
  - Constraining laser power within a validated stable operating window for process stability.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  These precedents are described in: paper_002_chunk_01.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Implement a controlled, single-variable sweep of laser power with all other parameters fixed, combined with pore morphology classification (lack-of-fusion vs keyhole) from SEM shape metrics and concurrent melt pool depth measurements on the same cross-sections. This links porosity mechanisms to power and observed melt pool geometry in one dataset.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Independent variable: Laser power at five levels within the machine’s stable window (example: 150, 200, 250, 300, 350 W; adjust to the machine’s validated range).
  - Held constant (document and fix for all builds):
    - Scan speed (e.g., 600 mm/min)
    - Hatch spacing (e.g., 120 µm)
    - Layer thickness (e.g., 50 µm)
    - Powder feed rate (e.g., 6 g/min)
    - Shielding gas flow rate and nozzle standoff as per argon shielding best practice
    - Scan strategy (e.g., 90° rotate per layer), interlayer dwell, and substrate preheat (if any)
  - Geometry: 10 × 10 × 10 mm bulk cubes; n=3 replicates per power level to capture variability.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections (mid-height and near-top of each cube).
  - Quantitative porosity metrics from SEM images:
    - Areal porosity fraction (%)
    - Pore size distribution and equivalent circular diameter
    - Pore aspect ratio/roundness to classify lack-of-fusion (irregular, planar) vs keyhole (rounded) pores
  - Melt pool depth and width from etched or high-contrast SEM backscattered images to correlate with porosity vs power.
  - Output plots:
    - Porosity fraction vs laser power
    - Fraction of keyhole-like vs lack-of-fusion pores vs laser power
    - Melt pool depth vs laser power and its correlation with porosity

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - High laser power may cause excessive keyholing and spatter-induced defects; low power may cause lack-of-fusion. Either extreme can yield non-buildable conditions or severe cracking in NiTi, reducing usable samples. Mitigation: choose powers strictly within the machine’s validated stable window and pilot a single-track at each power before full cubes.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - 3D porosity quantification (e.g., micro-CT) is not available; results are limited to 2D areal porosity from cross-sections and assumed representative.
  - No in situ monitoring to capture transient keyhole dynamics; reliance on post-mortem SEM only.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

Title: paper_002_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a randomized set of small DED NiTi cubes at five laser powers within a validated stable window, then section, mount, polish, and use SEM cross-sections to quantify porosity (area fraction, size distribution, and morphology) as a function of laser power.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All required steps—DED fabrication under argon shielding, mechanical sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM imaging—are supported by the provided equipment and materials.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding during DED NiTi and selection of a validated stable operating window for laser power.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing, followed by SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  Source: paper_002_chunk_01

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Include replicated center-point runs and randomized build order to quantify experimental variability and detect nonlinearity in the porosity–power relationship, enabling a basic response-curve model using only cross-sectional SEM data.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power at five levels within a stable window (example: 180, 200, 220, 240, 260 W)
  - Hold constant:
    - Scan speed: 600 mm/min
    - Hatch spacing: 0.5 mm
    - Layer thickness: 50 µm
    - Powder feed rate: 5 g/min
    - Shielding gas: Argon, 20 L/min (or per machine’s recommended flow ensuring inert atmosphere)
    - Build geometry: 6 × 6 × 6 mm cubes
  - Experimental design: 5 power levels × 3 replicates (15 total cubes), randomized spatial placement on the build plate to mitigate local thermal history effects; include at least three center-point repeats at 220 W among the replicates.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-section imaging at mid-height of each cube; BSE preferred for pore contrast.
  - Quantify:
    - Porosity area fraction (% area)
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter)
    - Pore shape metrics (circularity/aspect ratio) to infer gas- vs lack-of-fusion-type pores
    - Melt pool morphology indicators (overlap quality, lack-of-fusion features) qualitatively
  - Output: Porosity vs laser power curve with error bars from replicates; basic model (e.g., quadratic fit) to identify minimum-porosity power range.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating too close to the lower-power edge may cause lack-of-fusion porosity; too high power may increase keyholing/gas porosity or cause instability due to Ni evaporation altering wetting. Mitigation: confine powers to the literature-backed stable window and verify track stability with a brief pre-test.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes access to basic image analysis (thresholding/segmentation) for SEM images to compute pore area fraction and size distribution; if unavailable, a manual point-counting grid can be used but will reduce throughput.
  - Assumes the DED machine can accurately deliver and log set laser powers and maintain constant powder feed and argon flow.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Stepwise-power single-wall DED build in NiTi to quantify porosity vs. laser power. Build a straight, 5-layer wall where laser power is stepped across contiguous 10 mm segments (e.g., P1→P2→P3→P4→P5, all within the validated stable window), keeping scan speed, powder feed rate, and argon shielding constant. Precision-section each segment, mount, grind/polish, and use SEM to measure cross-sectional porosity (area fraction, size distribution, morphology) per power level.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - Uses only NiTi powder and argon gas.
  - DED machine can vary laser power segment-by-segment while holding other parameters constant.
  - Sample preparation and SEM cross-sectional imaging are supported by the available saw, mounting press, grinding/polishing kit, and SEM.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Argon shielding during DED of NiTi and operation within a validated stable laser-power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing of DED NiTi for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  (From: paper_002_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Implement a single continuous wall with discrete power steps along its length, so all power conditions are produced in one build under nearly identical ambient conditions. This reduces build-to-build variability and material use versus separate coupons. Include short dwell gaps between segments to limit thermal carryover.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power levels P1–P5, all within the validated stable window reported previously for NiTi DED (exact values to be chosen based on machine capability and prior runs; target roughly evenly spaced within that window).
  - Held constant: scan speed, powder feed rate, layer height, track overlap (single-bead wall, no hatch), shielding gas flow, standoff, and focus.
  - Thermal management: 5–10 s dwell between adjacent segments and/or 5 mm un-deposited gaps to reduce heat accumulation effects between power steps.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections: quantify
    - Porosity area fraction per segment (image thresholding on polished sections).
    - Pore size distribution and morphology (spherical keyhole-like vs. irregular lack-of-fusion).
    - Melt pool geometry (depth/width) as a contextual indicator of energy input.
  - Replication: Section at least two locations per power segment and analyze ≥3 fields of view per section to obtain mean ± SD.
  - Output: Porosity metrics vs. laser power curve; identify onset of keyhole porosity at higher power and lack-of-fusion at lower power, if present.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Thermal carryover along the wall may confound pure power effects (hotter downstream segments). Mitigation: insert short dwells or small gaps between power segments; build multiple short walls starting at different power orders to counter sequence bias.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - No micro-CT is available; porosity quantification relies on 2D cross-sectional SEM, assuming it is representative of bulk porosity.
  - Assumes prior knowledge of the stable laser-power window for the specific machine–powder combination, as referenced in the literature card; exact numeric limits must be confirmed on the local DED system.

  - paper_002_chunk_01

content.text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Stepwise-power single-wall DED build in NiTi to quantify porosity vs. laser power. Build a straight, 5-layer wall where laser power is stepped across contiguous 10 mm segments (e.g., P1→P2→P3→P4→P5, all within the validated stable window), keeping scan speed, powder feed rate, and argon shielding constant. Precision-section each segment, mount, grind/polish, and use SEM to measure cross-sectional porosity (area fraction, size distribution, morphology) per power level.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - Uses only NiTi powder and argon gas.
  - DED machine can vary laser power segment-by-segment while holding other parameters constant.
  - Sample preparation and SEM cross-sectional imaging are supported by the available saw, mounting press, grinding/polishing kit, and SEM.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Argon shielding during DED of NiTi and operation within a validated stable laser-power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing of DED NiTi for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  (From: paper_002_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Implement a single continuous wall with discrete power steps along its length, so all power conditions are produced in one build under nearly identical ambient conditions. This reduces build-to-build variability and material use versus separate coupons. Include short dwell gaps between segments to limit thermal carryover.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power levels P1–P5, all within the validated stable window reported previously for NiTi DED (exact values to be chosen based on machine capability and prior runs; target roughly evenly spaced within that window).
  - Held constant: scan speed, powder feed rate, layer height, track overlap (single-bead wall, no hatch), shielding gas flow, standoff, and focus.
  - Thermal management: 5–10 s dwell between adjacent segments and/or 5 mm un-deposited gaps to reduce heat accumulation effects between power steps.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections: quantify
    - Porosity area fraction per segment (image thresholding on polished sections).
    - Pore size distribution and morphology (spherical keyhole-like vs. irregular lack-of-fusion).
    - Melt pool geometry (depth/width) as a contextual indicator of energy input.
  - Replication: Section at least two locations per power segment and analyze ≥3 fields of view per section to obtain mean ± SD.
  - Output: Porosity metrics vs. laser power curve; identify onset of keyhole porosity at higher power and lack-of-fusion at lower power, if present.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Thermal carryover along the wall may confound pure power effects (hotter downstream segments). Mitigation: insert short dwells or small gaps between power segments; build multiple short walls starting at different power orders to counter sequence bias.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - No micro-CT is available; porosity quantification relies on 2D cross-sectional SEM, assuming it is representative of bulk porosity.
  - Assumes prior knowledge of the stable laser-power window for the specific machine–powder combination, as referenced in the literature card; exact numeric limits must be confirmed on the local DED system.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  DED-build a matrix of small NiTi pads while sweeping only laser power within the machine’s validated stable window, then quantify porosity from SEM cross-sections to establish the power–porosity relationship and identify transitions between lack‑of‑fusion and keyhole regimes.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All required steps—DED deposition of NiTi under argon shielding, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM imaging—are directly supported by the listed materials and equipment. No external characterization tools are needed beyond SEM.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding during DED of NiTi.
  - Operation within a validated stable laser-power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  Source: “paper_002_chunk_01”

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  Hold linear scan conditions constant and vary only laser power across 5 evenly spaced levels within the validated window, building simple single-bead walls and small 5×5×3 mm pads at each power. Compare porosity between single-track walls (to isolate melt-pool physics) and small pads (to incorporate limited remelting/hatch interactions), enabling attribution of porosity trends specifically to power rather than path complexity.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Standard abrasives/consumables from the grinding and polishing kit (e.g., SiC papers, polishing suspensions/cloths)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power (5 levels across the machine’s validated stable window from low to high; e.g., P1 < P2 < P3 < P4 < P5).
  - Hold constant: scan speed, powder feed rate, layer thickness, hatch spacing (for pads), track overlap, standoff/focal position, shielding gas flow rate.
  - Build geometry per power level:
    - Three single-bead walls (~20 mm length, ~5 layers) for replication.
    - Three small pads (~5×5×3 mm) using a simple raster with fixed hatch for replication.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Sample preparation: Section each wall at mid-length and each pad at center; mount, grind, and polish to a mirror finish suitable for SEM.
  - SEM imaging: Acquire stitched cross-sectional images at consistent magnification (e.g., capturing full build height/width).
  - Porosity quantification:
    - Area fraction porosity (%) per sample.
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circular diameter) and number density.
    - Pore morphology classification (rounded/keyhole-like vs irregular/lack‑of‑fusion) by shape/aspect ratio.
  - Outputs:
    - Porosity vs laser power curve for walls and pads.
    - Identification of power ranges associated with lack‑of‑fusion (low power) and keyhole (high power) signatures based on pore morphology.
    - Comparison of walls vs pads to assess the role of limited remelting/hatch overlap at constant power.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At the lowest power, track discontinuity may prevent building full-height walls/pads, yielding too little cross-sectional area for statistics. Mitigation: include an intermediate “buffer” power near the lower bound and, if a level fails, note it as “non-buildable” while proceeding with successful levels.
  - At the highest power, excessive keyholing or spatter could destabilize deposition. Mitigation: ensure selected powers remain within the validated window and verify melt pool stability during the first layer before proceeding.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED machine can accurately set and log laser power and maintain constant scan speed, powder feed, and argon flow.
  - Assumes basic image thresholding/area measurement can be performed using the SEM’s built-in software or simple manual thresholding; if advanced image analysis software is not available, pore metrics will be estimated via consistent manual thresholding protocols.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Laser-power sweep in DED of NiTi to quantify porosity from SEM cross-sections. Build a set of small multi-layer pads at fixed travel speed, hatch spacing, powder feed rate, and argon shielding; vary only laser power across the machine’s validated stable operating window. Section each pad, mount, grind/polish, and acquire SEM images to measure porosity area fraction and pore size distribution.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps—DED building under argon, sectioning with a precision saw, mounting in resin, grinding/polishing, and SEM imaging—are directly supported by the listed materials and equipment. Porosity in NiTi can be quantified from polished cross-sections without etching.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: 
    - Use argon shielding during DED NiTi.
    - Operate within a validated stable laser-power window.
    - Prepare metallographic cross-sections (section, mount, grind, polish) and use SEM to quantify porosity.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - A structured, single-factor laser-power sweep with replication and constant settings for all other parameters to isolate laser-power effects on porosity, coupled with a simple, standardized imaging protocol (fixed magnification fields at multiple build heights) to derive both porosity area fraction and pore size distributions. Adds a low-bias field-of-view sampling plan and replicate pads per power level to enable basic statistics without requiring micro-CT.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Grinding/polishing media/consumables included with the kit

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Independent variable: Laser power (e.g., 4–5 levels spanning the machine’s validated stable range; for example, P1 < P2 < P3 < P4 within that window).
  - Held constant:
    - Scan speed (single value)
    - Powder feed rate (single value)
    - Hatch spacing and track overlap (single value)
    - Layer thickness and number of layers (e.g., 5–8 layers to make ~3–4 mm thick pads)
    - Shielding gas: argon flow rate and shroud configuration
    - Build geometry: square pads, e.g., 10 mm × 10 mm
    - Inter-layer dwell (constant)
  - Replication: 3 pads per power level to assess variability.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections at mid-pad and near-top regions; acquire a fixed number of fields per sample (e.g., 10 fields at 200–500×).
  - Porosity area fraction (%) via thresholded image analysis or point-counting.
  - Pore size distribution and morphology classification (lack-of-fusion vs keyhole indicators).
  - Qualitative observations of melt track stability and balling in as-built surfaces (optional SEM of top surface).
  - Output plots: porosity vs laser power; pore size statistics vs laser power.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At high power, transition to keyhole mode may cause extensive porosity or spatter, compromising build integrity; at low power, lack-of-fusion porosity may dominate. Either can prevent producing analyzable cross-sections. Mitigation: begin with a scouting build at the center of the validated window; expand outward incrementally while monitoring surface quality.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Quantitative image analysis software is assumed to be available on the SEM workstation or a general lab computer; if not, a manual point-count grid can be used but increases labor and uncertainty.
  - No micro-CT is available; analysis is 2D cross-section based, assuming representativeness.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Fabricate a “power-ladder” DED NiTi wall composed of five sequential segments, each built at a different laser power within the machine’s validated stable window, while holding all other parameters constant. Prepare cross-sections from the mid-length of each segment for SEM imaging to quantify porosity (area fraction, pore size distribution, pore morphology). Include three identical ladders (replicates) in one build to assess repeatability.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps rely on available materials (NiTi powder, argon) and equipment (DED machine for fabrication with argon shielding, precision saw for sectioning, mounting press and resin for mounting, grinding/polishing kit for preparation, SEM for imaging). No external tools or consumables beyond the BOM are required for porosity quantification via unetched cross-sections.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding and operation within a validated stable laser power window for DED NiTi.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  Source: paper_002_chunk_01

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  Consolidate multiple laser power conditions into a single “power-ladder” artifact per build to minimize build-to-build variability and thermal history differences. This reduces powder use and time, while allowing direct within-part comparison of porosity versus power. The ladder approach is an adaptation inspired by the literature’s general guidance but not claimed as novel.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power at five levels spanning the machine’s validated stable window for NiTi (e.g., P1 < P2 < P3 < P4 < P5, evenly spaced within the pre-validated min–max).
  - Held constant: scan speed, hatch spacing (if using multi-track walls), layer thickness, powder feed rate, argon flow rate, focus/spot size, track count and wall height.
  - Geometry: Straight wall, single-bead-width with consistent layer count per segment; segment length ~10–15 mm to ensure steady-state.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections (unetched) at each power segment:
    - Pore area fraction (%) via image thresholding on backscattered or secondary electron images at consistent magnification.
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter) and number density (pores/mm²).
    - Pore morphology classification: near-spherical (gas) vs irregular/planar (lack-of-fusion).
    - Melt pool depth/width indicators from contrast, if discernible, to contextualize fusion quality.
  - Output: Porosity metrics vs laser power curve with repeatability (mean ± stdev across three ladders).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - High power may drive Ni/Ti vaporization or keyholing, confounding pure “power vs porosity” trends; low power may induce lack-of-fusion porosity. Mitigation: restrict to the validated stable window; inspect for morphology to distinguish gas vs lack-of-fusion pores. Ensure robust argon shielding to prevent oxidation that could alter wetting and porosity.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED machine’s stable laser power window for NiTi is already established and accessible.
  - Assumes basic SEM image measurement/thresholding capability on the SEM workstation or equivalent in-lab software for area fraction analysis. If not available, pore counting and manual measurement can be used but will increase uncertainty.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

content.text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Fabricate a “power-ladder” DED NiTi wall composed of five sequential segments, each built at a different laser power within the machine’s validated stable window, while holding all other parameters constant. Prepare cross-sections from the mid-length of each segment for SEM imaging to quantify porosity (area fraction, pore size distribution, pore morphology). Include three identical ladders (replicates) in one build to assess repeatability.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps rely on available materials (NiTi powder, argon) and equipment (DED machine for fabrication with argon shielding, precision saw for sectioning, mounting press and resin for mounting, grinding/polishing kit for preparation, SEM for imaging). No external tools or consumables beyond the BOM are required for porosity quantification via unetched cross-sections.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of argon shielding and operation within a validated stable laser power window for DED NiTi.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.
  Source: paper_002_chunk_01

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  Consolidate multiple laser power conditions into a single “power-ladder” artifact per build to minimize build-to-build variability and thermal history differences. This reduces powder use and time, while allowing direct within-part comparison of porosity versus power. The ladder approach is an adaptation inspired by the literature’s general guidance but not claimed as novel.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power at five levels spanning the machine’s validated stable window for NiTi (e.g., P1 < P2 < P3 < P4 < P5, evenly spaced within the pre-validated min–max).
  - Held constant: scan speed, hatch spacing (if using multi-track walls), layer thickness, powder feed rate, argon flow rate, focus/spot size, track count and wall height.
  - Geometry: Straight wall, single-bead-width with consistent layer count per segment; segment length ~10–15 mm to ensure steady-state.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections (unetched) at each power segment:
    - Pore area fraction (%) via image thresholding on backscattered or secondary electron images at consistent magnification.
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter) and number density (pores/mm²).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - High power may drive Ni/Ti vaporization or keyholing, confounding pure “power vs porosity” trends; low power may induce lack-of-fusion porosity. Mitigation: restrict to the validated stable window; inspect for morphology to distinguish gas vs lack-of-fusion pores. Ensure robust argon shielding to prevent oxidation that could alter wetting and porosity.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:

  - Assumes basic SEM image measurement/thresholding capability on the SEM workstation or equivalent in-lab software for area fraction analysis. If not available, pore counting and manual measurement can be used but will increase uncertainty.

[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01
DOI:

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric laser power levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a set of single-track, single-layer NiTi deposits using DED under argon while sweeping laser power across a stable operating window; section the tracks, mount, grind, and polish cross-sections; quantify porosity area fraction and pore morphology via SEM to establish the relationship between laser power and porosity.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps—DED deposition with argon shielding, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM imaging—are supported by the provided materials (NiTi powder, argon gas, mounting resin) and equipment (DED machine, precision saw, mounting press, grinding/polishing kit, SEM).

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_002_chunk_01”: Use of argon shielding in DED NiTi; operation within a validated stable laser power window; sample preparation workflow (section, mount, grind, polish) for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Implement a fine-grained laser power sweep within the stable window using identical single-track deposits to isolate laser power effects on porosity while holding other parameters constant. Include replicate tracks per power level to estimate variability and differentiate lack-of-fusion vs keyhole-induced porosity by morphology in SEM.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Laser power (e.g., 150, 175, 200, 225, 250, 275, 300 W) spanning the machine’s validated stable window for NiTi per in-house practice informed by literature.
  - Held constant (example values; keep fixed across all builds):
    - Scan speed: 600 mm/min
    - Powder feed rate: 6 g/min
    - Spot size (defocus): fixed to achieve ~800 µm nominal melt pool width
    - Track length: 20 mm
    - Shielding argon: 15–20 L/min to maintain inert atmosphere
    - Substrate: same alloy baseplate (if your DED requires), preheated/at ambient as per standard practice; do not vary
    - Overlap: single-track, single-pass (no hatch)
  - Replicates: n = 3 tracks per power level on the same substrate with ≥3 mm spacing.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections at mid-length of each track (and optionally near-start and near-end for consistency).
  - Porosity metrics:
    - Area fraction porosity (%) per cross-section.
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter bins, e.g., <10 µm, 10–30 µm, >30 µm).
    - Morphology classification: lack-of-fusion (irregular, inter-track-shaped) vs keyhole (spherical/elongated sub-surface) vs gas pores.
  - Melt pool geometry (depth and width) from SEM to correlate with porosity trends.
  - Output: Porosity vs laser power curve with variability (replicates), and qualitative transition points indicating lack-of-fusion at low power and keyhole onset at higher power.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At very low power, discontinuous tracks or severe lack-of-fusion may preclude meaningful cross-sectioning.
  - At very high power, excessive keyholing or spatter may destabilize the track and bias porosity upward; Ni evaporation could locally shift composition, affecting wetting and pore formation.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the SEM allows basic image export and either built-in measurement tools or access to any image analysis method for area fraction estimation. If not available, porosity quantification would rely on manual point-counting on printed SEM images, which increases uncertainty.
  - Assumes access to a suitable NiTi-compatible substrate/baseplate if required by the DED machine (not listed in BOM). If the machine mandates a substrate not included here, procurement would be needed or reuse an in-house standard plate.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01
DOI:

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Build a matrix of small DED-fabricated NiTi coupons while sweeping laser power within and slightly beyond the literature-stated stable window, then quantify porosity via SEM on polished cross-sections to map the relationship between power and pore fraction/morphology.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps—from DED building under argon shielding, through sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, to SEM imaging—are supported by the listed equipment and materials. No additional metrology beyond SEM is required to quantify porosity from cross-sections.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
The experiment leverages the literature-backed elements that DED NiTi uses argon shielding, that there exists a validated stable operating window for laser power, and that sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing followed by SEM cross-section imaging can be used to quantify porosity. (Source: paper_002_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Extend the laser power sweep to include points just below and just above the reported stable window to intentionally probe transitions between lack-of-fusion and keyhole regimes, while holding all other parameters constant. Include triplicate builds at each power and randomized build order to reduce machine drift and thermal history bias. Classify pore morphology (irregular inter-track pores vs near-spherical keyhole pores) alongside area fraction to delineate regime boundaries.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine
- SEM
- Precision saw
- Mounting press
- Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder
- Argon gas
- Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Primary sweep: Laser power (e.g., 150, 200, 250, 300, 350 W; adjust to match machine capability; include 1–2 points just below and above the stable window indicated for NiTi)
- Hold constant across all builds: scan speed (e.g., 600 mm/min), hatch spacing (e.g., 100 μm), layer thickness (e.g., 50 μm), powder feed rate (fixed), argon shield flow (fixed), focus position (on-surface), and path strategy (e.g., 10x10 mm, 5-layer square islands with alternating 90° hatch)
- Replicates: 3 coupons per power level; randomized build order

- Measurements / Outputs:
- SEM-based porosity area fraction from polished cross-sections (at least three fields of view per coupon, mid-height), using consistent imaging conditions and thresholding
- Pore size distribution (equivalent circular diameter) and morphology classification (irregular lack-of-fusion vs near-spherical/keyhole)
- Melt pool depth/width from etched contrast in cross-sections to relate energy input to melt geometry
- Power–porosity curve with confidence intervals; identification of transition powers between lack-of-fusion, optimal, and keyhole regimes

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
At higher powers, excessive keyholing or vaporization may cause spatter and unmelted defects that confound simple monotonic trends; at very low power, lack-of-fusion may prevent coupon integrity. Ni loss at high power could also alter melt behavior and pore formation.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Assumes the DED machine can reliably set and hold the specified laser power range and maintain argon shielding quality comparable to the literature. Real-time oxygen level monitoring and compositional analysis are not available in the BOM; effects of oxidation or Ni evaporation cannot be directly measured here.

- Source Papers Used:
- paper_002_chunk_01

[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_002_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how laser power affects porosity in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process uses argon shielding and a validated stable operating window for laser power. Samples can be sectioned, mounted, ground, and polished for SEM cross-section imaging to quantify porosity.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a set of NiTi DED multi-track pads while sweeping laser power within a validated stable window, then quantify porosity from SEM cross-sections. Specifically, fabricate 5-pad arrays with laser power levels P1–P5 (e.g., 160, 200, 240, 280, 320 W) at fixed scan speed, hatch spacing, powder feed, and argon shielding. Section each pad at mid-length, mount, grind/polish, and acquire SEM images to measure pore area fraction and pore size distribution versus laser power.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps—argon-shielded DED deposition, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM imaging—are directly supported by the listed equipment and materials. Porosity can be quantified from SEM cross-sections without needing additional consumables.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Argon shielding during DED NiTi.
  - Operating within a validated stable laser power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing for SEM cross-sectional porosity quantification.
  (All from: paper_002_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Power-ladder pad array in a single build: deposit five adjacent 10 × 10 × 3 mm pads on one substrate, each with a different laser power, to minimize build-to-build variability and thermal history differences. Include two identical arrays on the same plate for replication (n=2).
  - Standardized imaging protocol: fixed magnification and imaging area (e.g., three fields at pad center and edges) to reduce operator bias and enable consistent pore statistics across powers.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Laser power: 5 levels within the machine’s validated stable window for NiTi (e.g., 160, 200, 240, 280, 320 W)
  - Hold constant: scan speed (e.g., 600 mm/min), hatch spacing (e.g., 0.5× spot size), layer thickness (e.g., 200 µm), powder feed rate (e.g., 6 g/min), stand-off, spot size, argon flow rate (e.g., per machine-recommended shielding settings)
  - Geometry: multi-track pads, 6–8 layers to reach ~3 mm thickness; identical toolpath for all pads

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections: acquire standardized fields (center and two edge regions per pad; replicate array provides n=2 pads per power)
  - Quantify:
    - Porosity area fraction (%) by threshold-based area measurement on SEM images (using SEM measurement tools)
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter), and number density (pores/mm²)
    - Optional: melt pool depth/width from cross-sections to correlate with porosity
  - Output: Porosity vs. laser power curve with error bars; identify power threshold(s) where porosity sharply increases

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating outside the stable power window could cause excessive balling or lack of fusion, leading to unusable pads or misleading porosity spikes. Mitigation: pre-verify bead stability at each power with a short single-track test before building the full pads; if instability observed, adjust powers inward toward the validated window.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED system allows per-pad laser power programming and stable argon shielding flow control (common on DED machines). Assumes the SEM has basic measurement/threshold tools for area and length; if not, pore counting would need manual grid methods.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

content.text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a set of NiTi DED multi-track pads while sweeping laser power within a validated stable window, then quantify porosity from SEM cross-sections. Specifically, fabricate 5-pad arrays with laser power levels P1–P5 (e.g., 160, 200, 240, 280, 320 W) at fixed scan speed, hatch spacing, powder feed, and argon shielding. Section each pad at mid-length, mount, grind/polish, and acquire SEM images to measure pore area fraction and pore size distribution versus laser power.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps—argon-shielded DED deposition, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and SEM imaging—are directly supported by the listed equipment and materials. Porosity can be quantified from SEM cross-sections without needing additional consumables.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Argon shielding during DED NiTi.
  - Operating within a validated stable laser power window.
  - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing for SEM cross-sectional porosity quantification.
  (All from: paper_002_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Power-ladder pad array in a single build: deposit five adjacent 10 × 10 × 3 mm pads on one substrate, each with a different laser power, to minimize build-to-build variability and thermal history differences. Include two identical arrays on the same plate for replication (n=2).
  - Standardized imaging protocol: fixed magnification and imaging area (e.g., three fields at pad center and edges) to reduce operator bias and enable consistent pore statistics across powers.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - SEM
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Laser power: 5 levels within the machine’s validated stable window for NiTi (e.g., 160, 200, 240, 280, 320 W)
  - Hold constant: scan speed (e.g., 600 mm/min), hatch spacing (e.g., 0.5× spot size), layer thickness (e.g., 200 µm), powder feed rate (e.g., 6 g/min), stand-off, spot size, argon flow rate (e.g., per machine-recommended shielding settings)
  - Geometry: multi-track pads, 6–8 layers to reach ~3 mm thickness; identical toolpath for all pads

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - SEM cross-sections: acquire standardized fields (center and two edge regions per pad; replicate array provides n=2 pads per power)
  - Quantify:
    - Porosity area fraction (%) by threshold-based area measurement on SEM images (using SEM measurement tools)
    - Pore size distribution (equivalent circle diameter), and number density (pores/mm²)
    - Optional: melt pool depth/width from cross-sections to correlate with porosity
  - Output: Porosity vs. laser power curve with error bars; identify power threshold(s) where porosity sharply increases

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating outside the stable power window could cause excessive balling or lack of fusion, leading to unusable pads or misleading porosity spikes. Mitigation: pre-verify bead stability at each power with a short single-track test before building the full pads; if instability observed, adjust powers inward toward the validated window.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED system allows per-pad laser power programming and stable argon shielding flow control (common on DED machines). Assumes the SEM has basic measurement/threshold tools for area and length; if not, pore counting would need manual grid methods.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_002_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric scan speed levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-bead and short-wall scan-speed sweep in DED NiTi: deposit (a) single-pass beads-on-plate at 5–7 discrete scan speeds within the validated stable window, and (b) 10-layer straight walls at the same speeds. Measure track width from top-view optical micrographs of single beads and build height from caliper step-height on single beads and total wall height (to compute height-per-layer).

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All processing is standard DED with NiTi powder under argon shielding.
  - Track width can be quantified from top-view optical microscopy.
  - Build height can be quantified by digital calipers as step height (single bead) and total wall height (multi-layer).
  - No additional metrology (e.g., profilometry) is required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: there is a validated scan-speed window for stable DED NiTi deposition, and top-view optical microscopy plus caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Include both single-bead and 10-layer wall builds at identical scan speeds to decouple instantaneous bead geometry (width) from cumulative build behavior (effective height-per-layer), enabling correlation between first-pass melt pool stability and vertical build efficiency versus speed using only simple metrology.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., 5–7 evenly spaced levels within the machine’s validated stable window for NiTi as referenced in “paper_003_chunk_01”).
  - Hold constant across all runs: laser power, spot size/defocus, powder feed rate, standoff/work distance, shielding gas flow, substrate preheat (if any), and path length (e.g., 40–60 mm straight tracks).
  - Build designs:
    - Single-bead: one pass per speed.
    - Short wall: 10 layers at each speed, same path, minimal interlayer dwell (keep constant).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (single-bead): capture top-view optical images at 3 evenly spaced locations per bead; measure bead width in each image and report mean ± standard deviation versus speed.
  - Build height:
    - Single-bead step height: measure with calipers at 3 locations per bead; report mean ± standard deviation versus speed.
    - Wall height: measure total height at 3 locations per wall with calipers; compute effective height-per-layer (total/10) and report versus speed.
  - Qualitative defect mapping: note humping, balling, lack-of-fusion, spatter accumulation at each speed.
  - Outputs: curves of width vs. speed, height-per-layer vs. speed, and a speed window indicating stable geometry and efficient vertical buildup.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At speeds near window edges, beads may show humping/balling or insufficient fusion, compromising width/height measurements; calipers may have limited resolution for very small single-bead heights. Mitigation: replicate tracks, use walls for amplified height signal, and exclude obviously defective beads from quantitative averaging while still recording defect occurrence.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible flat substrate/build plate (preferably NiTi) for beads-on-plate and wall deposition; this substrate is not explicitly listed in the BOM. If only a generic DED build plate is available, assume it is metallurgically compatible with NiTi for preliminary geometry tests.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01
