available_bom.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric scan speed levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  - Build a single-substrate “speed matrix” combining:
    1) Single-track beads (one pass each) at multiple scan speeds to quantify top-view track width.
    2) Adjacent thin walls (e.g., 10-layer stacks) at the same scan speeds to quantify build height per layer via calipers.
  - Keep laser power, powder feed rate, spot size, standoff, and shielding gas constant across all scans. Use an initial quick ladder to confirm speeds lie within the validated stable-deposition window before running full replicates.
  - Replicates: n=3 per speed for both single-track and wall.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All fabrication uses the DED machine with NiTi powder under argon shielding.
  - Track width can be measured from top-view optical microscope images.
  - Build height for multi-layer walls can be measured with digital calipers (reporting total wall height and normalizing by layer count).

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: 
    - There exists a validated scan-speed window for stable DED NiTi deposition.
    - Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks are suitable to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Combine, on a single substrate and under identical machine conditions, a paired design: single-track beads for width and co-located thin walls for height-per-layer at the same speeds. This enables a direct mapping of scan speed to both lateral spreading (width) and vertical accumulation efficiency (height per layer) without extra metrology beyond the BOM.
  - Use the multi-layer height divided by layer count as an indirect “deposition efficiency” metric versus speed, enabling comparison even without profilometry or cross-sections.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Substrate/base plate compatible with the DED system (see Missing Capability / Assumption)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., 200, 300, 400, 500, 600 mm/min; adjust to your machine’s validated stable window).
  - Hold constant: laser power, powder feed rate, spot size, nozzle standoff, layer height command, argon flow rate, and path length.
  - Structure:
    - Single-track beads: 20–30 mm length per bead; 3 replicates per speed.
    - Thin walls: 10 layers stacked along same path as single-track; 3 replicates per speed.
  - Pretest: a short ladder of speeds to verify they fall within the stable window (stop if balling or lack-of-fusion appears).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (µm): From calibrated top-view micrographs of single beads; measure at three evenly spaced positions per bead and average.
  - Build height (mm): Total thin-wall height via calipers; compute height-per-layer by dividing by the number of deposited layers.
  - Qualitative stability notes: continuity, spatter, balling, and surface roughness class from optical images.
  - Outputs: Width vs. scan speed curve; height-per-layer vs. scan speed curve; identification of speed range giving narrowest width without sacrificing height-per-layer.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating outside the stable scan-speed window can cause discontinuous tracks (balling) or insufficient build height (lack of fusion/deposition). Also, NiTi can overheat at very low speeds, causing excessive spreading and porosity.
  - Mitigation: run the initial speed ladder; start with mid-range speeds reported as stable in precedent; monitor bead continuity in situ between passes and adjust if instability is observed.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - A suitable substrate/base plate is required but not explicitly listed in the BOM. Assumption: a compatible NiTi (preferred) or standard DED build plate is available in the DED system.
  - Without a profilometer or cross-sectioning tools, true cross-sectional bead geometry and porosity cannot be measured; the study relies on top-view width and caliper height as proxies.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01
DOI:

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric scan speed levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Sweep DED scan speed across a validated stable window and quantify its effect on single-track width (top-view) and multi-layer build height (caliper) in NiTi. For each scan-speed setting, print (a) three replicate single-bead tracks to measure track width and (b) one straight 10-layer wall (single bead stacked in place) to measure build height per layer.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  It uses only the DED machine for deposition, argon shielding, NiTi powder as feedstock, and post-build measurements with an optical microscope (top-view width) and digital calipers (height). No additional metrology or consumables are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of a validated scan-speed window for stable NiTi DED deposition.
  - Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.
  Source: paper_003_chunk_01

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Pair single-track width measurements with a stacked single-bead wall at the same scan speeds to directly relate track width to vertical build efficiency (height per layer) under otherwise fixed parameters.
  - Randomized run order and standardized inter-pass dwell to reduce thermal history bias across scan speeds.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (select 5–7 levels spanning the validated stable window cited in the literature; equally spaced or logarithmically spaced).
  - Hold constant across all runs: laser power, powder feed rate, layer height command (z-increment), shielding gas flow, standoff distance, track length (e.g., ~40–50 mm), and substrate preheat setting (if any).
  - Controls for thermal effects: fixed inter-pass dwell (e.g., 60–120 s) between layers for the multi-layer wall; randomized order of scan-speed conditions.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width: For each scan speed, print three replicate single-bead tracks; capture top-view optical micrographs; measure width at three evenly spaced positions per track; report mean ± SD.
  - Build height: For each scan speed, print a 10-layer straight wall (single bead repeatedly stacked on the same path); measure total height at three positions with calipers; compute mean height per layer (total height/10).
  - Optional derived metric: Approximate deposition efficiency proxy via width–height coupling, e.g., semi-elliptical cross-section estimate A ≈ (π/4)·width·(height per layer), reported as a comparative, not absolute, metric across speeds.
  - Output plots/tables: track width vs. scan speed; height per layer vs. scan speed; coefficient of variation to assess repeatability.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating near the lower speed limit may cause excessive heat input, leading to wide beads, sagging, or keyholing; near the upper limit, insufficient fusion or discontinuous tracks may occur. Mitigation: restrict to the validated stable window; start with a short pilot pass at each speed; monitor bead continuity before full replication.
  - Thermal accumulation in multi-layer walls can bias height per layer. Mitigation: fixed inter-pass dwell and randomized condition order.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:

content.text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Sweep DED scan speed across a validated stable window and quantify its effect on single-track width (top-view) and multi-layer build height (caliper) in NiTi. For each scan-speed setting, print (a) three replicate single-bead tracks to measure track width and (b) one straight 10-layer wall (single bead stacked in place) to measure build height per layer.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  It uses only the DED machine for deposition, argon shielding, NiTi powder as feedstock, and post-build measurements with an optical microscope (top-view width) and digital calipers (height). No additional metrology or consumables are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of a validated scan-speed window for stable NiTi DED deposition.
  - Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.
  Source: paper_003_chunk_01

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Pair single-track width measurements with a stacked single-bead wall at the same scan speeds to directly relate track width to vertical build efficiency (height per layer) under otherwise fixed parameters.
  - Randomized run order and standardized inter-pass dwell to reduce thermal history bias across scan speeds.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (select 5–7 levels spanning the validated stable window cited in the literature; equally spaced or logarithmically spaced).
  - Hold constant across all runs: laser power, powder feed rate, layer height command (z-increment), shielding gas flow, standoff distance, track length (e.g., ~40–50 mm), and substrate preheat setting (if any).
  - Controls for thermal effects: fixed inter-pass dwell (e.g., 60–120 s) between layers for the multi-layer wall; randomized order of scan-speed conditions.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width: For each scan speed, print three replicate single-bead tracks; capture top-view optical micrographs; measure width at three evenly spaced positions per track; report mean ± SD.
  - Build height: For each scan speed, print a 10-layer straight wall (single bead repeatedly stacked on the same path); measure total height at three positions with calipers; compute mean height per layer (total height/10).
  - Optional derived metric: Approximate deposition efficiency proxy via width–height coupling, e.g., semi-elliptical cross-section estimate A ≈ (π/4)·width·(height per layer), reported as a comparative, not absolute, metric across speeds.
  - Output plots/tables: track width vs. scan speed; height per layer vs. scan speed; coefficient of variation to assess repeatability.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating near the lower speed limit may cause excessive heat input, leading to wide beads, sagging, or keyholing; near the upper limit, insufficient fusion or discontinuous tracks may occur. Mitigation: restrict to the validated stable window; start with a short pilot pass at each speed; monitor bead continuity before full replication.
  - Thermal accumulation in multi-layer walls can bias height per layer. Mitigation: fixed inter-pass dwell and randomized condition order.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Substrate/baseplate material is not listed in the BOM. Assumption: a compatible NiTi (or other machine-approved) substrate/coupon and fixturing are available in the DED system for bead deposition.
  - No profilometry or cross-sectional polishing is available; width and height rely on top-view microscopy and calipers as proxies for geometry.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-track and multi-layer step-wall depositions of NiTi across a validated scan-speed window to quantify how scan speed affects (a) single-bead track width and (b) multi-layer build height per layer. For each scan-speed level, deposit:
  1) Three 30 mm single tracks for top-view width measurement.
  2) One 10-layer, 30 mm-long wall for height-per-layer measurement.
  Keep all other parameters constant. Randomize the order of scan-speed runs to mitigate drift.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - The DED machine can vary scan speed while holding other settings fixed.
  - Top-view optical microscopy supports track-width measurement.
  - Digital calipers can measure multi-layer wall height with sufficient resolution for typical DED layer heights.
  - NiTi powder and argon enable shielded NiTi deposition.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: A validated scan-speed window for stable DED NiTi deposition exists, and top-view optical microscopy plus caliper checks are suitable to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Use a single coupon with a “step-wall” comprising adjacent 10-layer wall segments, each built at a different scan speed, to reduce variability from part-to-part differences while still isolating scan-speed effects. Segments are separated by 5 mm gaps to limit heat overlap. Single-track beads for width are also placed on the same coupon, but spatially separated from the step-wall to avoid thermal history coupling.
  - Randomize the sequence of scan-speed segments and interleave with single-track runs to minimize confounding by machine warm-up or powder flow drift.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (select 5–7 levels spanning the validated stable window reported for DED NiTi).
  - Hold constant: laser power, powder mass flow rate, standoff/spot size, shielding gas flow, layer height command, track length, inter-track spacing, inter-layer dwell.
  - Replication: n=3 single tracks per speed; one 10-layer wall per speed.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (single tracks): For each bead, acquire top-view optical images at 3 evenly spaced positions (10, 15, 20 mm). Compute mean width and standard deviation per speed.
  - Build height (multi-layer walls): Measure total wall height at three positions along the length using digital calipers; compute mean and standard deviation. Derive average height per layer = total height/10.
  - Stability notes: Record presence/absence of balling, discontinuities, or excessive spatter in optical images.
  - Optional derived metric: Normalize width and height-per-layer versus scan speed to visualize trends across the window.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At the low end of scan speed, excessive heat input may cause over-widened beads and collapse between layers; at the high end, lack of fusion or bead discontinuity may occur, yielding unreliable width/height data. Limited caliper resolution may under-resolve very small single-bead heights, hence the use of 10-layer walls for height measurement.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes access to a compatible metallic substrate (preferably NiTi plate) and fixturing for DED; not listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the DED machine allows setting and logging laser power, powder feed rate, and shielding flow to maintain them constant across the scan-speed sweep.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-track DED depositions of NiTi on a flat coupon while sweeping scan speed within the validated stable window; quantify resulting track width (top-view optical microscopy) and build height (step height by calipers) to map width- and height-vs-speed relationships.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All processing can be done on the available DED machine using NiTi powder under argon shielding.
  - Top-view track width can be measured on the optical microscope.
  - Build height (step between substrate and deposited bead) can be measured with digital calipers, as supported in the literature card.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition, and top-view optical microscopy plus caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Systematic, center-out speed sweep: begin at the mid-point of the validated scan-speed window for this machine/material, then expand symmetrically to faster and slower speeds while holding laser power and powder feed constant, to establish both the linear regime and onset of instability. Include repeat beads (n=3) at each speed for simple statistics and to check process repeatability using only BOM-listed tools.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., select 5–7 levels spanning the machine’s validated stable window for NiTi per prior runs/documentation; start at midpoint, then ± increments).
  - Held constant: Laser power, powder feed rate, standoff/nozzle height, shielding gas flow, and track length (e.g., 20–30 mm straight beads).
  - Deposition strategy: Single-pass, single-track beads on a flat coupon with sufficient run-in and run-out (e.g., 5 mm) to avoid acceleration artifacts in the measurement region.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width: Measure bead top width in the central 10 mm region using the optical microscope; take 3–5 evenly spaced measurements per bead and average.
  - Build height: Measure step height between substrate and bead crest with digital calipers at 3 positions in the same central region and average.
  - Process notes: Qualitative stability observations (e.g., balling, spatter, lack of fusion) recorded for each speed.
  - Output plots/tables: Track width vs. scan speed; build height vs. scan speed; coefficient of variation across repeats to assess repeatability.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Exceeding the stable scan-speed window can produce balling or insufficient melting, yielding unusable beads and unreliable measurements. Mitigation: begin at the validated mid-point speed, expand in small increments, and stop expanding once clear instability is observed.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible flat substrate/coupon (ideally NiTi or a compatible alloy plate) and basic fixturing; these are not explicitly listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the digital calipers have sufficient resolution to resolve bead height differences typical for the selected parameter set (literature card indicates calipers are acceptable for comparative checks).

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

Title: paper_003_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  - Single-track-and-wall study of scan speed effects in DED NiTi:
    1) Single-track width mapping: deposit straight, 30–50 mm single beads at multiple scan speeds while holding laser power, powder feed rate, stand-off, spot size, and argon flow constant.
    2) Multi-pass wall build-rate mapping: at the same scan speeds, deposit 20 passes on the same path (retrace) to form a narrow wall; measure final wall height to quantify vertical build per pass vs scan speed.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - Uses only NiTi powder and argon in a DED machine.
  - Track width can be measured by top-view optical microscopy; wall height can be measured with digital calipers.
  - No cross-sectioning, profilometry, or advanced metrology required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: 
    - There exists a validated scan-speed window for stable DED NiTi deposition.
    - Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Bracket the literature-backed stable scan-speed window by including a few speeds just below and above it to map onset of instability (e.g., balling/lack of fusion) and define practical limits for width/height control.
  - Pair single-bead width mapping with a same-path multi-pass “wall” to convert tiny single-bead height changes into caliper-resolvable wall height, enabling build-rate vs scan-speed analysis without a profilometer.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging of beads)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., select 6–8 levels spanning the known stable window and ± two out-of-window points).
  - Hold constant: laser power, powder feed rate, spot size, stand-off, shielding gas flow, track length, toolpath, and substrate precondition.
  - Replicates: n=3 per speed for both single-bead and wall builds to capture variability.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Single-track width (optical microscope, top view; measure at 5 evenly spaced positions per bead and average).
  - Qualitative stability flags per track (continuous bead, balling, spatter, lack of fusion).
  - Wall height after 20 passes at each scan speed (digital calipers; measure at 3 positions and average).
  - Derived metrics: 
    - Width vs scan speed curve with stability overlay.
    - Build height per pass vs scan speed (wall height/20), as a proxy for vertical build rate.
    - Identification of practical scan-speed limits that meet both width and build-rate targets.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At high scan speeds: lack of fusion leading to discontinuous beads and poor wall integrity.
  - At low scan speeds: excessive heat input, balling, or excessive reinforcement that skews width/height.
  - Metrology resolution: single-bead height may be below caliper resolution; mitigated by multi-pass wall approach.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible metallic substrate plate (e.g., NiTi, Ti, or compatible steel) and fixturing within the DED machine; not listed in the BOM. If unavailable, the experiment is infeasible.
  - Assumes the DED machine can hold laser power, powder feed, and spot size constant across runs and can execute repeat passes on the same path.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

  - Pair single-bead width mapping with a same-path multi-pass “wall” to convert tiny single-bead height changes into caliper-resolvable wall height, enabling build-rate vs scan-speed analysis without a profilometer.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging of beads)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., select 6–8 levels spanning the known stable window and ± two out-of-window points).
  - Hold constant: laser power, powder feed rate, spot size, stand-off, shielding gas flow, track length, toolpath, and substrate precondition.
  - Replicates: n=3 per speed for both single-bead and wall builds to capture variability.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Single-track width (optical microscope, top view; measure at 5 evenly spaced positions per bead and average).
  - Qualitative stability flags per track (continuous bead, balling, spatter, lack of fusion).
  - Wall height after 20 passes at each scan speed (digital calipers; measure at 3 positions and average).
  - Derived metrics: 
    - Width vs scan speed curve with stability overlay.
    - Build height per pass vs scan speed (wall height/20), as a proxy for vertical build rate.
    - Identification of practical scan-speed limits that meet both width and build-rate targets.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At high scan speeds: lack of fusion leading to discontinuous beads and poor wall integrity.
  - At low scan speeds: excessive heat input, balling, or excessive reinforcement that skews width/height.
  - Metrology resolution: single-bead height may be below caliper resolution; mitigated by multi-pass wall approach.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible metallic substrate plate (e.g., NiTi, Ti, or compatible steel) and fixturing within the DED machine; not listed in the BOM. If unavailable, the experiment is infeasible.
  - Assumes the DED machine can hold laser power, powder feed, and spot size constant across runs and can execute repeat passes on the same path.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-track and multi-layer wall builds of NiTi via DED across a scan-speed sweep within the validated stable window, to quantify the effect of scan speed on (a) single-track width (top-view optical microscopy) and (b) multi-layer build height per unit length (digital calipers).

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  The DED machine and NiTi powder enable producing single tracks and simple straight walls at different scan speeds under argon. Track width can be measured from top-view images on the optical microscope, and wall height can be measured with digital calipers after stacking enough layers to exceed caliper resolution. No cross-sectioning or additional metrology is required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From "paper_003_chunk_01": Use of a validated scan-speed window for stable NiTi DED deposition, and use of top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Pair each speed with two coordinated builds: (1) a single-track on bare substrate for accurate width measurement, and (2) a 10-layer single-bead wall (same path and speed) to amplify height for reliable caliper measurement. This decouples width (first layer geometry) from cumulative height effects while keeping all other parameters constant.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Assumed: flat metal substrate plate compatible with the DED system (e.g., NiTi or steel build plate)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., choose 5 levels evenly spaced within the machine’s validated stable window for NiTi; confirm by a quick pre-check for melt stability).
  - Held constant across all builds: laser power, powder feed rate, spot size/focus, stand-off distance, shielding gas flow, hatch (N/A for single track), path length (e.g., 30–50 mm straight line), layer Z-increment for walls, interpass dwell time, substrate preheat status (if any).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (µm): For each speed, acquire top-view optical images of the single track at three evenly spaced positions along the track; compute mean and standard deviation of width.
  - Build height (mm): For each speed, print a 10-layer straight wall over the same path; after cooling, measure wall height at three positions with calipers; compute mean and standard deviation. Report height per layer (height/10) and height per unit length if desired.
  - Qualitative stability notes: presence of balling, discontinuities, or spatter for each speed (from visual inspection and microscopy).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Speeds near the edges of the window may cause balling or lack of fusion, yielding discontinuous tracks that confound width/height measurements. Mitigation: perform a brief pre-check at candidate speeds and adjust to remain within visibly stable melt regimes; increase layer count only after confirming single-track continuity.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible flat substrate/build plate and standard DED parameter control (laser power, powder feed, focus) integral to the DED machine. If a substrate is not available in the lab, this must be procured; otherwise the experiment cannot proceed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Print single-track beads and short multi-layer walls of NiTi via DED across a controlled range of scan speeds within the machine’s stable-deposition window, then quantify (a) track width from top-view optical microscopy and (b) build height from caliper measurements of the walls.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  The DED machine can vary scan speed while holding other parameters fixed. Top-view optical microscope images allow bead-width measurement, and digital calipers can measure the height of short walls. Only NiTi powder and argon shielding are required for deposition.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  From “paper_003_chunk_01”: 
  - A validated scan-speed window for stable DED NiTi deposition exists.
  - Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Split the build into two geometries at each scan speed: 
    1) Single-pass beads for accurate width measurement.
    2) Short 10-layer walls (stacked tracks in place) to amplify Z so calipers can more reliably resolve build height differences versus scan speed.
  - Use constant linear energy input target (laser power/scan speed) in one sub-series and constant power in another sub-series to decouple pure speed effects from energy-density changes, enabling two complementary datasets:
    - Series A (constant power): isolates practical “what happens if I only change speed.”
    - Series B (constant linear energy): evaluates geometry sensitivity at similar nominal energy input.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (select 5–7 levels spanning the machine’s validated stable-deposition window for NiTi; e.g., low, mid, high within that window).
  - Hold constant for Series A: laser power, powder feed rate, standoff/defocus, shielding gas flow, track length, interlayer dwell.
  - Adjust for Series B: vary laser power proportionally with scan speed to keep approximate linear energy input constant; other parameters held fixed as above.
  - Geometry:
    - Beads: single-pass, 20–40 mm long, on flat substrate.
    - Walls: 10 layers, same path stacked, no hatch, same track length.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (beads): top-view optical micrographs; measure average width over central 10–20 mm, excluding start/stop transients; n≥3 repeats per speed.
  - Build height (walls): digital calipers; measure wall height at 3 evenly spaced positions; report mean ± stdev per speed.
  - Qualitative stability notes: continuous bead vs balling/lack of fusion vs excessive swelling; simple visual scoring from optical images.
  - Outputs: width vs scan speed curves (Series A and B), height vs scan speed curves (Series A and B), plus a simple process map highlighting stable vs unstable observations.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Without cross-sectional metallography, subtle lack-of-fusion may be missed; surface roughness may bias caliper height readings. Multi-layer walls mitigate resolution limits but can accumulate geometry error if interlayer bonding is poor at high speeds.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Flat substrate compatible with NiTi DED (e.g., NiTi or compatible alloy plate) is assumed available; it is not listed in the BOM. If unavailable, the experiment cannot proceed.
  - Basic fixturing and surface prep (abrasion/acetone wipe) are assumed standard shop capability.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Single-track and single-wall DED builds of NiTi at multiple scan speeds to quantify how scan speed affects (a) single-bead track width and (b) multi-layer wall build height per unit length. For each scan-speed level within a validated stable-deposition window, deposit:
1) Three replicate single-pass beads (single layer) for top-view width measurement.
2) One straight single-bead wall (e.g., 20–40 mm long, ~10 layers) for caliper step-height measurement.
Keep all other parameters fixed (laser power, powder feed rate, stand-off, shielding flow).

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
- DED machine can vary scan speed while holding other setpoints constant.
- Optical microscope supports top-view width measurements on the single-pass beads.
- Digital calipers can measure the step height of the multi-layer wall relative to the substrate.
- Required materials (NiTi powder, argon gas) match the BOM.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
- From “paper_003_chunk_01”: Use of a validated scan-speed window for stable DED NiTi deposition; use top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
- Split the study into two complementary geometries at each scan speed: single-pass beads to isolate track width, and multi-layer single-bead walls to amplify vertical growth for reliable caliper height measurement. This reduces measurement uncertainty for height while keeping width characterization on minimally confounded single beads.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine (for NiTi)
- Optical microscope (top-view imaging of tracks)
- Digital calipers (step-height of multi-layer wall)

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder
- Argon gas (shielding)
- Substrate plate compatible with DED NiTi (see assumption)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Primary sweep: Scan speed (select 4–6 evenly spaced levels within the validated stable window referenced in the literature card).
- Hold constant across all runs: laser power, powder feed rate, stand-off distance, shielding gas flow, layer height increment, and path length.
- Replicates: at least n=3 single-pass beads per speed; one multi-layer wall per speed.

- Measurements / Outputs:
- Track width: Measure bead width from top-view optical microscope images at three positions (start, mid, end) for each replicate; report mean ± SD per speed.
- Build height: Measure wall step height with calipers at three positions along the wall; divide by number of deposited layers to estimate average layer-wise height; report mean ± SD per speed.
- Outputs: Plots of track width vs. scan speed and build height per unit length (or per layer) vs. scan speed; tabulate setpoints used.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
- Risk: Single-layer height may be too small for accurate caliper resolution.
- Mitigation: Use multi-layer walls (e.g., ~10 layers) to increase measurable step height; verify caliper repeatability on a gauge block or substrate edge before measurement.
- Additional risk: Operating outside the stable scan-speed window may cause balling/lack of fusion; mitigate with a short preliminary bead trial at each speed and stay within stable window.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Assumption: Availability of a flat, compatible substrate plate (e.g., NiTi or steel) that can be fixtured in the DED machine. If not available, this must be procured; the BOM does not explicitly list a substrate.

- Source Papers Used:
- paper_003_chunk_01

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric scan speed levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-track scan-speed sweep plus matching multi-layer wall builds to quantify how scan speed affects (a) single-bead track width and (b) build height per layer in DED NiTi.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  Uses only the DED machine, NiTi powder, argon shielding, an optical microscope for top-view width measurements, and digital calipers for wall height after multi-layer deposition. No additional metrology or consumables are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  From “paper_003_chunk_01”: (1) There exists a validated scan-speed window for stable NiTi DED deposition; (2) top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  A two-stage, same-speed protocol to directly link single-bead width to multi-layer build height:
  1) Deposit single straight tracks at several speeds within the validated window to measure track width by optical microscopy.
  2) For the exact same speeds, build short multi-layer straight walls (e.g., 10 layers) and measure total wall height by calipers, computing height-per-layer. This amplifies vertical differences beyond caliper resolution and ties width-to-height behavior at identical speeds.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (select 5–7 levels spanning the validated stable window cited in paper_003_chunk_01).
  - Hold constant across all builds: laser power, powder feed rate, spot size/focus, argon flow, layer height command (for walls), track length, intertrack cooling time, and substrate preheat (if applicable).
  - Geometry:
    - Single-bead tracks: length ~30–50 mm per speed, 3 replicates per speed.
    - Multi-layer walls: same path and speed as single-beads, fixed number of layers (e.g., 10), 2 replicates per speed.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (single-beads): top-view optical images; measure width at three evenly spaced positions along the central 60% of each track and average per replicate.
  - Build height (multi-layer walls): total wall height by digital calipers; compute mean height-per-layer (total height / number of layers).
  - Process observations: continuity/lack of track breakup, visible balling, spatter, or porosity indications (qualitative).
  - Outputs/analysis:
    - Plot width vs scan speed.
    - Plot height-per-layer vs scan speed.
    - Report mean ± standard deviation for each speed; note any speeds showing instability per visual criteria.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At the extremes of the speed window, tracks may ball-up or under-deposit, giving unreliable widths/heights. Mitigation: confine speeds to the validated stable window and randomize run order to minimize drift effects; use three replicates for width and two for height to identify outliers.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - A compatible NiTi (or other suitable) substrate plate is required but not listed in the BOM; this is assumed available. If not, the experiment cannot be executed.
  - Assumes access to the validated scan-speed window boundaries referenced in paper_003_chunk_01 (either from internal notes or the cited source).
  - Assumes the DED machine is calibrated (focus/spot size, powder flow, and power) and can log or accurately set scan speed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01
DOI:

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric scan speed levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Deposit NiTi bead-on-plate tracks and thin multi-layer walls at a series of scan speeds within a validated DED window to quantify (a) single-track top-view width and (b) average per-layer build height. For each scan speed, print:
  1) One 20 mm long single-pass track for width measurement, and
  2) One 20 mm long, 10-layer stacked wall (same path repeated without overlap) for build height measurement. Keep laser power, powder feed rate, stand-off, and argon shielding constant across the sweep.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - The DED machine can produce single tracks and stacked thin walls from NiTi powder under argon shielding.
  - Track width can be measured via top-view optical microscope.
  - Total wall height can be measured with digital calipers; dividing by the known number of layers yields average layer height per pass.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: “The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.”
  - Directly using: (i) scan-speed window concept for stable deposition, and (ii) top-view optical microscopy and calipers as the measurement methods for width and build height, respectively.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Combine single-pass tracks (for width) with multi-layer thin walls at the same scan speeds to extract per-layer build height using only calipers, ensuring sufficient total height for reliable resolution.
  - Normalize build height by number of layers to decouple measurement resolution limits and small-bead variability, enabling a simple, instrument-light method that aligns with the available BOM.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (shielding)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., 200–800 mm/min or machine/material’s validated stable window; 5–7 evenly spaced levels).
  - Held constant across all runs: laser power (single fixed value), powder feed rate, stand-off distance, argon flow rate, path length (20 mm), interlayer dwell (fixed short pause, e.g., 10–20 s), and substrate precondition (same plate, same location spacing).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (single-pass specimens): For each track, acquire top-view optical images at 5 evenly spaced positions; measure and report mean width ± standard deviation versus scan speed.
  - Build height (multi-layer walls): Measure total wall height with calipers at 3 positions along the wall; compute mean height and divide by number of layers to obtain average per-layer build height (µm/pass); report mean ± standard deviation versus scan speed.
  - Qualitative stability notes (from visual inspection): presence of balling, lack of fusion, excessive spatter, or surface waviness at each scan speed.
  - Deliverables: plots of width vs. scan speed and average per-layer height vs. scan speed; identification of apparent stable window consistent with continuous beads and monotonic width/height trends.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At high or low scan speeds, instability (balling/lack of fusion) may prevent formation of continuous tracks or consistent layer stacking. Mitigations: constrain speeds to the validated window, add brief interlayer dwell to limit heat buildup, and increase layer count (if needed) to ensure measurable wall height without overheating.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible, fixtured substrate plate (e.g., NiTi or steel) and basic plate preparation within the DED machine; this substrate is not listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the optical microscope has sufficient field of view or stitching capability for top-view width measurement; otherwise, multiple images per location will be manually stitched.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

Title: paper_003_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Print (a) single-track beads and (b) multi-layer thin walls of NiTi at a series of scan speeds within a stable DED window, holding laser power, powder feed, and argon shielding constant. Measure track width from top-view optical micrographs of the single beads, and measure wall height with digital calipers to extract average build height per layer at each scan speed.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  The DED machine with NiTi powder and argon can deposit single beads and thin walls. Top-view optical microscopy suffices for track-width measurement, and digital calipers can measure multi-layer wall height with adequate resolution when multiple layers are stacked.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of a validated scan-speed window for stable NiTi DED deposition.
  - Use of top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.
  (From: paper_003_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Two-specimen approach at each scan speed: single-track beads to isolate lateral melt-pool spreading (track width), and 10-layer thin walls to amplify vertical growth so calipers can resolve per-layer build height. The per-layer height is computed as total wall height divided by layer count, minimizing single-layer measurement error.
  - Replicate each condition three times to quantify variability without needing additional metrology.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., 200, 300, 400, 500, 600 mm/min as placeholders; refine to the machine’s validated stable window during a brief pre-check).
  - Held constant across all conditions: laser power, powder feed rate, stand-off/working distance, shielding gas flow, layer count for walls (e.g., 10 layers), track length (e.g., 30–40 mm), interlayer dwell (e.g., 30–60 s), and substrate preheat (if available, keep constant or off).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (μm or mm): From top-view optical images of single beads; measure width at 5 evenly spaced locations per bead, average across three beads per speed.
  - Total wall height (mm): From calipers; measure height at 5 positions along each 10-layer wall, average across three walls per speed; compute average layer height = (mean wall height)/10.
  - Qualitative stability notes per speed: continuous bead vs balling/lack of fusion, oxidation/discoloration.
  - Outputs: plots of scan speed vs average track width; scan speed vs average layer height per layer; error bars from replicate variability.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating outside the stable scan-speed window may cause lack of fusion or balling, invalidating width/height comparisons. Also, rough wall tops can add caliper uncertainty.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability and fixturing of a compatible build substrate/plate for DED (e.g., NiTi or stainless steel) and basic surface cleaning; these are not listed in the BOM but are typically required.
  - Assumes the DED machine allows setting and holding constant laser power, powder feed, and gas flow; specific values are not provided.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01
DOI: 

The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric scan speed levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  - Deposit, for each scan speed within a validated stable window, two geometries on the same NiTi substrate: (1) three replicate single-pass, single-layer tracks for top-view track-width measurement; and (2) one short single-track multi-layer wall (e.g., 10 layers stacked at the same path) to amplify build height for caliper measurement. Keep all other parameters constant (laser power, spot size, powder feed rate, layer increment command, stand-off, shielding argon flow). Measure track width by optical microscopy from the top view and total wall height by digital calipers; compute average height gain per layer. Analyze how scan speed affects (a) single-bead track width and (b) per-layer build height.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All fabrication uses a DED machine with NiTi powder and argon shielding.
  - Width can be measured by the optical microscope from the top, as supported by the literature.
  - Height can be measured with digital calipers if we build multi-layer walls to increase step height above caliper resolution.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: Use a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition; use top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Pairing, at each scan speed, both single-pass beads (for width) and a short single-track multi-layer wall (for amplified height) enables resolving both metrics using only the available optical microscope and calipers, minimizing metrology limitations without cross-sectioning.
  - Spacing single tracks at least 3× expected bead width and measuring away from start/stop regions reduces thermal overlap and transient effects using only top-view imaging.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging with scale calibration)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Assumed: Flat NiTi substrate plate compatible with the DED system (see Missing Capability / Assumption)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., select 5–7 levels spanning the validated stable window cited in the lab’s prior NiTi runs)
  - Held constant: laser power, spot size/defocus, powder feed rate, carrier/shield gas flow, stand-off, path length (~30–50 mm per bead), inter-track spacing (≥3× anticipated bead width), layer increment command for the wall, and identical start/stop strategy.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (μm or mm): For each single-pass track, capture top-view images at 3–5 positions along the central 60% of length; compute mean and standard deviation per speed.
  - Build height (mm): Measure total wall height at 3 positions along the wall with calipers; divide by number of layers to estimate average height gain per layer at each speed.
  - Derived: Plots of track width vs scan speed and height gain per layer vs scan speed; optional deposition efficiency proxy = (average height gain per layer) × (track width) to indicate cross-sectional buildup trend versus speed.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating outside the stable scan-speed window may cause balling or lack of fusion, invalidating width/height readings; even within the window, surface roughness may introduce caliper error on short walls.
  - Mitigations: Pre-screen a narrow speed range with single beads to confirm continuity; use at least 10 layers for the wall to boost measurable height; gently deburr loose spatter before caliper contact without altering geometry; measure away from start/stop regions.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible flat NiTi substrate plate and standard DED fixturing; these are not listed in the BOM but are typically required for DED deposition. If unavailable, the experiment cannot proceed as described.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01
DOI:

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric scan speed levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-bead tracks and multi-layer walls across a scan-speed sweep in DED NiTi to quantify how scan speed affects single-track width (optical microscopy) and cumulative build height per layer (digital calipers).

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All print and measurement steps use only the DED machine, optical microscope, digital calipers, NiTi powder, and argon.
  - Track width can be captured from top-view micrographs; build height can be measured on multi-layer walls with calipers to amplify the signal.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: use a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition; use top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Height amplification by printing short 10-layer single-bead walls at each scan speed to enable reliable caliper measurements of per-layer build height (height per layer = total wall height / 10).
  - Randomized print order of speeds to reduce systematic thermal-history effects on a shared substrate.
  - Use both single-layer single tracks (for width only) and 10-layer walls (for width and height) to cross-check width consistency across layer counts.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed at 5 levels spanning the machine’s validated stable window for NiTi (e.g., S1 < S2 < S3 < S4 < S5; exact values chosen within the window referenced in paper_003_chunk_01 and/or via a quick pre-check). Keep all below constant:
    - Laser power: fixed
    - Powder feed rate: fixed
    - Spot size/focal offset: fixed
    - Stand-off distance: fixed
    - Shielding argon flow: fixed
    - Track length: 20 mm
    - Layer count: 1 for single tracks; 10 for walls
    - Inter-layer dwell: fixed (e.g., 30–60 s) to limit heat accumulation
  - Replicates: 3 per speed for both single tracks and 10-layer walls
  - Layout: Randomize the print order of speeds; space features to avoid overlap of heat-affected zones.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (single tracks and top layer of walls): capture top-view optical micrographs; measure width at mid-length (exclude 5 mm from each end), take 3 positions per feature, average; report mean ± SD per speed.
  - Build height (10-layer walls): measure total wall height above adjacent substrate with calipers at 3 positions per wall; compute per-layer height = total height / 10; report mean ± SD per speed.
  - Stability notes: visually record lack of keyholing/balling and continuity.
  - Outputs: width vs scan speed; per-layer build height vs scan speed; aspect ratio (height/width) vs scan speed.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Caliper resolution may be marginal for single-layer height; mitigated by using 10 layers to amplify height.
  - Out-of-window scan speeds may cause balling or lack of fusion, corrupting measurements; mitigated by staying within the validated window and performing a short pre-check at each speed.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a suitable substrate/fixture compatible with the DED machine for NiTi deposition (e.g., NiTi or steel base plate). This is not listed in the BOM; if unavailable, the experiment cannot proceed as described.
  - Assumes the optical microscope can capture calibrated images (built-in scale or known pixel size). If not, a stage micrometer would be needed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Measure how DED scan speed affects (a) single-bead track width and (b) per-layer build height in NiTi by printing, at each scan speed, both a single-layer line (for width via top-view optical microscopy) and a 10-layer thin-wall “tower” (for height via digital calipers), keeping all other process parameters constant.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps use only NiTi powder, argon shielding, a DED machine, an optical microscope for top-view width measurements, and digital calipers for tower height. No cross-sectioning or profilometry is required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  From “paper_003_chunk_01”:  
  - The existence of a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition in DED NiTi.  
  - Use of top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Use paired artifacts per speed: (1) a single-pass, single-layer line for width and (2) a 10-layer thin-wall at identical speed for height, then compute per-layer height as total height divided by 10. This amplifies the height signal for reliable caliper measurement while keeping width assessment isolated to a single pass.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine (with control of scan speed, laser power, powder feed, shielding, stand-off)  
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging with scale calibration)  
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder  
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., select 5–7 levels spanning the known stable window from literature/machine baseline; example spacing: v1 < v2 < v3 < v4 < v5).  
  - Hold constant: laser power, spot size, powder feed rate, layer height command, stand-off distance, shielding gas flow, substrate preheat (if any), path length, and inter-layer dwell.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (single-layer line):  
    - Top-view optical micrographs at 3–5 evenly spaced locations along each line; extract mean and standard deviation of width per speed.  
    - Qualitative defect notes (balling, discontinuities) per speed.
  - Build height (10-layer thin-wall):  
    - Total wall height measured at 3 positions with calipers; compute mean per-layer height = (mean total height)/10.  
    - Qualitative straightness/warpage notes.  
  - Derived relations: width vs scan speed, per-layer height vs scan speed; identify operational “stable” subset where continuity is acceptable.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At speeds outside the stable window, lines may ball or be discontinuous, and thin-walls may collapse or underbuild, yielding unusable measurements. Mitigation: start with mid-window speed identified from prior NiTi DED baselines, then expand outward; abort conditions if visible instability occurs.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a flat, compatible substrate plate and standard DED fixturing in the DED machine (not listed in BOM).  
  - Assumes the optical microscope can be calibrated for pixel-to-micrometer conversion.  
  - Assumes the DED system allows independent control of scan speed while keeping power and feed constant.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Deposit a series of straight, single-bead walls (multi-layer bead-on-bead tracks) on the same substrate using NiTi powder and argon shielding, varying only the scan speed across a validated stable window. For each speed, build a short wall segment of fixed length (e.g., 20–30 mm) and fixed layer count (e.g., 10 layers), keeping laser power, powder feed rate, standoff, and argon flow constant. Measure top-view bead/track width by optical microscopy after the first layer and after the final layer for each wall; measure build height of each wall with digital calipers using the depth probe from the substrate surface to the wall top.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - Uses only the DED machine, NiTi powder, argon shielding, an optical microscope for width, and digital calipers for height.
  - The literature supports a validated scan-speed window and the use of top-view microscopy and calipers to compare track width and build height.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: 
    - There is a validated scan-speed window for stable DED NiTi deposition.
    - Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Standardized “short-wall” protocol: for each scan speed, build an identical 10-layer straight wall to enable direct, caliper-accessible build height comparisons while also capturing first-layer and final-layer track widths via microscopy. This pairs single-bead track width data with multi-layer build height data under strictly fixed non-speed parameters and a controlled interlayer cooldown (e.g., 60–90 s) to minimize thermal-history confounds.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine (existing)
  - Optical microscope (existing)
  - Digital calipers with depth probe (existing)

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder (existing)
  - Argon gas (existing)
  - Substrate plate compatible with the DED system (e.g., NiTi or steel) and standard fixturing clamps (see Missing Capability / Assumption)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed — 5 levels spanning the validated stable window (e.g., at ~10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 90% of the allowed range for this machine/material).
  - Held constant: Laser power, powder feed rate, layer height/standoff, shielding gas flow rate, track length, and interlayer dwell (e.g., 60–90 s).
  - Geometry: For each speed, deposit one 20–30 mm long wall with 10 layers at identical path and start/stop positions.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (µm or mm) from top-view optical micrographs:
    - After layer 1 (single-bead width per speed)
    - After layer 10 (top-bead width per speed)
  - Build height (mm) per wall using caliper depth probe from substrate surface to wall crest (average of 3–5 positions along the wall)
  - Qualitative observations of defects (balling, lack of fusion, spatter) via optical images
  - Derived relationships: width vs scan speed; height per 10 layers vs scan speed; optional deposition “height-per-layer” vs scan speed

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating at or beyond the stable scan-speed limits could cause lack of fusion or balling, compromising width/height measurements. Rough top surfaces may introduce caliper scatter; mitigate by averaging multiple locations and using consistent probe placement and gentle contact force.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a suitable flat substrate plate (e.g., NiTi or steel) and standard DED fixturing. If not available, the experiment cannot proceed because DED requires a substrate.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Print single-bead tracks and short multi-layer walls of NiTi using DED at fixed laser power and powder feed rate while sweeping scan speed across a validated stable window; quantify track width (top-view optical microscopy) and build height per layer (digital calipers on multi-layer walls). Analyze how scan speed influences track width and build height.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - Uses only NiTi powder and argon shielding on a DED machine.
  - Track width can be measured from top-view optical micrographs.
  - Build height can be measured with digital calipers on multi-layer features.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: There is a validated scan-speed window for stable DED NiTi deposition, and top-view optical microscopy plus caliper checks are suitable to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Combine single-layer track width mapping with a companion multi-layer height-per-layer metric at the same scan speeds to decouple lateral spreading from vertical accumulation, using only optical microscopy and calipers.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine (with argon shielding, fixed laser power and powder feed control)
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging with scale/bar)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Assumed: flat metallic substrate plate compatible with DED NiTi (see Missing Capability / Assumption)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed within the validated stable window (e.g., 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 mm/min as placeholders; replace with machine-validated values per “paper_003_chunk_01” guidance).
  - Hold constant: laser power, spot size, hatch (N/A for single tracks), powder feed rate, stand-off distance, shielding gas flow, substrate preheat (if any).
  - Replicates: 3 per speed for single-bead tracks; 2 per speed for 10-layer walls.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (single-layer): For each bead, capture top-view optical micrographs; measure average width over a central 20–30 mm gauge length, excluding start/stop transients.
  - Build height (multi-layer): Print 10-layer walls at each speed (same power/feed). After cooling, measure wall height with calipers at 3 positions; compute height-per-layer and variability.
  - Deposition stability notes: Qualitative bead continuity, balling, spatter, or underfill observations.
  - Output plots: track width vs scan speed; height-per-layer vs scan speed; stability map indicating acceptable vs unstable regimes.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At low scan speed: excessive heat input causing bead swelling and lack of dimensional control; potential torch/nozzle collision with tall beads.
  - At high scan speed: lack of fusion or discontinuous beads (balling), making width/height measurements unreliable.
  - Mitigations: Start with a quick single-bead scout across speeds to confirm continuity before committing to multi-layer builds; use consistent inter-layer dwell; maintain constant powder feed and shielding; avoid measuring near acceleration zones.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumed availability of a suitable, flat substrate plate (e.g., NiTi or steel) compatible with the DED machine’s fixture and with NiTi deposition. If unavailable, this is a blocking item.
  - No profilometer or cross-sectioning capability; relying on top-view optical microscopy and calipers as per precedent.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01
DOI:

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric scan speed levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a set of single-track beads and corresponding multi-layer single-track walls in NiTi by DED while sweeping scan speed within the validated stable window. For each scan speed, deposit:
  1) a straight single-pass bead (e.g., 40–50 mm long) to measure top-view track width by optical microscopy;
  2) a vertical wall made by stacking N identical passes along the same path (e.g., N = 10 layers at fixed hatch = 0) to amplify and measure build height by digital calipers. Keep laser power, powder feed rate, shielding gas flow, stand-off, and spot size constant across the sweep.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - Only requires: DED machine (for deposition), argon gas (shielding), NiTi powder (feedstock), optical microscope (top-view width), and digital calipers (wall height). No cross-sectioning or advanced metrology needed.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: There is a validated scan-speed window for stable DED of NiTi, and top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings. The experiment follows this precedent for both the parameter sweep and the two measurement methods.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Rather than attempting caliper-based single-bead step-height (which can be near caliper resolution), the protocol builds short multi-layer single-track walls at each scan speed to amplify height to a caliper-measurable scale. This pairs single-pass width measurement with multi-layer height efficiency at the same speed, enabling a simple map of “scan speed → track width and wall build rate” with the minimal metrology available.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging with scale)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (use the validated stable window referenced in paper_003_chunk_01; e.g., select 5–7 evenly spaced speeds within that window).
  - Hold constant across all runs: laser power, spot size, powder feed rate, carrier/shielding argon flow, stand-off distance, travel path length, inter-layer dwell/cooling time, and layer count for walls (e.g., N = 10).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (μm or mm): For each single-pass bead, capture top-view microscope images at 5 evenly spaced locations along the central 30 mm of the bead; measure width with the microscope scale; report mean ± SD vs. scan speed.
  - Wall build height (mm): For each multi-layer wall at the corresponding speed, measure total height with calipers at 3 positions; report mean ± SD vs. scan speed. Optionally compute apparent height-per-layer.
  - Qualitative stability notes from top-view OM: presence of balling, discontinuities, excessive spatter, or lack-of-fusion indicators for each speed.
  - Acceptance criterion: Identify the subrange of scan speeds yielding continuous tracks with monotonic, well-behaved width and height trends.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating outside the stable scan-speed window may cause balling or under-/over-deposition, invalidating measurements. Mitigation: begin with a small pilot at mid-window speed; expand outward only if tracks are continuous and visually acceptable.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible substrate plate and fixturing in the DED machine (e.g., NiTi or a commonly used metallic build plate) to receive the beads/walls; the substrate is not listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the optical microscope can provide calibrated top-view measurements (reticle or known pixel size); if not, a simple calibration slide would be needed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-track speed-ladder in DED NiTi to quantify how scan speed affects track width and build height. Program one continuous bead where scan speed steps through 5 levels within the validated stable window (e.g., S1→S5), holding all other parameters constant. Each speed segment is 12–15 mm long, separated by 3 mm dwell pauses with the laser off to mark boundaries. Repeat the ladder 3 times on fresh locations to capture variability. Measure top-view track width for each segment via optical microscopy; measure bead height relative to adjacent substrate with digital calipers.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - Uses only NiTi powder, argon shielding, a DED machine, an optical microscope for top-view width, and digital calipers for step-height checks.
  - Paper precedent supports top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks for width/height comparison in DED NiTi.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: 
    - There exists a validated scan-speed window for stable DED NiTi deposition.
    - Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Use a “speed-ladder” within a single continuous track so all speeds are deposited under nearly identical transient thermal and environmental conditions, minimizing run-to-run variability.
  - Insert brief laser-off dwells between speed steps to create visible demarcations for microscopy without introducing extra materials or fixturing.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed S1–S5 spanning the lower, mid, and upper portions of the machine’s validated stable-speed window (as established for this NiTi powder and optics).
  - Held constant: Laser power, powder feed rate, spot size/defocus, stand-off, shielding gas flow, substrate preheat (if used), and path length per segment (12–15 mm).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width per speed segment from top-view optical microscopy (≥5 width measurements per segment; report mean ± SD).
  - Build height per segment measured by digital calipers as step height from adjacent substrate to bead crest (≥3 measurements per segment).
  - Derived relationships: width vs scan speed; height vs scan speed; width/height ratio vs scan speed.
  - Process stability notes: visual continuity, spatter/balling incidence per segment.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operation near the edges of the stable-speed window may cause balling or lack of fusion, compromising width/height measurement. Mitigation: begin with mid-window speeds; expand outward only after confirming stability. Ensure adequate argon shielding to avoid oxidation that can bias bead geometry.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a flat, compatible substrate/build plate (preferably NiTi or a metallurgically compatible alloy) for track deposition; this substrate is not listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the DED machine can execute programmed, segment-wise speed changes and brief laser-off dwells.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01
