task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Bead-on-plate and short wall builds to quantify how DED scan speed affects (a) single-track width and (b) multi-layer build height for NiTi. For each selected scan speed within a validated stable window, deposit:
  1) Three replicate single-pass tracks (constant laser power, powder feed, and standoff).
  2) One short straight wall (e.g., 10 layers stacked along the same path).
  Measure track width by top-view optical microscopy and resulting build/step height by digital calipers.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All depositions can be done on the DED machine using NiTi powder under argon shielding.
  - Width measurements use the optical microscope; height/step measurements use digital calipers.
  - No additional characterization tools are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: There is a validated scan-speed window for stable DED NiTi deposition; top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Two-scale assessment at each speed: single-pass beads to isolate melt-pool geometry (track width, bead height) and a 10-layer wall to capture cumulative build behavior (effective layer gain per pass) under the same speed.
  - Simple replication (n=3 beads per speed) for basic statistics and outlier rejection without extra metrology.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (select 5–7 values spanning the machine’s known-stable window for NiTi as indicated in literature and confirmed by a short pretest).
  - Hold constant across all runs: laser power, powder mass flow rate, nozzle standoff, shielding gas flow, layer dwell time, and path length.
  - Geometry:
    - Beads: single straight tracks, identical length, with 3 replicates per speed.
    - Walls: same path as beads, 10 stacked layers at the same speed.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (W): top-view optical microscope; measure at 5 evenly spaced points per track; average and standard deviation per track and per speed.
  - Single-bead step height (H1): digital calipers; zero on substrate adjacent to bead, measure peak height at 3 points per bead; average and standard deviation.
  - Wall height after 10 layers (H10): digital calipers; measure at 5 points; compute effective height gain per layer (H10/10).
  - Qualitative observations: track continuity, balling, excessive spatter, or lack of fusion at each speed.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating outside the stable scan-speed window may cause balling or lack of fusion, compromising width/height measurements. Mitigation: perform a brief pretest to confirm the lower and upper practical speed bounds for the fixed power/feed settings.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of compatible flat substrate coupons (e.g., NiTi or a standard DED-compatible alloy plate) and basic surface preparation; these are not listed in the BOM and would be required to execute the builds.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_003_chunk_01
DOI:

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric scan speed levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-bead-on-plate DED of NiTi with a scan-speed sweep to quantify how scan speed affects (a) top-view track width and (b) bead build height. Deposit 10–15 mm long single tracks at a fixed laser power, fixed powder feed, and fixed stand-off, varying only scan speed across a bracketed window. For each speed, print 3 replicate beads spaced ≥10 mm apart to avoid thermal overlap. After cooling, measure track width from calibrated top-view optical micrographs and measure bead height relative to the substrate using digital calipers at 3 points along each bead.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps use only the DED machine (to print), argon (to shield), an optical microscope (to capture top-view images for width), and digital calipers (to estimate build height), which are explicitly available. The workflow mirrors practices described in the literature card for NiTi DED.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: 
    - Use of a validated scan-speed window for stable NiTi DED deposition.
    - Use of top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Adaptive bracketing: start with a coarse scan-speed sweep to identify the stable deposition window on the specific machine/lot of powder, then perform a finer sweep (smaller step size) near the transitions where balling or underfill first appear. This concentrates measurement effort where width/height are most sensitive to scan speed.
  - Replicated single-bead arrays on a single coupon with controlled spacing to minimize cross-heating, enabling direct side-by-side microscopy comparison under identical substrate conditions.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (with calibrated scale bar or known pixel size)
  - Digital calipers
  - Inert gas plumbing for argon shielding (part of DED setup)

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., 8–10 levels spanning below, within, and above the expected stable window; example increments such as 200–800 mm/min with 75–100 mm/min steps if machine allows).
  - Held constant: Laser power (single value), powder feed rate, stand-off distance, spot size, shielding gas flow, and substrate preheat (if any).
  - Replicates: 3 beads per speed, 10–15 mm length each, ≥10 mm spacing.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (μm or mm) from top-view optical micrographs:
    - For each bead, measure width at 3 equally spaced positions (avoid start/stop transients by excluding first/last 2 mm).
    - Report mean ± standard deviation per speed.
  - Bead build height (mm) via digital calipers:
    - Zero calipers on the substrate adjacent to the bead; measure height at the bead apex at 3 positions per bead; report mean ± standard deviation per speed.
  - Qualitative stability notes per speed: evidence of balling, lack of fusion, excessive spatter, or surface ripples.
  - Output plots/tables: track width vs scan speed; build height vs scan speed; annotate unstable regimes.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Out-of-window scan speeds may cause balling or lack of fusion, yielding unreliable geometry. Mitigation: start with conservative speeds suggested by prior NiTi runs, execute short pilot beads, and use the adaptive bracketing to avoid extensive deposition in unstable zones.
  - Build-height measurement with calipers can be sensitive to bead roughness and jaw positioning. Mitigation: take multiple measurements per bead, use light, consistent jaw force, and average; avoid measuring within the start/stop regions.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a flat NiTi substrate/coupon compatible with the DED system and clamping/fixturing; this substrate is not explicitly listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the optical microscope can be calibrated (scale bar or known magnification) for quantitative width measurement.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  DED-deposit single tracks and short multi-layer walls of NiTi at multiple scan speeds within a validated stable window; quantify (i) single-track width by top-view optical microscopy and (ii) wall build height by digital calipers to establish width–speed and height-per-layer–speed relationships.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  Uses only the DED machine for deposition, NiTi powder with argon shielding, an optical microscope for top-view width measurements, and digital calipers for height measurements. No additional metrology or materials are required beyond a substrate assumption noted below.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:

  - A validated scan-speed window for stable NiTi DED deposition is used to choose speeds.
  - Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks are appropriate to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Pair a single-track width measurement (layer 1) with a multi-layer wall built on the same path at each scan speed to compute height per layer as a practical build-rate proxy versus speed.
  - Use standardized lead-in/lead-out segments and exclude the first/last 5 mm of each track to minimize transient effects without needing advanced sensors.
  - Normalize wall height by number of layers to reduce sensitivity to small caliper resolution limits.
  - Include triplicate repeats per speed for simple statistics using available tools.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., 5 levels spanning the validated stable window from paper_003_chunk_01; exact values selected after a brief pilot to confirm stability with the specific machine and powder lot).
  - Held constant across all conditions: laser power, powder feed rate, spot size (defocus), standoff distance, shielding gas flow, substrate preheat setting (if any), path length (e.g., 50 mm), layer count for walls (e.g., 10 layers), and inter-layer dwell (e.g., 60 s) to keep average part temperature consistent.
  - Replicates: n = 3 per scan speed.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (single-track, layer 1):
    - Capture top-view optical images with a known scale.
    - Measure width at 5 evenly spaced positions along the central 40 mm; report mean ± SD per track, then per speed.
  - Build height (multi-layer wall at same path):
    - After depositing N layers on the same line, measure wall height relative to adjacent substrate with digital calipers at 3 positions along the central region; compute mean ± SD.
    - Calculate height-per-layer = (mean wall height)/N.
  - Outputs:
    - Plot mean track width vs. scan speed.
    - Plot height-per-layer vs. scan speed.
    - Tabulate qualitative stability notes (e.g., balling, lack of fusion, excessive wetting) observed visually.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Outside the stable speed window, defects like balling (too fast) or excessive swelling/keyholing (too slow/high energy) may distort width/height trends or halt builds. Mitigation: restrict speeds to the validated window and confirm with a short pilot pass; discard end regions.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible NiTi substrate plate/fixture for DED deposition (not listed in materials).
  - Assumes basic microscope scale calibration (stage micrometer or verified pixel-to-length calibration). If unavailable, width measurements may carry higher uncertainty.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Single-bead-on-plate DED of NiTi across a scan-speed sweep to quantify how scan speed affects (a) top-view track width and (b) single-bead build height, using optical microscopy for width and digital calipers for bead height.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps can be executed with the DED machine (to deposit single beads of NiTi powder under argon shielding) and measured using only an optical microscope (top-view width) and digital calipers (bead height). No additional metrology or materials are strictly required beyond a suitable substrate.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From “paper_003_chunk_01”:
- The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition.
- Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
- Apply a randomized spatial layout of single beads with controlled inter-track spacing and inter-pass dwell to reduce cumulative heat history bias across speeds, enabling a cleaner speed-to-geometry relationship with minimal instrumentation.
- Use replicated beads at each speed (n=3) to capture variability and compute basic statistics (mean, standard deviation) of track width and height, creating a practical “speed vs. geometry” map suitable for on-the-fly parameter tuning during future builds.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine
- Optical microscope (top-view imaging with scale calibration)
- Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder
- Argon gas
- Assumed substrate plate compatible with the DED system (see “Missing Capability / Assumption”)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
Primary sweep:
- Scan speed: 6 levels spanning the known stable window for NiTi DED (e.g., evenly spaced between the lower and upper stability limits indicated by prior NiTi runs in your lab).
Fixed/controlled parameters:
- Laser power: fixed at a value known to yield stable single-bead deposition in NiTi with the chosen powder feed (use your lab’s standard setting).
- Powder feed rate: fixed.
- Stand-off distance / focus: fixed per machine setup.
- Shield gas: argon flow rate fixed per standard practice.
- Track length: 20–30 mm per bead.
- Inter-track spacing: ≥10 mm to limit thermal overlap.
- Inter-pass dwell: ≥2 minutes or until substrate returns near baseline temperature (touch-free IR read if available; otherwise timed dwell).
Replicates:
- n = 3 independent beads per speed, randomized order on the plate.

- Measurements / Outputs:
For each bead:
- Track width (mm): top-view optical micrograph with calibrated scale; measure average width over the central 10 mm, avoiding start/stop zones. Report mean ± SD across three locations per bead and then across replicates.
- Build height (mm): digital calipers measure step height from adjacent substrate surface to bead crest at the mid-length position; take three readings per bead and report mean ± SD across replicates.
Derived:
- Aspect ratio (height/width) vs. scan speed.
Deliverables:
- Plots of width vs. speed, height vs. speed, and aspect ratio vs. speed, with error bars; identification of the “stable geometry” band where variability is minimal.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
- Operating outside the stable scan-speed window can cause balling or lack-of-fusion, leading to discontinuous beads that are hard to measure. Mitigation: begin with a brief scouting pass at mid-window speed to confirm stability; proceed outward in smaller increments; terminate levels showing obvious instability.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Assumes availability of a compatible, flat substrate plate (preferably NiTi or another DED-compatible alloy plate) mounted in the DED machine. If not available in the current BOM, this substrate is required to proceed.

- Source Papers Used:
- paper_003_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Print a “speed matrix” on a single substrate using NiTi powder via DED: for each of five scan speeds spanning the validated window, deposit (a) three single-pass, single-track beads for top-view width measurements, and (b) one 10-layer co-linear stack at the same speed to amplify build height for caliper measurement. Hold all other parameters constant (laser power, powder feed rate, stand-off, argon flow). Randomize the order of speeds across the plate to minimize location/thermal-history bias.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All processing uses the DED machine with NiTi powder under argon shielding.
  - Track width can be captured by top-view optical microscopy.
  - Build height can be measured with digital calipers if height is amplified via a 10-layer stack.
  - No additional metrology or materials beyond the BOM are strictly required, assuming a compatible substrate is available.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: 
    - There exists a validated scan-speed window for stable NiTi DED deposition.
    - Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks are suitable to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Use a paired-geometry per speed: single-pass tracks for width plus a localized 10-layer stack for height, enabling both outputs with only a microscope and calipers.
  - Randomize the print order of speeds across the plate to decouple speed effects from local thermal accumulation.
  - Replicate per speed (n=3 single tracks) to capture variability without extra metrology.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: scan speed (e.g., 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 mm/min within the validated window).
  - Held constant: laser power (fixed value appropriate for the powder and spot size), powder feed rate, stand-off distance, spot size, shielding gas flow, layer count (10 for height stacks; 1 for width tracks), inter-layer dwell (e.g., 20–30 s to limit heat accumulation).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (µm): top-view optical micrographs of the central 10 mm of each single-pass track; measure width at three evenly spaced points per track; report mean ± SD per speed.
  - Build height (mm): digital caliper measurement of the step height between the 10-layer stack and adjacent bare substrate at three positions; mean ± SD per speed.
  - Qualitative stability notes: presence of balling, lack of fusion, or excessive spatter observed optically.
  - Optional derived metrics: width-to-height ratio versus scan speed.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Very high scan speeds may cause lack of fusion and discontinuous beads; very low speeds may cause excessive reinforcement and spatter, confounding width/height readings. Calipers may struggle with sub-0.3 mm heights if the stack does not build adequately.
  - Mitigations: constrain speeds to the validated window; use 10 layers for height amplification; include inter-layer dwell to avoid overheating; discard visibly discontinuous segments from width measurement and reprint if necessary.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible substrate/build plate (e.g., NiTi or stainless steel) mounted in the DED machine; this substrate is not explicitly listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the DED machine can set and hold constant laser power, feed rate, and argon flow, though exact values are not provided in the BOM.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Measure how scan speed affects (a) single-track bead width and (b) multi-layer wall build height in DED of NiTi by printing:
  - Part A: Single straight tracks (30 mm) at 5 scan speeds within a stable window, constant laser power and powder feed.
  - Part B: For each speed, a 10-layer thin wall along the same path (alternating travel direction; 60 s interlayer pause).
  Quantify top-view track width by optical microscopy (Part A) and final wall height by calipers (Part B).

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - Uses only DED machine, argon shielding, NiTi powder, optical microscope, and digital calipers.
  - Literature supports using top-view optical microscopy and calipers to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - A validated scan-speed window exists for stable DED NiTi deposition.
  - Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.
  (From: paper_003_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Link single-pass bead width (Part A) to per-layer build height in a thin wall (Part B) at identical scan speeds to examine whether single-track width is a predictor of vertical build rate under repeated passes.
  - Control thermal history by alternating scan direction and adding fixed interlayer cooldowns to reduce heat accumulation bias while isolating scan-speed effects.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 mm/min), chosen inside the validated stability window.
  - Held constant: laser power, spot size, powder feed rate, shielding gas flow, stand-off, layer height command/path, and track length.
  - Deposition strategy: single track per speed (Part A); 10 layers stacked on same path per speed (Part B) with 60 s cooldown and alternating direction.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Part A (single tracks):
    - Track width: optical microscope top-view images; measure width at 5 evenly spaced positions per track (exclude 5 mm lead-in/out). Report mean ± SD vs. speed.
    - Qualitative bead continuity/balling notes from images.
  - Part B (10-layer walls):
    - Final wall height: calipers at 5 positions along the wall; compute mean ± SD and height-per-layer (height/10) vs. speed.
    - Visual defect notes (undercut, waviness).
  - Derived:
    - Correlation between single-track width and height-per-layer across speeds.
    - Identification of practical speed window yielding continuous beads and predictable height gain.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At low speed/high input: excessive melt pool leading to sagging/overbuild; at high speed: lack of fusion/balling causing discontinuous tracks and reduced height. Mitigations: stay within literature-supported speed window; verify shielding; use interlayer cooldowns.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible flat substrate plate (e.g., NiTi or steel) and basic fixturing/cleaning within the DED system; not listed in BOM.

  - Part A: Single straight tracks (30 mm) at 5 scan speeds within a stable window, constant laser power and powder feed.
  - Part B: For each speed, a 10-layer thin wall along the same path (alternating travel direction; 60 s interlayer pause).
  Quantify top-view track width by optical microscopy (Part A) and final wall height by calipers (Part B).

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Link single-pass bead width (Part A) to per-layer build height in a thin wall (Part B) at identical scan speeds to examine whether single-track width is a predictor of vertical build rate under repeated passes.
  - Control thermal history by alternating scan direction and adding fixed interlayer cooldowns to reduce heat accumulation bias while isolating scan-speed effects.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-bead and multi-layer wall depositions of NiTi using DED while sweeping scan speed within the validated stable window; quantify (a) single-track width via top-view optical microscopy and (b) build height per layer from multi-layer walls via digital calipers.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - Uses only NiTi powder and argon shielding on a DED machine.
  - Track width can be measured with the available optical microscope (top-view).
  - Build height can be measured with digital calipers on multi-layer walls to achieve sufficient step height.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: There is a validated scan-speed window for stable DED of NiTi, and top-view optical microscopy plus caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Combine single-track beads and short multi-layer walls at the same scan-speed settings to decouple lateral melt-pool behavior (track width) from vertical accumulation (build height per layer), using only optical microscopy and calipers.
  - Introduce a simple continuity rating (continuous, minor balling, severe balling) per track to annotate stability versus scan speed without extra metrology.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging of beads)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (shielding/purge)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (choose 5 evenly spaced values within the machine’s and/or prior NiTi validated stable window; e.g., V1–V5).
  - Hold constant across all runs:
    - Laser power (fixed at a value previously yielding sound NiTi tracks)
    - Powder feed rate
    - Layer height setpoint (for multi-layer walls)
    - Shielding gas flow and nozzle standoff
    - Track length (e.g., 20–30 mm)
    - Hatch spacing: not applicable for single beads; for walls, use single-bead stacking at same path.
  - Replicates: n = 3 per scan-speed condition for both single-bead and wall builds.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Single-bead track width:
    - For each track, capture top-view optical micrographs at 3 locations (start/mid/end).
    - Measure bead width at each location; report mean ± SD per track and per speed.
  - Build height per layer (wall):
    - For each speed, build a 10-layer wall (same path stacked).
    - Measure total wall height with calipers at 3 positions; compute average height per layer = (avg total height)/10.
  - Continuity/stability rating from optical images: continuous, minor balling, severe balling.
  - Outputs/plots:
    - Track width vs. scan speed.
    - Height per layer vs. scan speed.
    - Overlay continuity rating on both plots to indicate stability zones.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating outside the stable scan-speed window can cause lack-of-fusion or balling, yielding discontinuous tracks that confound width/height trends. Mitigation: begin with a short preliminary ladder at wider spacing to identify and exclude clearly unstable speeds, then fill in 5 evenly spaced speeds within the stable range.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible build plate/substrate (e.g., NiTi or stainless steel) and basic DED fixturing/alignment, which are not explicitly listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the DED machine allows independent control of scan speed, laser power, and powder feed rate and that a previously validated power/feed setting for NiTi is known.
  - No surface profilometer or cross-sectioning is available; height resolution is achieved by using multi-layer walls and calipers.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

card_context:

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how scan speed affects track width and build height in a DED NiTi process.

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Deposit an array of parallel single-bead tracks of NiTi using DED at five scan speeds spanning the validated stable window. Hold laser power, powder feed rate, spot size, stand-off, and argon shielding constant. Produce three replicate tracks per speed on the same substrate with ≥5 mm spacing to avoid thermal overlap. Measure track width from top-view optical micrographs at mid-length, and measure build height as the step height from substrate to bead crest using digital calipers at three equally spaced positions along each track; report mean and standard deviation versus scan speed.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  It uses only the DED machine, NiTi powder, argon shielding, an optical microscope for top-view width measurements, and digital calipers for build height step-height checks, which are explicitly supported.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Using a validated scan-speed window for stable NiTi DED deposition.
  - Comparing track width and build height across scan-speed settings using top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks.
  (From: paper_003_chunk_01)

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Single-build “track matrix” design: interleave three replicates per speed on one plate to reduce thermal history bias (print order randomized across speeds) while maintaining constant parameters.
  - Height robustness check: measure build height at three positions per track (¼, ½, ¾ length) to capture any tapering, and flag instability if within-track height slope exceeds a preset threshold.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Substrate/build plate compatible with the DED system (see Missing Capability / Assumption)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., select 5 levels spanning low → high within the validated stable window reported for NiTi on your system).
  - Held constant: Laser power, powder feed rate, beam spot size, stand-off, shielding gas flow rate, track length (e.g., 20 mm), inter-track spacing (≥5 mm), and substrate temperature (ambient).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (µm): From calibrated top-view optical micrographs at mid-length; three fields per track, averaged.
  - Build height (mm): Step height from substrate to bead crest using calipers at three positions per track, averaged.
  - Stability notes (qualitative): Presence of balling, lack-of-fusion, discontinuities from top-view inspection.
  - Output plots: Width vs. scan speed; Height vs. scan speed; coefficients of variation across replicates.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating outside the stable scan-speed window can cause balling or lack-of-fusion, corrupting width/height trends. Mitigation: pre-test two bracketing speeds to confirm bead continuity before the full matrix, and maintain constant powder/laser parameters.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a flat, compatible substrate/build plate (e.g., NiTi or stainless steel) and standard DED fixturing; this substrate is not listed in the provided materials but is typically required.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-track DED deposits of NiTi on a metallic substrate with scan speed as the only swept parameter; measure track width by top-view optical microscopy and build height by digital calipers to map width/height versus scan speed and identify a stable deposition window.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All required deposition and measurement steps use the available DED machine, optical microscope, and digital calipers.
  - Materials align with NiTi powder and argon shielding.
  - The literature precedent supports using top-view microscopy and calipers specifically for comparing track width and build height across scan speeds.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition in DED NiTi.
  - Use of top-view optical microscopy to measure track width and caliper checks to compare build height across scan-speed settings.
  - Source: paper_003_chunk_01.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Interleaved randomized track layout: on a single substrate, deposit short, separated single tracks (e.g., 20–30 mm long) in a randomized order of scan speeds to reduce thermal history and gradient bias across the plate.
  - Triplicate tracks per speed for basic statistics; report mean and standard deviation for both width and height to robustly map the process window.
  - Define a simple stability score (continuous bead without balling, undercut, or excessive spatter) from top-view images to complement quantitative width/height data.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (shielding)
  - Assumed: metallic substrate compatible with the DED system (preferably NiTi plate or 304/316 stainless steel)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: scan speed v (example plan: 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 mm/s; adjust to machine-safe window).
  - Hold constant:
    - Laser power (e.g., fixed at a setting known to produce continuous beads with NiTi on your machine)
    - Powder feed rate (constant mass flow)
    - Stand-off distance
    - Laser spot size/focus
    - Argon shielding flow and shroud arrangement
    - Track length (e.g., 25 mm) and inter-track spacing (e.g., ≥8 mm to limit overlap heating)
  - Replicates: n = 3 tracks per speed, randomized order across the plate.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width: top-view optical microscope images with scale; measure width at 5 evenly spaced positions per track; compute mean ± SD per track and per speed.
  - Build height: use digital calipers to measure step height at bead ends and midspan (e.g., bridge caliper jaws across substrate and bead); take 3 readings per track; compute mean ± SD.
  - Qualitative stability: classify each track as continuous/balling/undercut based on optical images.
  - Outputs: plots of track width vs scan speed and build height vs scan speed; tabulated stability score vs speed; identification of the practical scan-speed window for stable, dimensionally consistent beads under fixed power/feed.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At high speeds: lack of fusion and discontinuous beads leading to under-measured width/height.
  - At low speeds: excessive heat input causing swelling, spatter, or oxidation if shielding is insufficient.
  - Mitigation: begin with a pilot pass at mid-range speed to confirm melt pool behavior; verify argon shielding effectiveness; keep inter-track spacing and randomized order to limit local preheating.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible metallic substrate (e.g., NiTi or stainless steel) and routine surface preparation/cleaning; these are not explicitly listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the DED machine can achieve and stably control the proposed scan-speed range and maintain constant powder feed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All required deposition and measurement steps use the available DED machine, optical microscope, and digital calipers.
  - Materials align with NiTi powder and argon shielding.
  - The literature precedent supports using top-view microscopy and calipers specifically for comparing track width and build height across scan speeds.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - Use of a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition in DED NiTi.
  - Use of top-view optical microscopy to measure track width and caliper checks to compare build height across scan-speed settings.
  - Source: paper_003_chunk_01.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Interleaved randomized track layout: on a single substrate, deposit short, separated single tracks (e.g., 20–30 mm long) in a randomized order of scan speeds to reduce thermal history and gradient bias across the plate.
  - Triplicate tracks per speed for basic statistics; report mean and standard deviation for both width and height to robustly map the process window.
  - Define a simple stability score (continuous bead without balling, undercut, or excessive spatter) from top-view images to complement quantitative width/height data.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope (top-view imaging)
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (shielding)
  - Assumed: metallic substrate compatible with the DED system (preferably NiTi plate or 304/316 stainless steel)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: scan speed v (example plan: 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 mm/s; adjust to machine-safe window).
  - Hold constant:
    - Laser power (e.g., fixed at a setting known to produce continuous beads with NiTi on your machine)
    - Powder feed rate (constant mass flow)
    - Stand-off distance
    - Laser spot size/focus
    - Argon shielding flow and shroud arrangement
    - Track length (e.g., 25 mm) and inter-track spacing (e.g., ≥8 mm to limit overlap heating)
  - Replicates: n = 3 tracks per speed, randomized order across the plate.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width: top-view optical microscope images with scale; measure width at 5 evenly spaced positions per track; compute mean ± SD per track and per speed.
  - Build height: use digital calipers to measure step height at bead ends and midspan (e.g., bridge caliper jaws across substrate and bead); take 3 readings per track; compute mean ± SD.
  - Qualitative stability: classify each track as continuous/balling/undercut based on optical images.
  - Outputs: plots of track width vs scan speed and build height vs scan speed; tabulated stability score vs speed; identification of the practical scan-speed window for stable, dimensionally consistent beads under fixed power/feed.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At high speeds: lack of fusion and discontinuous beads leading to under-measured width/height.
  - At low speeds: excessive heat input causing swelling, spatter, or oxidation if shielding is insufficient.
  - Mitigation: begin with a pilot pass at mid-range speed to confirm melt pool behavior; verify argon shielding effectiveness; keep inter-track spacing and randomized order to limit local preheating.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible metallic substrate (e.g., NiTi or stainless steel) and routine surface preparation/cleaning; these are not explicitly listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the DED machine can achieve and stably control the proposed scan-speed range and maintain constant powder feed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

task.evidence_summary: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-track and multilayer-wall deposition of NiTi via DED across a scan-speed sweep to quantify how scan speed affects (a) single-bead track width and (b) per-layer build height. For each scan speed, deposit:
  1) Three replicate single-pass beads (~30–50 mm length) for top-view width measurement.
  2) One 5-layer single-bead wall (same path stacked 5 times) to amplify height for caliper measurement; per-layer build height = total wall height/5.
  Keep laser power, powder feed rate, stand-off, and argon shielding constant.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All processing uses the DED machine with NiTi powder and argon only.
  - Width can be captured by top-view optical microscopy.
  - Build height can be measured with digital calipers on the multilayer wall (amplifies height above base to exceed caliper resolution).
  - No additional metrology (e.g., profilometer, cross-sectioning) is required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition; top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Use a multilayer single-bead wall (5 layers) at each scan speed to indirectly estimate per-layer build height with only calipers, while measuring width on single-pass beads. This separates width (minimally remelted single track) from height (averaged over repeated passes) with the limited metrology available.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (select 5–7 levels spanning the machine’s validated stable range for NiTi; e.g., evenly spaced within the vendor- or lab-validated window).
  - Held constant: Laser power, spot size, powder feed rate, stand-off distance, shielding gas flow, layer height command (for stacking), path length (~30–50 mm), and substrate preheat (if available, keep fixed).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (single-pass beads): Capture top-view images with the optical microscope; measure bead width at 5 evenly spaced locations per bead; report mean ± SD per speed.
  - Build height (5-layer walls): Measure total wall height at 5 locations along the wall with calipers; compute per-layer build height = mean(total height)/5; report mean ± SD per speed.
  - Qualitative stability notes per speed: continuity, balling, excessive spatter, lack-of-fusion indications (visual).
  - Outputs: Curves of track width vs. scan speed and per-layer build height vs. scan speed; identification of the practical stable window where tracks are continuous and geometrically consistent.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At too high scan speeds: balling and discontinuous tracks, compromising width measurement.
  - At too low scan speeds: excessive dilution and overbuild, causing wall slumping and caliper contact ambiguity.
  - Mitigation: Pre-check two speeds within the known stable window; if instability observed, adjust speeds toward the center of the validated range while keeping other parameters fixed.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible flat substrate/build plate (preferably NiTi or a standard DED-compatible plate) mounted in the DED machine; not explicitly listed in BOM.
  - Assumes the DED system provides adequate argon shielding/coverage; oxygen monitoring is not listed and is assumed managed by the machine’s enclosure.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

card_context:

evidence.text: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition. Top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can be used to compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Single-track and multi-layer bead-on-plate study of how scan speed affects track width and cumulative build height in DED of NiTi. For each scan-speed level within the validated stable window, deposit:
  1) Three replicate single tracks (beads) to measure track width by top-view optical microscopy.
  2) One 10-layer straight wall (same track location stacked) to measure cumulative build height with digital calipers.
  Hold laser power, powder feed rate, stand-off, and argon flow constant. Space tracks/walls sufficiently to avoid thermal cross-talk. Program speed-ladder segments as a rapid scouting pass if needed, then run full replicates.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All operations use only the DED machine, NiTi powder, and argon gas.
  - Track width can be captured by the optical microscope (top view).
  - Cumulative build height over 10 layers yields millimeter-scale heights measurable by digital calipers, avoiding the need for a profilometer or cross-sectioning.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_003_chunk_01”: The DED NiTi process has a validated scan-speed window for stable deposition; top-view optical microscopy and caliper checks can compare track width and build height across scan-speed settings.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Use a two-tier approach: (a) a single continuous “speed ladder” bead (sequential 10–20 mm segments at increasing speeds within the validated window) to quickly verify stability and approximate trends; followed by (b) replicated single tracks and 10-layer walls at selected speeds for quantitative measurement. This compresses screening time while still producing statistically useful data.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Optical microscope
  - Digital calipers

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Scan speed (e.g., select 5 levels within the validated stable window reported in the literature card).
  - Held constant: Laser power, powder feed rate, stand-off distance, shielding gas flow rate, layer height command/path, track length.
  - Replicates: ≥3 single-track replicates per speed; 1 multi-layer wall per speed (10 layers).

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Track width (single tracks): Top-view optical microscopy; calibrate pixel size with the microscope scale bar; measure width at three evenly spaced points along each track and average.
  - Cumulative build height (10-layer walls): Digital calipers; measure at three positions along the wall and average; compute height-per-layer by dividing by 10.
  - Qualitative defect notes: visible balling, spatter, underfill/lack-of-fusion, or excessive convexity per segment/speed.
  - Outputs: Width vs. scan speed curve; height-per-layer vs. scan speed curve; identification of speed ranges yielding stable geometry.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Operating outside the stable scan-speed window may cause balling or lack of fusion, corrupting measurements. Mitigation: first run the short “speed ladder” bead to confirm stability zones before committing to full replicates; stay within stable segments for the main dataset.
  - Single-bead height may be below caliper resolution; mitigated by using 10-layer walls for height.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible, flat DED substrate plate/fixture (e.g., NiTi or steel) as part of the DED machine setup; not listed in materials. If not available, this is a missing item.
  - Assumes basic build-plate cleaning and alignment tools are available as part of standard DED operations.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_003_chunk_01

available_bom.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how solution treatment temperature affects retained austenite fraction in DED NiTi coupons.

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_004_chunk_01
DOI: 

DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how solution treatment temperature affects retained austenite fraction in DED NiTi coupons.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric solution treatment temperatures.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

evidence.text: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Study the effect of solution treatment temperature on retained austenite fraction in DED-fabricated NiTi by fabricating identical coupons, then vacuum-furnace solution treating at a series of temperatures (e.g., 700, 750, 800, 850 C for 30 min) with a fixed controlled cooling rate, followed by room-temperature XRD phase fraction quantification.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All required steps—DED fabrication of NiTi, vacuum-furnace heat treatment at controlled temperatures, and XRD-based retained austenite quantification—are directly supported by the available materials (NiTi powder, argon) and equipment (DED machine, vacuum furnace, XRD).

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_004_chunk_01”: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Systematically isolate solution treatment temperature as the only swept variable by:
    - Holding DED parameters and coupon geometry constant across all builds.
    - Using a single heat cycle per temperature but loading all replicates for that temperature together to minimize batch-to-batch variance.
    - Fixing soak time and a programmed cooling rate within the same furnace to remove cooling-rate as a confounder.
  - Perform replicate coupons (e.g., n=3 per temperature) to quantify variability in retained austenite fraction due to build stochasticity.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine (for NiTi)
  - Vacuum furnace with programmable temperature profile
  - XRD with phase identification/quantification capability

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (DED shielding and purge/backfill)
  - Standard XRD sample holders

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Solution treatment temperature = 700, 750, 800, 850 C
  - Held constant:
    - Soak time: 30 min at temperature
    - Furnace atmosphere: high vacuum, argon backfill on cool-down if available
    - Cooling rate: programmed controlled cool at 10 C/min to room temperature
    - DED parameters (example constants): laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, shielding gas flow; keep fixed for all coupons
    - Coupon geometry: e.g., 10 x 10 x 3 mm pads, built on same substrate, same scan strategy

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - XRD at room temperature on the coupon surface:
    - Identify B2 (austenite) and B19' (martensite) peaks
    - Quantify retained austenite fraction via whole-pattern fitting (Rietveld or similar) using B2 and B19' structure files
    - Report retained austenite fraction vs. solution temperature (mean ± stdev, n=3)
  - Ancillary XRD outputs:
    - Peak widths (as a proxy for microstrain/defects) and lattice parameters for B2
    - Note any secondary phases if detected

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Preferred crystallographic texture from DED may bias XRD quantitative phase analysis, leading to errors in retained austenite fraction.
  - Mitigations: measure each coupon with sample spinning and multiple incident angles (if instrument allows), analyze multiple locations per coupon to average texture effects, and keep identical scan strategy across builds to maintain comparable texture.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the vacuum furnace can execute a controlled cooling rate (e.g., 10 C/min) and maintain adequate vacuum to limit oxidation; if not, results may conflate temperature effects with oxidation/precipitation differences.
  - Assumes access to XRD phase-quantification software/workflow (Rietveld or equivalent). If unavailable, semi-quantitative peak area ratios would be a lower-fidelity substitute.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_004_chunk_01

    - Holding DED parameters and coupon geometry constant across all builds.
    - Using a single heat cycle per temperature but loading all replicates for that temperature together to minimize batch-to-batch variance.
    - Fixing soak time and a programmed cooling rate within the same furnace to remove cooling-rate as a confounder.
  - Perform replicate coupons (e.g., n=3 per temperature) to quantify variability in retained austenite fraction due to build stochasticity.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine (for NiTi)
  - Vacuum furnace with programmable temperature profile
  - XRD with phase identification/quantification capability

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (DED shielding and purge/backfill)
  - Standard XRD sample holders

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Solution treatment temperature = 700, 750, 800, 850 C
  - Held constant:
    - Soak time: 30 min at temperature
    - Furnace atmosphere: high vacuum, argon backfill on cool-down if available
    - Cooling rate: programmed controlled cool at 10 C/min to room temperature
    - DED parameters (example constants): laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, shielding gas flow; keep fixed for all coupons
    - Coupon geometry: e.g., 10 x 10 x 3 mm pads, built on same substrate, same scan strategy

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - XRD at room temperature on the coupon surface:
    - Identify B2 (austenite) and B19' (martensite) peaks
    - Quantify retained austenite fraction via whole-pattern fitting (Rietveld or similar) using B2 and B19' structure files
    - Report retained austenite fraction vs. solution temperature (mean ± stdev, n=3)
  - Ancillary XRD outputs:
    - Peak widths (as a proxy for microstrain/defects) and lattice parameters for B2
    - Note any secondary phases if detected

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Preferred crystallographic texture from DED may bias XRD quantitative phase analysis, leading to errors in retained austenite fraction.
  - Mitigations: measure each coupon with sample spinning and multiple incident angles (if instrument allows), analyze multiple locations per coupon to average texture effects, and keep identical scan strategy across builds to maintain comparable texture.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the vacuum furnace can execute a controlled cooling rate (e.g., 10 C/min) and maintain adequate vacuum to limit oxidation; if not, results may conflate temperature effects with oxidation/precipitation differences.
  - Assumes access to XRD phase-quantification software/workflow (Rietveld or equivalent). If unavailable, semi-quantitative peak area ratios would be a lower-fidelity substitute.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_004_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build identical DED NiTi coupons, then vacuum-furnace solution treat them at a series of temperatures to quantify how solution temperature affects retained austenite fraction at room temperature by XRD. Constant DED parameters and constant hold time/cooling are used; only solution temperature is swept.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps use only NiTi powder, argon, a DED machine, a vacuum furnace, and XRD. Literature supports vacuum heat treatment of DED NiTi and XRD phase-fraction comparison.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  From “paper_004_chunk_01”: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  Implement a controlled temperature-sweep study (single variable: solution temperature) on identically built, thin DED coupons to minimize thermal gradients, with standardized vacuum hold and an argon backfill cool-down to keep cooling rate consistent across all temperatures. Include an as-built control to contextualize heat-treatment effects.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Vacuum furnace (with vacuum capability and argon backfill)
  - XRD

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (for DED shielding and furnace backfill)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Solution treatment temperature (primary sweep): 750, 800, 850, 900 °C
  - Fixed parameters (held constant for all coupons):
    - DED: laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, shielding argon flow; build identical 10 × 10 × 2 mm coupons on the same plate to minimize variability.
    - Heat treatment: vacuum ≤ 10^-4 mbar (or best achievable), ramp 10 °C/min, hold 30 min at target temperature, argon backfill to 1 atm at end of hold, passive cool in argon to room temperature on the same furnace shelf.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - XRD at room temperature on coupon top surfaces.
  - Quantify retained austenite fraction using Rietveld refinement or integrated peak methods (e.g., B2 austenite peaks vs B19’ martensite peaks).
  - Secondary outputs: peak widths (indicative of residual stress) and preferred orientation indicators to flag texture effects.
  - Compare phase fraction across temperature sweep; include as-built control.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Cooling-rate variability between runs could change martensite/austenite balance independent of solution temperature. Mitigation: process all temperatures in a single furnace cycle if possible using separate furnaces shelves and staged insertion is not feasible; otherwise, keep identical load, placement, backfill pressure, and cooling protocol for each batch.
  - Composition shift during DED (Ni evaporation) may dominate transformation behavior. Mitigation: use conservative laser parameters and abundant argon shielding to limit evaporation; build all coupons in one job.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Surface preparation for XRD (grinding/polishing) is not listed; assume XRD can analyze the as-built/heat-treated surfaces with acceptable signal, or that minimal surface prep is available. Surface roughness and texture may bias quantitative phase analysis.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_004_chunk_01

evidence.text: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Print a single batch of identical DED NiTi coupons and perform post-build solution treatments at a controlled set of temperatures in a vacuum furnace, holding time and cooling history fixed. Quantify room-temperature retained austenite fraction by XRD and compare across temperatures.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps use only NiTi powder, argon shielding/backfill, a DED machine for coupon fabrication, a vacuum furnace for solution treatments, and XRD for phase quantification.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From “paper_004_chunk_01”: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Apply a controlled temperature sweep (e.g., 700–900 C) at constant time and identical argon-assisted backfill/cool to isolate the effect of solution temperature alone on retained austenite after DED. Use identical, single-batch printed coupons to minimize composition/porosity variability across conditions and perform Rietveld-based phase quantification from XRD to estimate retained austenite fraction.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine
- Vacuum furnace with vacuum and argon backfill capability
- XRD (lab diffractometer with Cu Kα or equivalent)

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder (equiatomic target)
- Argon gas (high purity for DED shielding and furnace backfill)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Solution treatment temperature: 700, 750, 800, 850, 900 C
- Fixed hold time: 45 minutes (constant across all temperatures)
- Furnace atmosphere: high vacuum to ≤1E-4 mbar, then argon backfill to ~1 bar before cool
- Cooling protocol: identical for all samples; argon backfill at temperature, then cool in furnace under flowing argon (or rapid argon backfill quench if available) to hold cooling rate constant
- DED build parameters: held constant for all coupons (example starting point—adjust to machine capability): laser power ~300–400 W, scan speed ~600–800 mm/min, hatch spacing ~0.6 mm, layer thickness ~50–60 µm, 3–4 scan rotations, argon shielding to keep O2 < 100 ppm. Coupon geometry: 10 × 10 × 3 mm; n = 3 per temperature.

- Measurements / Outputs:
- Room-temperature XRD scans (e.g., 2θ = 35–55° and 60–85° with slow step size ~0.02° and sufficient counting) to capture B2 austenite and B19' martensite peaks
- Quantitative phase fraction of retained austenite via Rietveld refinement using standard NiTi phase files, reported as mean ± SD across replicates
- Secondary XRD outputs: lattice parameters for B2/B19' to check composition/strain shifts across temperatures
- Report: retained austenite fraction vs. solution temperature; note any systematic peak broadening or texture changes qualitatively

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
- Compositional drift (Ni evaporation during DED) or oxygen pickup may shift transformation temperatures and confound the temperature effect. Mitigations: maintain low O2 with robust argon shielding during DED; keep laser energy density moderate; print all coupons in one session and randomize heat-treatment order.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- No DSC is available, so inference relies on XRD phase fractions at room temperature rather than direct transformation temperatures. Assumes argon backfill/cooling provides a consistent cooling rate across all samples.

- Source Papers Used:
- paper_004_chunk_01

evidence.text: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Temperature-sweep solution treatment of DED-fabricated NiTi coupons to quantify retained austenite fraction by XRD. Build a batch of identical DED coupons, then solution treat subsets at four temperatures (750, 800, 850, 900 C) for 30 minutes in a vacuum furnace, followed by standardized argon backfill cooling. Measure retained austenite fraction via XRD and compare across temperatures, including an as-built control.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All required steps use available items: DED machine (fabrication), vacuum furnace (solution treatment), XRD (phase quantification), argon gas (shielding during DED and furnace backfill).
  - Only NiTi powder and argon are needed as materials.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From "paper_004_chunk_01": DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is an evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - A controlled, single-variable temperature sweep with constant hold time and standardized argon-backfill cooling to isolate the effect of solution temperature on retained austenite in DED NiTi.
  - Include an as-built control and build-all-in-one batch strategy (identical toolpath and parameters) to minimize composition and thermal history variability across conditions, addressing DED-induced variability without adding new equipment.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Vacuum furnace with vacuum capability and argon backfill
  - XRD (laboratory diffractometer with Cu Kα or equivalent)

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Solution treatment temperature: 750, 800, 850, 900 C
  - Fixed parameters (held constant across all conditions):
    - Hold time: 30 minutes at temperature
    - Furnace environment: high vacuum (<1×10^-4 mbar if achievable), then argon backfill to ~1 atm for cooling
    - Cooling protocol: initiate argon backfill immediately after hold; allow forced convection cooling with continuous argon purge
    - DED build parameters: laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, shielding argon flow; keep constant and document
    - Geometry: simple coupons (e.g., 10×10×3 mm) to ensure uniform through-thickness heat treatment
    - Replicates: n=3 per temperature plus n=3 as-built controls

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - XRD phase analysis on each coupon surface:
    - Scan 2θ range to capture B2 austenite and B19' martensite peaks; use identical scan settings across all samples
    - Semi-quantitative retained austenite fraction via Rietveld refinement or integrated peak intensity ratios (consistent method across all samples)
  - Secondary outputs:
    - Record DED build log (energy density) and measured coupon dimensions/mass for traceability
  - Outcome: retained austenite fraction versus solution temperature curve, with statistical spread from replicates

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Composition drift during DED (e.g., Ni loss) shifting transformation temperatures and confounding the temperature-sweep effect. Mitigation: single-batch build with identical parameters, minimize overheating (optimize energy density), and maintain robust argon shielding to limit oxidation/evaporation.
  - Cooling-rate variability after furnace hold can alter phase fractions. Mitigation: identical argon backfill timing/pressure and purge flow for all samples; process samples in small, consistent loads.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes access to standard XRD phase-quantification software or established peak-integration workflow; if unavailable, report relative changes using consistent peak area ratios rather than absolute phase fractions.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_004_chunk_01

Use the literature cards below as precedent, but do NOT pretend the experiment is already published.

[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_004_chunk_01

- If the BOM is insufficient, say so explicitly.
- Cite source papers by exact title from the retrieved cards.

card_context:
[Paper Card 1]
Title: paper_004_chunk_01

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how solution treatment temperature affects retained austenite fraction in DED NiTi coupons.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric solution treatment temperatures.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

task.evidence_summary: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

evidence.text: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a single batch of DED NiTi coupons, section them into identical subspecimens, then perform a controlled solution-treatment temperature sweep in a vacuum furnace (e.g., 750, 800, 850, 900, 950 C for 30 minutes, high vacuum), followed by rapid argon backfill cooling. Quantify room-temperature retained austenite fraction for each temperature condition using XRD with phase-fraction analysis.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - DED machine + NiTi powder + argon gas: to fabricate the coupons with inert shielding.
  - Vacuum furnace: to apply solution treatments at controlled temperatures.
  - Argon gas: for backfill and accelerated cooling after solution treatment.
  - XRD: to measure retained austenite fraction as the outcome metric described in the literature card.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_004_chunk_01”: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Apply a tightly controlled single-variable sweep of solution-treatment temperature on coupons produced in one DED build, then use argon-backfilled rapid cooling to standardize post-treatment cooling across temperatures. This isolates temperature as the primary variable impacting retained austenite at room temperature.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Vacuum furnace with argon backfill capability
  - XRD

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (DED shielding and furnace backfill)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary: Solution treatment temperature (e.g., 750, 800, 850, 900, 950 C)
  - Held constant:
    - Hold time: 30 minutes at temperature
    - Furnace vacuum level: high vacuum prior to heat (as available)
    - Cooling: argon backfill to a fixed pressure (e.g., 1 atm) initiated within 1 minute of soak end
    - DED build parameters: fix laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, and argon shielding flow to produce a single uniform batch; then section coupons to minimize build-to-build variability

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - XRD at room temperature on each condition
    - Phase identification and quantitative phase fraction of B2 austenite vs B19’ martensite via Rietveld or reference-intensity-ratio analysis
    - Optional: lattice parameter shift of B2 to infer composition/strain trends across temperatures
  - Report: retained austenite fraction vs solution-treatment temperature

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Composition drift during DED (e.g., Ni loss) or oxygen pickup can shift transformation temperatures, confounding the temperature–austenite relationship. Mitigation: robust argon shielding during DED, conservative energy input to limit evaporation, and using one build split into subspecimens to reduce variability.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the vacuum furnace can reach up to ~950 C with controlled argon backfill for accelerated cooling.
  - Assumes standard metallographic sample preparation tools to produce a flat surface for XRD (if not available, surface roughness may degrade quantitative phase analysis).

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_004_chunk_01

task.evidence_summary: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

evidence.text: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build identical DED NiTi coupons, then vacuum solution-treat at a set of temperatures, and quantify retained austenite fraction by XRD to establish the temperature–fraction relationship. Specifically:
  1) Produce 10 identical DED pads (≈10×10×3 mm) using one fixed DED parameter set.
  2) Split into 5 groups (n=2 per temperature) for vacuum furnace solution treatment at 700, 750, 800, 850, and 900°C, 30 minutes hold, high vacuum, then argon backfill and controlled cool to room temperature.
  3) Perform XRD on all coupons post-heat-treatment and quantify retained austenite fraction (e.g., from integrated intensities of B2 austenite vs B19' martensite peaks under identical scan conditions).
  4) Plot retained austenite fraction vs solution temperature; assess reproducibility across duplicates.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - DED machine can fabricate the NiTi coupons.
  - Vacuum furnace provides controlled solution treatment temperatures.
  - Argon gas can be used for backfilling and an inert cooling environment.
  - XRD enables retained austenite fraction comparison across conditions.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From "paper_004_chunk_01": DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Use a tightly controlled, single-build batch of DED coupons to minimize as-built variability, then isolate the effect of solution temperature by applying identical hold time and cooling protocol across all groups.
  - Standardize XRD acquisition parameters and quantify retained austenite fraction via consistent peak-integration workflow across all coupons to generate a direct temperature–fraction calibration for this specific DED parameter set.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Vacuum furnace
  - XRD

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Solution treatment temperature = 700, 750, 800, 850, 900°C
  - Fixed parameters (held constant):
    - Hold time = 30 minutes
    - Vacuum level = as low as the furnace permits prior to argon backfill
    - Cooling protocol = argon backfill at temperature end, then cool in furnace to room temperature at the furnace’s natural rate
    - DED build parameters (single fixed recipe): laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, and preheat (choose one stable set and keep constant for all coupons)

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - XRD scans post-heat-treatment (identical 2θ range and step size for all samples)
  - Quantified retained austenite fraction from integrated peak intensities of B2 (e.g., {110}B2) vs B19' (e.g., {001}B19' / {020}B19') under identical analysis settings
  - Replicate variability (difference between duplicates at each temperature)
  - Output: Retained austenite fraction vs solution temperature curve for this DED NiTi

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Cooling rate after solution treatment may not be fast enough to freeze in the high-temperature phase consistently, causing temperature effects to be conflated with cooling-rate effects. Also, texture from DED may bias XRD quantitative phase analysis.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the vacuum furnace can be backfilled with argon at the end of the hold and provide a consistent, inert cool to room temperature; no active water quench capability is listed.
  - Assumes the XRD system and analysis workflow can provide semi-quantitative phase fractions via peak integration under identical settings (no dedicated Rietveld software is specified).

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_004_chunk_01

task.evidence_summary: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

evidence.text: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Systematically vary solution treatment temperature of DED-built NiTi coupons and quantify retained austenite fraction at room temperature by XRD. Produce a single batch of nominally identical DED coupons, then heat treat each at one of five temperatures (700, 750, 800, 850, 900 C) for 30 minutes in a vacuum furnace under the same vacuum and cooling schedule. After heat treatment, perform room-temperature XRD and use peak deconvolution/Rietveld refinement to estimate retained B2 austenite fraction versus treatment temperature.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps (DED build, vacuum furnace heat treatment at controlled temperature, and XRD-based phase quantification) are explicitly supported by the available equipment and materials.
  - No additional consumables beyond NiTi powder and argon shielding/backfill are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_004_chunk_01”: DED NiTi coupons can be heat treated in a vacuum furnace at controlled temperatures, and XRD is the evidence-supported method for comparing retained austenite fraction afterward.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - A tightly controlled, single-variable sweep of solution treatment temperature with fixed hold time and identical cooling schedule to isolate temperature effects on retained austenite in DED NiTi, reducing confounding from variable cooling or atmospheres.
  - Standardized DED build strategy (constant energy density and argon shielding) to minimize composition drift across coupons, thereby improving interpretability of the temperature sweep.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine (for NiTi)
  - Vacuum furnace with programmable temperature and controlled cooling, plus argon backfill
  - XRD instrument capable of phase fraction analysis

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (DED shielding and furnace backfill)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Solution treatment temperature = 700, 750, 800, 850, 900 C
  - Fixed parameters (held constant across all coupons):
    - DED build: laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness, shielding argon flow (select values that yield dense, near-equiatomic NiTi; e.g., fixed energy density and identical scan strategy for all coupons)
    - Heat treatment: hold time = 30 minutes; vacuum level ≤ 1e-4 mbar (or as low as furnace allows); ramp rate = 10 C/min up and down
    - Cooling: controlled furnace cool at 10 C/min to room temperature under vacuum, then argon backfill at room temperature before venting
    - Sample geometry: same coupon dimensions and orientation to limit texture differences

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - XRD patterns at room temperature for each coupon
  - Quantitative retained austenite (B2) fraction via Rietveld refinement or peak ratio method using characteristic B2 and B19′ peaks
  - Secondary outputs: lattice parameters of B2/B19′ (from XRD) and qualitative texture notes (via peak intensity deviations)
  - Report: retained austenite fraction vs solution treatment temperature with error bars from replicate coupons (n ≥ 2 per temperature)

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Composition variability (e.g., Ni loss during DED) can shift transformation behavior and confound the temperature effect; also, XRD texture can bias phase fraction estimates.
  - Mitigations: use identical, conservative DED energy density and strong argon shielding to limit evaporation/oxidation; fabricate all coupons in one run; sample the same surface orientation; rotate/rock samples during XRD acquisition; use consistent scan parameters and apply the same refinement protocol to all datasets.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the vacuum furnace can achieve stable vacuum (≤ 1e-4 mbar) and programmed ramp/cool rates, and the XRD system has software for phase fraction analysis. If Rietveld software is unavailable, use a consistent peak-area ratio method as a fallback with clearly stated limitations.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_004_chunk_01
