evidence.text: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a single DED NiTi coupon subdivided into four adjacent 10 × 10 mm subregions, each printed in the same build with identical parameters except hatch spacing. Use hatch spacings H = {0.25, 0.35, 0.45, 0.55 mm} covering a stable process window. Include a 1–2 mm contour scan barrier around each subregion to limit thermal cross-talk. After printing, section the coupon transverse to the scan plane, mount, grind, and polish to mirror finish. Perform Vickers microhardness mapping (e.g., HV0.2) on each subregion’s cross-section using a 15 × 10 grid with 200 µm spacing to capture through-height and lateral hardness variation. Keep laser power, scan speed, layer thickness, layer rotation, and shielding gas flow constant across all subregions. As an optional internal control, repeat the two extreme hatch spacings (0.25 and 0.55 mm) in a second coupon while adjusting scan speed to maintain constant areal energy input, to separate geometric overlap effects from energy density effects.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps (DED build, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and microhardness mapping) are directly supported by the listed equipment and consumables.
  - No external characterization tools beyond the microhardness tester are required.
  - Argon shielding is available for DED processing.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window.
  - From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Use a single coupon partitioned into multiple hatch-spacing subregions with contour barriers to minimize thermal cross-talk, enabling a controlled, high-throughput comparison under identical global thermal boundary conditions.
  - Optional internal control: at two hatch spacings, adjust scan speed to keep areal energy input constant, helping distinguish the effect of geometric overlap from energy density.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Microhardness tester (Vickers mode)
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Standard grinding papers and polishing suspensions/cloths compatible with the polishing kit

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Hatch spacing H = 0.25, 0.35, 0.45, 0.55 mm
  - Held constant for all subregions: laser power, scan speed (unless doing optional control), layer thickness, layer rotation (e.g., 90°), spot size, powder feed rate, argon flow, interlayer dwell
  - Optional control subset: adjust scan speed at H = 0.25 and 0.55 mm to maintain constant areal energy input relative to the baseline condition

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Vickers microhardness maps (HV0.2 or similar) across each subregion’s cross-section
  - Metrics: mean hardness, standard deviation, hardness gradient with build height, and lateral uniformity for each hatch spacing
  - Comparative plots of hardness vs hatch spacing; for the optional control, compare constant-speed vs constant-areal-energy cases

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Extreme hatch spacings may cause lack of fusion (too large H) or overheating/porosity (too small H), leading to erratic hardness or unusable regions. Thermal interaction between adjacent subregions could still bias results if contour barriers and spacing are insufficient.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED machine’s existing NiTi “stable window” recipe is known and can be used to set fixed parameters; numerical values are not provided in the literature card.
  - Assumes the microhardness tester has sufficient optical capability to place indents on polished NiTi without additional etching.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

After printing, section the coupon transverse to the scan plane, mount, grind, and polish to mirror finish. Perform Vickers microhardness mapping (e.g., HV0.2) on each subregion’s cross-section using a 15 × 10 grid with 200 µm spacing to capture through-height and lateral hardness variation. Keep laser power, scan speed, layer thickness, layer rotation, and shielding gas flow constant across all subregions. As an optional internal control, repeat the two extreme hatch spacings (0.25 and 0.55 mm) in a second coupon while adjusting scan speed to maintain constant areal energy input, to separate geometric overlap effects from energy density effects.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  - All steps (DED build, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and microhardness mapping) are directly supported by the listed equipment and consumables.
  - No external characterization tools beyond the microhardness tester are required.
  - Argon shielding is available for DED processing.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window.
  - From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Use a single coupon partitioned into multiple hatch-spacing subregions with contour barriers to minimize thermal cross-talk, enabling a controlled, high-throughput comparison under identical global thermal boundary conditions.
  - Optional internal control: at two hatch spacings, adjust scan speed to keep areal energy input constant, helping distinguish the effect of geometric overlap from energy density.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Microhardness tester (Vickers mode)
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Standard grinding papers and polishing suspensions/cloths compatible with the polishing kit

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Hatch spacing H = 0.25, 0.35, 0.45, 0.55 mm
  - Held constant for all subregions: laser power, scan speed (unless doing optional control), layer thickness, layer rotation (e.g., 90°), spot size, powder feed rate, argon flow, interlayer dwell
  - Optional control subset: adjust scan speed at H = 0.25 and 0.55 mm to maintain constant areal energy input relative to the baseline condition

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Vickers microhardness maps (HV0.2 or similar) across each subregion’s cross-section
  - Metrics: mean hardness, standard deviation, hardness gradient with build height, and lateral uniformity for each hatch spacing
  - Comparative plots of hardness vs hatch spacing; for the optional control, compare constant-speed vs constant-areal-energy cases

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Extreme hatch spacings may cause lack of fusion (too large H) or overheating/porosity (too small H), leading to erratic hardness or unusable regions. Thermal interaction between adjacent subregions could still bias results if contour barriers and spacing are insufficient.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED machine’s existing NiTi “stable window” recipe is known and can be used to set fixed parameters; numerical values are not provided in the literature card.
  - Assumes the microhardness tester has sufficient optical capability to place indents on polished NiTi without additional etching.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Build a single DED NiTi coupon containing a 3×2 array of 10×10×5 mm tiles, each tile printed with a different hatch spacing while keeping laser power, scan speed, and layer thickness constant. Section each tile, mount, grind/polish, and perform microhardness mapping on polished cross-sections to quantify the effect of hatch spacing on local and average hardness.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps use only the DED machine, NiTi powder, argon shielding, and standard metallography plus a microhardness tester, which are present in the BOM. The literature card supports varying hatch spacing within a stable DED NiTi window and using polished cross-sections for microhardness mapping.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Implement a within-one-build hatch-spacing array to minimize build-to-build variability, with randomized tile placement to reduce positional/thermal bias. Conduct fine-grid microhardness mapping across tracks and through height to separate intra-tile gradients (due to thermal overlap) from tile-average effects. Maintain all other parameters fixed to isolate hatch spacing as the primary variable.

- Needed Equipment:
DED machine, microhardness tester (Vickers), precision saw, mounting press, grinding and polishing kit.

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
NiTi powder, argon gas, mounting resin.

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Primary sweep: Hatch spacing h = [80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 200] µm (6 tiles).
- Hold constant across all tiles (example setpoints, adjust to machine capability):
  - Laser power P ≈ 250 W
  - Scan speed v ≈ 600 mm/min
  - Layer thickness t ≈ 50 µm
  - Scan strategy: bidirectional raster with 90° rotation between layers
  - Shielding: argon, O2 as low as achievable on the machine
- Build layout: 3×2 tile array, randomize hatch-setting assignment to positions.

- Measurements / Outputs:
- Microhardness mapping (Vickers): e.g., HV0.3–HV0.5 load depending on imprint size vs feature scale.
  - For each tile: 2D grid on a mid-height cross-section with 200–300 µm spacing between indents; at least 3 rows near top/middle/bottom to capture height-wise gradients.
- Outputs:
  - Tile-average HV vs hatch spacing
  - Intra-tile HV variance vs hatch spacing
  - Hardness line scans perpendicular to scan tracks to assess effect of melt pool overlap
  - Qualitative notes on surface/edge quality and any observable lack-of-fusion in polished cross-sections (without etching)
- Data analysis:
  - One-way ANOVA of tile-average HV over hatch spacing
  - Correlate hardness variance with hatch spacing to infer change in melt-pool overlap/porosity tendencies.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
Excessively large hatch spacing may induce lack-of-fusion, causing severe scatter or unusable tiles; excessively small spacing may cause overheating and distortion. Mitigation: start with a pilot on two hatch values (e.g., 100 and 160 µm) to verify stability, then complete full array.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Assumes the DED machine allows per-tile hatch spacing programming within one build and maintains low oxygen with argon shielding.
- Assumes the microhardness tester has a Vickers indenter and supports 300–500 g loads.
- No etchants are listed; interpretation relies on polished-section visibility without etching.

- Source Papers Used:
paper_005_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build three identical NiTi DED cubic coupons (≈10 x 10 x 6 mm) while varying only hatch spacing across three levels, then prepare cross-sections and perform Vickers microhardness mapping to quantify how hatch spacing influences spatial hardness distributions.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps—DED fabrication under argon, sectioning with a precision saw, mounting, grinding/polishing, and microhardness testing—are supported by the listed materials and equipment. No additional metrology beyond the microhardness tester is required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  Apply a controlled three-level hatch spacing sweep while holding other scan parameters constant, and perform region-resolved microhardness mapping (top/mid/bottom and near-track-center vs inter-track zones) on each coupon’s mid-plane to separate layer-wise thermal history effects from track overlap effects. Include a simple areal hardness variance metric as a proxy for microstructural uniformity versus hatch spacing.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Microhardness tester (Vickers capable with built-in optics)
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - SiC papers and polishing media (e.g., up to 1 µm finish; included in kit)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Hatch spacing (HS) at three levels, e.g., HS1 = 0.3 mm, HS2 = 0.5 mm, HS3 = 0.7 mm.
  - Hold constant across all builds:
    - Laser power (e.g., 250–300 W; fixed)
    - Scan speed (e.g., 10–12 mm/s; fixed)
    - Layer thickness (e.g., 0.2 mm; fixed)
    - Shielding gas: argon with consistent flow
    - Scan strategy: bidirectional, 90° rotation between layers
    - Track length and build footprint identical for all coupons
    - Inter-layer dwell time (e.g., 5–10 s) to maintain comparable heat accumulation

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Vickers microhardness mapping (e.g., HV0.2) on a polished mid-plane cross-section:
    - Grid: 0.5 mm spacing over the full cross-section
    - Region bins: top third, mid third, bottom third; and annotations for nominal track-center vs inter-track zones based on hatch geometry
  - Metrics:
    - Mean HV and standard deviation per coupon and per region
    - Through-thickness hardness gradient (top-to-bottom delta)
    - Areal hardness variance as a uniformity indicator versus hatch spacing
  - Qualitative notes during polishing about any visible lack-of-fusion or macro porosity (no additional imaging required)

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Too-large hatch spacing may cause lack-of-fusion, leading to erratic hardness or indenter pop-ins.
  - Excessive overlap (very small hatch spacing) may raise heat input, producing cracking or altered composition via evaporation, thus skewing hardness.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumption: The DED system’s laser spot size/nozzle setup yields melt pool widths compatible with the proposed hatch spacings; if not, the absolute HS values will be adjusted to target approximately 30–60% track overlap.
  - No DSC or microscopy is available to tie hardness directly to phase fractions; hardness will be interpreted as a comparative metric only.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a set of small DED NiTi cubic coupons in a single run, each with a different hatch spacing while keeping all other process parameters constant. Prepare polished cross-sections and perform Vickers microhardness mapping to quantify how hatch spacing influences hardness distribution through the build.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  The DED machine and available NiTi powder with argon shielding enable fabrication of NiTi coupons with controlled hatch spacing. The precision saw, mounting press, and grinding/polishing kit allow standard metallographic preparation. The microhardness tester enables hardness mapping on polished sections without additional instrumentation.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  From “paper_005_chunk_01”: 
  - Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window.
  - Polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Build an array of 4–5 small coupons (e.g., 10 × 10 × 6 mm each) on the same substrate in one run, each using a distinct hatch spacing, to minimize run-to-run variability.
  - Map hardness on two orthogonal planes per coupon (mid-height cross-section and a sidewall section) to capture both inter-track and inter-layer effects of hatch spacing while still using only the available tools.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Microhardness tester
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Standard grinding papers and polishing suspensions/cloths (assumed part of the grinding/polishing kit)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary variable: Hatch spacing (e.g., 0.6×, 0.8×, 1.0×, 1.2× of melt pool width; translate to absolute spacings once a baseline track width is established on the machine).
  - Held constant across coupons:
    - Laser power (fixed at a value already known to produce fully dense NiTi on your DED system)
    - Scan speed
    - Layer thickness
    - Shielding gas flow and chamber/shroud configuration
    - Scan strategy (e.g., bidirectional with 90° rotation between layers)
    - Number of layers/part height
  - Optional check: Record melt pool track width from the machine’s standard optics/readback (if available) to normalize hatch spacing as a fraction of track width; if not available, proceed with nominal hatch spacings and note the assumption.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Microhardness (Vickers, e.g., HV0.2 or HV0.3) maps on polished cross-sections:
    - For each coupon, prepare a mid-height cross-section. Create a 2D grid of indents (e.g., 10 × 10 array at 200–300 µm spacing) spanning multiple tracks and layers.
    - For one coupon per condition, also prepare a longitudinal side section to probe inter-layer effects.
  - Outputs:
    - Mean and standard deviation of hardness per coupon
    - Hardness profiles across inter-track directions (to capture overlap effects) and through build height
    - Compare hardness homogeneity and mean hardness vs hatch spacing to identify under/overlap regimes (e.g., soft bands from lack-of-fusion at large spacing; elevated hardness from higher remelt/overlap at small spacing)

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Excessive hatch spacing may cause lack-of-fusion, porosity, and erratic hardness; too small spacing may cause overheating, composition shifts, or cracking in NiTi. Mitigation: start from a baseline hatch spacing known to yield fully dense tracks, then step ±20–40% in spacing; abort conditions showing visible balling or track instability.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible build substrate/plate (e.g., NiTi or stainless steel) and standard DED fixturing; the substrate is not listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the microhardness tester has built-in optics sufficient for placing and measuring indents (typical for such instruments).
  - No etchants are listed; procedure relies on mechanical polishing to a mirror finish without etching.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

  - Polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Build an array of 4–5 small coupons (e.g., 10 × 10 × 6 mm each) on the same substrate in one run, each using a distinct hatch spacing, to minimize run-to-run variability.
  - Map hardness on two orthogonal planes per coupon (mid-height cross-section and a sidewall section) to capture both inter-track and inter-layer effects of hatch spacing while still using only the available tools.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Microhardness tester
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Standard grinding papers and polishing suspensions/cloths (assumed part of the grinding/polishing kit)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary variable: Hatch spacing (e.g., 0.6×, 0.8×, 1.0×, 1.2× of melt pool width; translate to absolute spacings once a baseline track width is established on the machine).
  - Held constant across coupons:
    - Laser power (fixed at a value already known to produce fully dense NiTi on your DED system)
    - Scan speed
    - Layer thickness
    - Shielding gas flow and chamber/shroud configuration
    - Scan strategy (e.g., bidirectional with 90° rotation between layers)
    - Number of layers/part height
  - Optional check: Record melt pool track width from the machine’s standard optics/readback (if available) to normalize hatch spacing as a fraction of track width; if not available, proceed with nominal hatch spacings and note the assumption.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Microhardness (Vickers, e.g., HV0.2 or HV0.3) maps on polished cross-sections:
    - For each coupon, prepare a mid-height cross-section. Create a 2D grid of indents (e.g., 10 × 10 array at 200–300 µm spacing) spanning multiple tracks and layers.
    - For one coupon per condition, also prepare a longitudinal side section to probe inter-layer effects.
  - Outputs:
    - Mean and standard deviation of hardness per coupon
    - Hardness profiles across inter-track directions (to capture overlap effects) and through build height
    - Compare hardness homogeneity and mean hardness vs hatch spacing to identify under/overlap regimes (e.g., soft bands from lack-of-fusion at large spacing; elevated hardness from higher remelt/overlap at small spacing)

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Excessive hatch spacing may cause lack-of-fusion, porosity, and erratic hardness; too small spacing may cause overheating, composition shifts, or cracking in NiTi. Mitigation: start from a baseline hatch spacing known to yield fully dense tracks, then step ±20–40% in spacing; abort conditions showing visible balling or track instability.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible build substrate/plate (e.g., NiTi or stainless steel) and standard DED fixturing; the substrate is not listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the microhardness tester has built-in optics sufficient for placing and measuring indents (typical for such instruments).
  - No etchants are listed; procedure relies on mechanical polishing to a mirror finish without etching.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build three identical NiTi DED coupons while sweeping hatch spacing (low/medium/high overlap based on in-session single-bead width), then prepare polished cross-sections and perform Vickers microhardness mapping to quantify how hatch spacing affects average hardness and through-thickness hardness gradients.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  The DED machine and argon gas allow controlled variation of hatch spacing while holding other parameters constant. The precision saw, mounting press, and grinding/polishing kit enable cross-section preparation. The microhardness tester enables hardness mapping on polished sections. All required materials and tools are present in the BOM.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  Before building the test coupons, deposit single-bead tracks at the selected power/speed to measure the actual bead width on the substrate; define three hatch spacings as percentages of this measured bead width to target ~70%, ~50%, and ~30% overlap (e.g., HS = 0.3×, 0.5×, 0.7× bead width). This normalizes hatch spacing to the melt pool geometry of the specific machine setup and powder lot, improving comparability across builds.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Microhardness tester
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - SiC papers and polishing media (from the polishing kit’s standard supplies)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Hatch spacing (three levels set as fractions of measured single-bead width; target overlaps ≈70%, 50%, 30%)
  - Held constant (single “baseline” setting for all builds): laser power, scan speed, layer thickness, spot size (as configured), layer rotation strategy (e.g., 90°), shielding gas flow, substrate type and temperature (ambient), build geometry (e.g., 10×10×10 mm cube), and scan order.
  - Suggested baseline (to be validated on the machine during single-bead trials): choose power/scan speed that produce fully dense tracks without excessive keyholing; then record bead width for hatch-spacing normalization.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Vickers microhardness map on a mid-plane cross-section of each coupon (e.g., HV0.5 or similar), using a grid with ~0.5–1.0 mm spacing and ≥5×5 indents, excluding a 0.5 mm edge margin.
  - Metrics: mean hardness per coupon, standard deviation, top-to-bottom hardness profile, and hardness vs. distance to hatch boundaries if visible.
  - Secondary observations: qualitative assessment of lack-of-fusion indications (via polish etch-contrast), presence of large pores intersecting indents (flag and exclude).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Too-large hatch spacing may cause lack of fusion and erratic hardness; too-small spacing may induce overheating, porosity, or cracking in NiTi. Mitigation: choose hatch levels after bead-width measurement and a quick two-layer pilot to verify bonding; adjust to avoid extremes while preserving separation between levels.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the microhardness tester supports a Vickers load suitable for NiTi (e.g., 300–500 g). If limited, use the closest available load and keep it constant.
  - Assumes the DED machine can set hatch spacing explicitly and maintain stable argon shielding and logging of power/speed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

evidence.text: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a single combinatorial DED NiTi coupon containing four adjacent 10 mm × 10 mm × 6 mm blocks, each deposited with a different hatch spacing, then prepare polished cross-sections and perform Vickers microhardness mapping to quantify how hatch spacing influences hardness through the build height and across tracks.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps rely only on the available DED machine, argon shielding, NiTi powder, and standard metallography and microhardness equipment listed. No external characterization tools are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  From “paper_005_chunk_01”: hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi processing window, and polished cross-sections are suitable for microhardness mapping after preparation with standard grinding/polishing tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  Implement a single-build, side-by-side “multi-hatch” coupon to minimize between-build variability in thermal history. Perform height-resolved microhardness line scans (near-substrate, mid-height, near-top) within each hatch-spacing block to separate in-layer overlap effects from layer-by-layer remelting effects, while holding all other parameters constant.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Microhardness tester
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Standard grinding papers and polishing media (from kit)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Hatch spacing (e.g., 0.4 mm, 0.6 mm, 0.8 mm, 1.0 mm). Choose values within the machine’s known stable NiTi window.
  - Hold constant across all blocks:
    - Laser power (e.g., 300 W; use your established stable setting)
    - Scan speed (e.g., 8–10 mm/s; consistent with the stable window)
    - Layer height (e.g., 0.3 mm)
    - Scan strategy: bidirectional, 90° rotation between layers
    - Shielding: argon per standard machine practice
    - Substrate and preheat: as per your standard NiTi recipe (unchanged across blocks)

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Sample preparation: Section coupon perpendicular to scan plane to expose cross-sections of all four blocks; mount, grind, and polish to a mirror finish.
  - Microhardness mapping:
    - Vickers, e.g., 200 gf, 10 s dwell (or your lab’s standard)
    - For each block, perform three horizontal line scans at:
      1) 0.5 mm above the substrate,
      2) mid-height,
      3) 0.5 mm below the top surface.
    - Indent spacing: ≥3× diagonal length and ≥1 mm from free edges to avoid interaction/edge effects.
    - Target ~15 indents per line per block (≈45 per block; ≈180 indents total).
  - Outputs:
    - Mean and standard deviation of hardness for each line and block
    - Through-thickness hardness gradients per block
    - Trend of average hardness vs hatch spacing
    - Optional derived metric: areal energy input proxy = (laser power / scan speed) ÷ hatch spacing, to correlate overlap with hardness

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Excessive hatch spacing may cause lack-of-fusion and very low/localized hardness; too-small spacing may cause overheating, dilution changes, or excessive remelting. Either can distort hardness trends or lead to build defects.
  - Mitigation: select hatch spacings within the machine’s verified stable NiTi window; build a short pilot strip to confirm bead overlap before the full coupon.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED machine allows explicit hatch spacing control and that a stable parameter recipe for NiTi is already established.
  - No imaging tool is listed; porosity or melt pool geometry cannot be quantified directly—interpretation will rely on hardness maps and process parameters.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Build a matrix of DED NiTi cubic coupons while varying hatch spacing only, then perform cross-sectional Vickers microhardness mapping to quantify how hatch spacing influences hardness distribution across and through the build.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps (DED build → sectioning → mounting → grinding/polishing → microhardness mapping) are supported by the provided DED machine, precision saw, mounting press with resin, grinding/polishing kit, and microhardness tester. Argon gas supports shielding. No etching is strictly required for microhardness as long as a high-quality polish is achieved.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From “paper_005_chunk_01”: (1) Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi processing window, and (2) polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with standard metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Within one controlled build job, produce four nominally identical 10 mm × 10 mm × 5 mm blocks, each with a distinct hatch spacing, while holding laser power, scan speed, layer thickness, and shielding identical. Perform high-density 2D microhardness mapping (e.g., 0.5 mm grid) across the mid-height cross-section of each coupon to resolve both through-thickness gradients and lateral variations between hatch tracks. This isolates hatch spacing effects without confounding changes to linear energy input.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine
- Precision saw
- Mounting press
- Grinding and polishing kit
- Microhardness tester

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder
- Argon gas
- Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Hatch spacing (primary sweep): propose four levels, e.g., 0.30 mm, 0.45 mm, 0.60 mm, 0.75 mm
- Hold constant across all coupons:
  - Laser power (e.g., a value within your established stable NiTi DED window)
  - Scan speed (fixed)
  - Layer thickness (fixed, e.g., 0.3 mm or as per your standard NiTi recipe)
  - Shielding gas flow/composition (argon; fixed flow rate)
  - Scan strategy (e.g., bidirectional, 90° rotation between layers if that is your standard—kept identical for all)
  - Substrate preheat and interlayer dwell (if used in your standard practice—kept identical for all)

- Measurements / Outputs:
- Cross-section preparation: cut each coupon at mid-length; mount; grind to 1200–4000 grit; polish to 1 µm finish (no etch required).
- Microhardness mapping: Vickers HV0.2 (or your lab’s standard load) on a 0.5 mm × 0.5 mm grid across the cross-section, avoiding edges by ≥2.5× indent diagonal spacing; record at least 8×8 indents per coupon.
- Outputs:
  - Mean HV and standard deviation per coupon
  - 2D hardness contour maps to visualize lateral variation tied to hatch spacing
  - Through-thickness hardness profiles (top vs. middle vs. near-substrate)
  - Simple porosity/defect flags from indentation behavior (e.g., anomalously low HV or spalling under the indenter) as qualitative indicators
- Optional process data capture: melt pool power/speed logs for traceability.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
- Excessively large hatch spacing may cause lack-of-fusion porosity leading to erratic hardness and potential build failure; excessively small spacing may induce overheating, cracking, or compositional shifts in NiTi.
Mitigation: start with moderate spacings (0.45–0.60 mm) known to be stable in your prior runs, then expand outward; stop criteria if visual spatter/balling or delamination occurs.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Assumes availability of a compatible substrate plate (e.g., NiTi or another qualified substrate) and standard DED fixturing; this substrate is not listed in the provided materials but is typically required. If not available, this must be procured or the experiment cannot proceed.

- Source Papers Used:
- paper_005_chunk_01

Build a matrix of DED NiTi cubic coupons while varying hatch spacing only, then perform cross-sectional Vickers microhardness mapping to quantify how hatch spacing influences hardness distribution across and through the build.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps (DED build → sectioning → mounting → grinding/polishing → microhardness mapping) are supported by the provided DED machine, precision saw, mounting press with resin, grinding/polishing kit, and microhardness tester. Argon gas supports shielding. No etching is strictly required for microhardness as long as a high-quality polish is achieved.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From “paper_005_chunk_01”: (1) Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi processing window, and (2) polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with standard metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Within one controlled build job, produce four nominally identical 10 mm × 10 mm × 5 mm blocks, each with a distinct hatch spacing, while holding laser power, scan speed, layer thickness, and shielding identical. Perform high-density 2D microhardness mapping (e.g., 0.5 mm grid) across the mid-height cross-section of each coupon to resolve both through-thickness gradients and lateral variations between hatch tracks. This isolates hatch spacing effects without confounding changes to linear energy input.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine
- Precision saw
- Mounting press
- Grinding and polishing kit
- Microhardness tester

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder
- Argon gas
- Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Hatch spacing (primary sweep): propose four levels, e.g., 0.30 mm, 0.45 mm, 0.60 mm, 0.75 mm
- Hold constant across all coupons:
  - Laser power (e.g., a value within your established stable NiTi DED window)
  - Scan speed (fixed)
  - Layer thickness (fixed, e.g., 0.3 mm or as per your standard NiTi recipe)
  - Shielding gas flow/composition (argon; fixed flow rate)
  - Scan strategy (e.g., bidirectional, 90° rotation between layers if that is your standard—kept identical for all)
  - Substrate preheat and interlayer dwell (if used in your standard practice—kept identical for all)

- Measurements / Outputs:
- Cross-section preparation: cut each coupon at mid-length; mount; grind to 1200–4000 grit; polish to 1 µm finish (no etch required).
- Microhardness mapping: Vickers HV0.2 (or your lab’s standard load) on a 0.5 mm × 0.5 mm grid across the cross-section, avoiding edges by ≥2.5× indent diagonal spacing; record at least 8×8 indents per coupon.
- Outputs:
  - Mean HV and standard deviation per coupon
  - 2D hardness contour maps to visualize lateral variation tied to hatch spacing
  - Through-thickness hardness profiles (top vs. middle vs. near-substrate)
  - Simple porosity/defect flags from indentation behavior (e.g., anomalously low HV or spalling under the indenter) as qualitative indicators
- Optional process data capture: melt pool power/speed logs for traceability.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
- Excessively large hatch spacing may cause lack-of-fusion porosity leading to erratic hardness and potential build failure; excessively small spacing may induce overheating, cracking, or compositional shifts in NiTi.
Mitigation: start with moderate spacings (0.45–0.60 mm) known to be stable in your prior runs, then expand outward; stop criteria if visual spatter/balling or delamination occurs.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Assumes availability of a compatible substrate plate (e.g., NiTi or another qualified substrate) and standard DED fixturing; this substrate is not listed in the provided materials but is typically required. If not available, this must be procured or the experiment cannot proceed.

- Source Papers Used:
- paper_005_chunk_01

evidence.text: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Fabricate three identical DED NiTi cubic coupons while varying only hatch spacing, expressed as a fraction of the measured melt pool width (overlap-controlled hatch spacing), and quantify the effect on microhardness via cross-sectional hardness mapping.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  The BOM includes a DED machine (to control hatch spacing and hold other parameters constant), argon shielding, NiTi powder, and full metallography prep and microhardness capability for polished cross-sections. No additional analytics are strictly required to measure microhardness trends.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  From “paper_005_chunk_01”: 
  - Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window.
  - Polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with standard metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  Two-step protocol to normalize hatch spacing to melt pool width:
  1) Run single-track beads at the selected power/speed to measure resulting melt pool width on polished cross-sections.
  2) Define hatch spacing levels as 0.3W, 0.5W, and 0.7W (W = measured melt pool width), to compare microhardness at low, medium, and high overlap while holding all other parameters fixed. This overlap-based normalization helps isolate hatch spacing effects across potentially varying melt pool sizes.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Microhardness tester
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Abrasive papers and polishing media included in the kit

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Hatch spacing = {0.3W, 0.5W, 0.7W}, where W is melt pool width measured from preliminary single-track deposits.
  - Hold constant across all builds:
    - Laser power (single selected value within the known stable DED NiTi window)
    - Scan speed (fixed)
    - Layer thickness (fixed)
    - Shielding gas flow and composition (argon; fixed)
    - Scan strategy (e.g., 90° rotation between layers; fixed)
    - Interlayer dwell/time between layers (fixed)
    - Powder feed rate (fixed)
  - Preliminary step to determine W:
    - Deposit 3–5 single tracks at the chosen power/speed on a flat substrate, section one track, polish, and measure track width using the microhardness tester’s viewing optics or a simple optical readout.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Prepare mid-plane cross-sections of each coupon:
    - Section, mount, grind, and polish without etch (etch not required for hardness).
  - Microhardness mapping:
    - Vickers microhardness (e.g., HV0.5 or HV0.2) on a predefined grid:
      - At least three rows: near-top (within ~1 mm of top surface), mid-height, and near-bottom (within ~1 mm of substrate interface if applicable).
      - Grid pitch ~0.5–1.0 mm, with ≥20 indents per region per sample.
    - Outputs:
      - Mean and standard deviation of hardness per region and overall per coupon.
      - Through-thickness hardness gradients.
      - Hardness uniformity vs. hatch spacing (proxy for thermal history and overlap quality).
  - Optional qualitative notes (no extra equipment): observe any macro porosity or lack of fusion if visible during polishing/under the microhardness scope optics.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Very small hatch spacing (high overlap) may cause overheating, Ni evaporation, or excessive remelting, altering composition and reducing hardness uniformity.
  - Very large hatch spacing (low overlap) may induce lack-of-fusion defects that depress local hardness and cause high scatter.
  - Mitigation: use the single-track-derived W to limit the tested range to overlap ratios commonly stable in NiTi (e.g., 30–70%), and verify build stability on a small pilot coupon before full mapping.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the microhardness tester provides basic optical viewing to position indents and allow simple dimensional measurements of the polished single-track width. If it does not, an external optical microscope would be needed for accurate W; in that case, W could be approximated from track spacing/overlap trials, acknowledging added uncertainty.
  - Assumes the DED system can reliably set hatch spacing and maintain argon shielding adequate for NiTi.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Fabricate three adjacent DED NiTi coupons with identical process parameters except hatch spacing, then prepare cross-sections and perform Vickers microhardness mapping to quantify how hatch spacing influences hardness across and through the build.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps (DED deposition, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and microhardness testing) are directly supported by the listed equipment and materials, and the literature card indicates hatch spacing can be varied and that polished cross-sections are appropriate for microhardness mapping.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From "paper_005_chunk_01":
• Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window.
• Polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Within a single build session, produce three side-by-side rectangular coupons (to keep thermal history and powder lot constant) each with a distinct hatch spacing, then map microhardness both vertically (build height) and laterally (across multiple tracks) to capture gradients attributable to remelt/overlap differences caused by hatch spacing. Include a small overlap region between coupons to check for edge effects on hardness.

- Needed Equipment:
• DED machine
• Precision saw
• Mounting press
• Grinding and polishing kit
• Microhardness tester

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
• NiTi powder
• Argon gas
• Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
• Hatch spacing (independent variable): e.g., 0.20 mm, 0.30 mm, 0.40 mm
Held constant across all coupons:
• Laser power: fixed (e.g., select a value previously validated for sound NiTi in your shop)
• Scan speed: fixed
• Layer thickness: fixed
• Shielding gas: argon with constant flow rate
• Scan strategy: same pattern across coupons (e.g., 90° rotation each layer or unidirectional raster), same dwell/turn parameters
• Track order: maintain consistent sequence per layer for all coupons
Replicates: fabricate two layers of coupons per hatch spacing (i.e., 2 coupons per condition) to assess repeatability within the same build.

- Measurements / Outputs:
• Vickers microhardness (e.g., HV0.2) maps on polished cross-sections:
  - Vertical line profiles: indents every 300 µm from substrate interface to top surface at the coupon mid-width.
  - Lateral line profiles: indents every 150–200 µm across the coupon width at mid-height to sample multiple tracks/overlaps.
  - Target ≥50 indents per coupon to capture variability.
• Derived metrics:
  - Mean hardness and standard deviation per coupon
  - Hardness vs. build height slope
  - Hardness spatial variance across tracks (indicator of microstructural heterogeneity with hatch spacing)
• Build notes:
  - Qualitative observations of surface quality and visible lack-of-fusion during sectioning/polishing (documented in lab notes).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
At the largest hatch spacing, lack-of-fusion porosity may occur, causing poor polish and scattered hardness data; at the smallest spacing, excessive remelting may cause keyholing or distortion. Mitigation: begin with a pilot layer to verify track overlap, and adjust hatch spacing bounds if severe defects are observed.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
Assume the DED machine’s path-planning allows setting hatch spacing per coupon/region within a single build. If not, run three separate builds with identical settings except hatch spacing.

- Source Papers Used:
• paper_005_chunk_01

evidence.text: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Build a single NiTi coupon subdivided into four 10 mm × 10 mm regions, each processed with a different hatch spacing while keeping all other DED parameters constant. After sectioning and metallographic preparation, perform Vickers microhardness mapping on polished cross-sections to quantify how hatch spacing affects local and through-thickness hardness.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps (DED build, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and microhardness testing) are supported by the listed equipment and consumables. Argon shielding and NiTi powder are available for processing, and no external characterization tools beyond the microhardness tester are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Implement a “multi-zone” coupon where four hatch spacings are printed side-by-side in a single build to reduce material/time while ensuring identical global thermal boundary conditions. Add a buffer band (~1 mm untested margin) around each region to limit edge effects during microhardness mapping.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine
- Precision saw
- Mounting press
- Grinding and polishing kit
- Microhardness tester

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder
- Argon gas
- Mounting resin
- Standard grinding papers (e.g., SiC) and polishing media (e.g., diamond suspensions/alumina) compatible with the kit

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Primary sweep: Hatch spacing (Hs) at four levels around your lab’s current baseline H0, for example:
  - Hs1 = 0.6·H0
  - Hs2 = 0.8·H0
  - Hs3 = 1.0·H0 (baseline)
  - Hs4 = 1.2·H0
- Hold constant across all regions:
  - Laser power (e.g., fixed per your qualified NiTi recipe)
  - Scan speed
  - Powder feed rate
  - Layer thickness
  - Shielding gas flow
  - Scan strategy (e.g., bidirectional with fixed rotation per layer)
  - Substrate, standoff, and track overlap strategy other than hatch spacing

Build geometry:
- Single coupon: ~20 mm × 20 mm × 6–8 mm
- Four 10 mm × 10 mm interior regions, each assigned one Hs level
- 1 mm perimeter margin to mitigate edge effects (no hardness mapping in margin)

- Measurements / Outputs:
- Cross-sectioning: Section the coupon normal to the build direction to expose the hatch planes at mid-length of each region.
- Metallographic prep: Standard grind (e.g., 240→1200 grit) and polish to 1 µm finish.
- Microhardness mapping:
  - Vickers HV0.2 (or load appropriate for your tester to yield ~30–60 µm indents without overlap).
  - For each region, perform a 5 × 10 grid (50 indents) with 250 µm spacing covering mid-thickness; add one vertical line scan (10 indents at 200–300 µm spacing) from near-substrate to top to probe through-thickness variation.
- Outputs:
  - Mean and standard deviation of HV for each Hs
  - Hardness gradients (top vs near-substrate)
  - Spatial hardness heatmaps to visualize heterogeneity
  - Qualitative notes on surface/porosity indications inferred from indent quality and any visible pullouts after polish

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
At the largest hatch spacing, insufficient overlap may produce lack-of-fusion porosity, causing scattered or artificially low hardness. At the smallest spacing, overheating or keyholing could coarsen microstructure or induce defects that also skew hardness. Mitigation: choose Hs bounds within the lab’s previously stable NiTi DED window and verify track continuity during a short trial pass before the full coupon.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Assumes the DED machine can assign per-region parameter sets within one job; if not, run four separate small coupons under identical conditions.
- Assumes the microhardness tester includes optical imaging sufficient to place and verify indents; if not, an optical microscope would be needed (not listed in BOM).

- paper_005_chunk_01

content.text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Build a single NiTi coupon subdivided into four 10 mm × 10 mm regions, each processed with a different hatch spacing while keeping all other DED parameters constant. After sectioning and metallographic preparation, perform Vickers microhardness mapping on polished cross-sections to quantify how hatch spacing affects local and through-thickness hardness.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps (DED build, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and microhardness testing) are supported by the listed equipment and consumables. Argon shielding and NiTi powder are available for processing, and no external characterization tools beyond the microhardness tester are required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Implement a “multi-zone” coupon where four hatch spacings are printed side-by-side in a single build to reduce material/time while ensuring identical global thermal boundary conditions. Add a buffer band (~1 mm untested margin) around each region to limit edge effects during microhardness mapping.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine
- Precision saw
- Mounting press
- Grinding and polishing kit
- Microhardness tester

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder
- Argon gas
- Mounting resin
- Standard grinding papers (e.g., SiC) and polishing media (e.g., diamond suspensions/alumina) compatible with the kit

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Primary sweep: Hatch spacing (Hs) at four levels around your lab’s current baseline H0, for example:
  - Hs1 = 0.6·H0
  - Hs2 = 0.8·H0
  - Hs3 = 1.0·H0 (baseline)
  - Hs4 = 1.2·H0
- Hold constant across all regions:
  - Laser power (e.g., fixed per your qualified NiTi recipe)
  - Scan speed
  - Powder feed rate
  - Layer thickness
  - Shielding gas flow
  - Scan strategy (e.g., bidirectional with fixed rotation per layer)
  - Substrate, standoff, and track overlap strategy other than hatch spacing

Build geometry:
- Single coupon: ~20 mm × 20 mm × 6–8 mm
- Four 10 mm × 10 mm interior regions, each assigned one Hs level
- 1 mm perimeter margin to mitigate edge effects (no hardness mapping in margin)

- Measurements / Outputs:
- Cross-sectioning: Section the coupon normal to the build direction to expose the hatch planes at mid-length of each region.
- Metallographic prep: Standard grind (e.g., 240→1200 grit) and polish to 1 µm finish.
- Microhardness mapping:
  - Vickers HV0.2 (or load appropriate for your tester to yield ~30–60 µm indents without overlap).
  - For each region, perform a 5 × 10 grid (50 indents) with 250 µm spacing covering mid-thickness; add one vertical line scan (10 indents at 200–300 µm spacing) from near-substrate to top to probe through-thickness variation.
- Outputs:
  - Mean and standard deviation of HV for each Hs
  - Hardness gradients (top vs near-substrate)
  - Spatial hardness heatmaps to visualize heterogeneity
  - Qualitative notes on surface/porosity indications inferred from indent quality and any visible pullouts after polish

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
At the largest hatch spacing, insufficient overlap may produce lack-of-fusion porosity, causing scattered or artificially low hardness. At the smallest spacing, overheating or keyholing could coarsen microstructure or induce defects that also skew hardness. Mitigation: choose Hs bounds within the lab’s previously stable NiTi DED window and verify track continuity during a short trial pass before the full coupon.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Assumes the DED machine can assign per-region parameter sets within one job; if not, run four separate small coupons under identical conditions.
- Assumes the microhardness tester includes optical imaging sufficient to place and verify indents; if not, an optical microscope would be needed (not listed in BOM).

- Source Papers Used:
- paper_005_chunk_01

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how hatch spacing affects microhardness in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric hatch spacing levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

evidence.text: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a series of small DED NiTi pads while sweeping hatch spacing, then prepare polished cross-sections and perform Vickers microhardness mapping to quantify how overlap ratio (via hatch spacing) affects local and average hardness.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps (DED fabrication, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and microhardness testing) are directly supported by the listed equipment and materials. Argon shielding is available for DED. No external characterization beyond microhardness is required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From "paper_005_chunk_01": (a) Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and (b) polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Normalize hatch spacing to the machine- and parameter-specific melt pool width measured on this system via a short single-track calibration step. This controls the actual overlap ratio across all test conditions.
  - Map microhardness separately in track-center vs. overlap regions within each pad to isolate local thermal history effects of hatch spacing, not just bulk averages.
  - Capture hardness through build height (bottom/mid/top layers) to see whether hatch spacing interacts with reheating from subsequent layers.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit
  - Microhardness tester (with built-in optics for indent placement/spacing)

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Standard metallography papers/cloths and polishing suspensions (as part of the kit)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Hatch spacing set as a fraction of measured melt pool width (w): HS = {0.4w, 0.6w, 0.8w, 1.0w}. This spans high overlap to zero-overlap/underlap conditions within a stable window.
  - Held constant across builds (example constants; adjust to known stable NiTi settings on your DED):
    - Laser power: fixed (e.g., P0)
    - Scan speed: fixed (e.g., V0)
    - Layer thickness: fixed (e.g., t0)
    - Shielding: argon, constant flow setpoint
    - Scan strategy: bidirectional, 90° rotation between layers disabled (keep single orientation to isolate hatch-spacing effect)
    - Inter-track and inter-layer dwell: fixed
  - Calibration step to determine w:
    - Print 5 single beads (same P0, V0, t0). Cross-section one bead, polish, and use the microhardness tester optics to measure the as-solidified bead/melt pool width. Average to obtain w.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Specimens: For each HS, fabricate one 10 mm × 10 mm × 5 mm pad (enough area to host multiple tracks per layer).
  - Metallography:
    - Precision saw to section a mid-plane cross-section normal to hatch direction.
    - Mount, grind, and polish to a mirror finish suitable for microhardness.
  - Microhardness mapping (Vickers):
    - Three vertical lines of indents: near left edge (track-center biased), mid-pad (captures overlap region), and right edge (track-center biased).
    - Spacing: maintain ≥3× diagonal distance between indents to avoid interaction; typical 200–300 µm pitch depending on load.
    - Through-thickness: rows at ~0.5 mm from bottom, mid-height, and ~0.5 mm from top to capture reheating effects.
    - Recommended load: choose a load (e.g., 100–300 gf) yielding indents small enough to resolve gradients without overlap.
  - Outputs:
    - Average hardness per pad and standard deviation.
    - Hardness profiles vs. position (center vs. overlap) and vs. height (bottom/middle/top).
    - Correlation of hardness with overlap ratio (computed as 1 − HS/w).
    - Qualitative notes on visible lack-of-fusion or keyhole indications in the polished cross-sections (as observable via the tester’s optics).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - At large HS (e.g., ~1.0w), lack-of-fusion or porosity may occur, compromising hardness interpretation or preventing polishability. Mitigation: cap the maximum HS at the largest value that maintains contiguous tracks after a quick pilot build; if necessary, exclude the most defective condition from analysis but document it.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the microhardness tester includes basic optical imaging/reticle to position indents and measure bead width and indent diagonals (common for such instruments). If not available, an external optical microscope would be needed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

evidence.text: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Build three NiTi DED coupons with identical process settings except for hatch spacing, then prepare polished cross-sections and perform microhardness mapping to quantify how hatch spacing influences hardness distribution through the build.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
All steps (DED build, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, microhardness testing) are supported by the listed equipment and materials. Argon shielding is available. No characterization beyond microhardness and basic metallography is required.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From “paper_005_chunk_01”: (1) Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi processing window. (2) Polished cross-sections are suitable for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Systematically sweep hatch spacing across a practical DED range while holding other parameters constant, and map microhardness both laterally (across multiple hatch tracks) and vertically (near-substrate, mid-height, top) to separate overlap-induced hardening from thermal-history gradients within the same coupon geometry.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine
- Precision saw
- Mounting press
- Grinding and polishing kit
- Microhardness tester

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder
- Argon gas
- Mounting resin
- Grinding/polishing media and lubricants (assumed part of the kit)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Primary variable:
  - Hatch spacing (center-to-center): 0.30 mm, 0.45 mm, 0.60 mm
- Held constant across all builds:
  - Laser power: fixed (select within known stable NiTi DED window used at your site)
  - Scan speed: fixed
  - Layer thickness: fixed
  - Powder feed rate: fixed
  - Shielding argon flow: fixed
  - Scan strategy: bidirectional stripes with 90° rotation between layers
  - Interlayer dwell: fixed time (e.g., 30–60 s) to normalize thermal history
  - Build geometry: 12 mm × 12 mm × 10 mm prisms; 3 coupons total (one per hatch spacing)
  - Substrate preheat: if available, keep constant; if not, document ambient condition

- Measurements / Outputs:
- Sectioning: Cut each coupon to expose a central cross-section perpendicular to the hatch direction.
- Metallography: Mount, grind, and polish to a mirror finish suitable for microhardness.
- Microhardness mapping:
  - Vickers microhardness (e.g., HV0.2, 15 s dwell; adjust to your instrument’s standards).
  - Three depth bands per coupon: 0.5–1.0 mm from the substrate (bottom), mid-height, and top 1 mm.
  - In each band, perform a 5 × 5 grid of indents with 200–300 µm spacing aligned to span multiple hatch tracks.
- Outputs:
  - Mean and standard deviation of hardness per band and per hatch spacing.
  - Lateral hardness profiles across hatch tracks to assess overlap effects.
  - Vertical hardness gradients (bottom to top) to assess thermal-history effects.
  - Compare hardness distributions versus hatch spacing to identify an optimal overlap range.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
- Too-small hatch spacing can cause excessive remelting/heat accumulation, leading to distortion or cracking; too-large spacing can leave lack-of-fusion porosity that depresses hardness and causes erratic indents. Mitigation: start with the mid value (0.45 mm) to validate stability, verify track consolidation visually during build, and abort/adjust if lack of fusion or overheating is observed.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Substrate/baseplate material is not listed in the BOM. Assumption: a compatible substrate (e.g., NiTi or steel plate) and standard DED fixturing are available in the lab.
- No optical microscope is listed; indenter optics will be used only for placement/spacing. Quantitative porosity assessment is not included.

- Source Papers Used:
- paper_005_chunk_01

  From "paper_005_chunk_01": Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  A two-step approach: 
  1) Pilot single-track pass to estimate as-built track width using the microhardness tester’s optics for measurement after polishing. 
  2) Define hatch spacings as fractions of the measured track width (e.g., 40%, 60%, 80%, 100%) to ensure the sweep spans under- to near-full overlap without exceeding stability, then build multi-track, multi-layer pads at each spacing on a single plate for direct comparison.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Microhardness tester (used for both indenting and optical measurement/placement)
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin
  - Assumed: standard metallic substrate plate compatible with the DED system (e.g., steel or Ni-based), fixtures, and standard DED nozzles

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary sweep: Hatch spacing (as fraction of measured single-track width): 0.4W, 0.6W, 0.8W, 1.0W, where W is the pilot-measured track width
  - Held constant across all pads:
    - Laser power (fixed value within stable DED NiTi window)
    - Scan speed (fixed)
    - Layer thickness (e.g., constant nominal, such as 0.3 mm; exact per machine capability)
    - Powder feed rate (fixed)
    - Shielding gas flow (argon; fixed)
    - Scan strategy (bidirectional raster), scan rotation between layers (e.g., 90°), and number of layers (e.g., 5 layers) to reach ~2–3 mm thickness

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Microhardness (Vickers, e.g., HV0.2): 
    - For each pad, mount and polish a cross-section normal to the scan direction.
    - Create a hardness map: a lateral line at mid-height (e.g., 15 indents at 200–300 µm spacing) and a vertical line through thickness (e.g., 10 indents at 200–300 µm spacing).
    - Report mean ± SD per pad, lateral variability (edge vs center), and vertical gradient.
  - Pilot metric: Single-track width W measured after polishing using the microhardness tester’s optics.
  - Quality proxy: Fraction of rejected indents due to pores/defects (if indent straddles a void), as a simple porosity surrogate across hatch spacings.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Too-large hatch spacing may cause lack of fusion and high porosity, yielding scattered hardness or unusable pads; too-small spacing may cause overheating, distortion, or excessive remelting. Mitigation: choose fractions around the measured W and perform a quick visual/optical check of the first layer tracks before completing all layers.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible substrate plate and standard DED fixturing; these are not listed in the BOM but are typically part of DED operations.
  - Assumes the microhardness tester provides sufficient optical magnification to measure track width and place indents accurately; if not, an optical microscope would be needed.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how hatch spacing affects microhardness in a DED NiTi process.

constraints: Use only listed equipment and materials.
Use explicit numeric hatch spacing levels.
Keep all non-target process settings fixed.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
Fabricate a set of small DED NiTi coupons while sweeping hatch spacing, then prepare polished cross-sections and perform microhardness mapping to quantify how hatch spacing influences hardness distribution and average hardness.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
The DED machine can vary hatch spacing within a stable NiTi processing window, and the listed metallography tools (precision saw, mounting press, grinding/polishing kit) plus the microhardness tester are sufficient to produce and test cross-sections. Argon gas provides shielding during deposition. All required materials and equipment are present in the BOM.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
From “paper_005_chunk_01”: (1) Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window. (2) Polished cross-sections are appropriate for microhardness mapping after standard metallographic preparation with the listed tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
Implement a single-build “parameter island” coupon comprising five adjacent 8×8×5 mm regions, each printed with a different hatch spacing while holding all other parameters constant. This reduces powder use and build time, keeps thermal history comparable, and enables side-by-side microhardness mapping across regions in one mount. Within each region, apply the same raster orientation (e.g., 0/90° layer rotation) so that hatch spacing is the only varied factor.

- Needed Equipment:
- DED machine
- Microhardness tester
- Precision saw
- Mounting press
- Grinding and polishing kit

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
- NiTi powder
- Argon gas
- Mounting resin
- Standard grinding papers and polishing suspensions/cloths (as part of the kit)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
- Hatch spacing (primary factor): five levels, e.g., 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.35, 0.40 mm (values chosen to span from high-overlap to low-overlap while remaining within a stable processing window).
- Hold constant across all regions:
  - Laser power (one value within your established stable window)
  - Scan speed (fixed)
  - Layer thickness (fixed)
  - Shielding gas flow and environment (argon)
  - Raster strategy: bidirectional with 0/90° rotation between layers
  - Interlayer dwell, if any

- Measurements / Outputs:
- Prepare a single cross-section that intersects all five parameter islands:
  - Section perpendicular to the build direction through the mid-plane of the islands.
  - Mount, grind, and polish to mirror finish.
- Microhardness mapping:
  - Vickers microhardness with a fixed load (e.g., HV0.3 or HV0.5; choose based on your indenter and surface finish).
  - For each island: a 2D grid of indents spanning from top to near-substrate and from interior to edge (e.g., 5×5 grid with ≥3 diagonal indent spacings between indents to avoid interaction).
  - Outputs: mean hardness per island, standard deviation, vertical hardness gradient, and edge-to-center variation.
- Optional derived metrics:
  - Hardness vs. nominal overlap fraction (computed from track width estimate used in your process setup).
  - ANOVA or simple trend analysis of hardness versus hatch spacing.

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
- Excessive porosity or lack of fusion at the largest hatch spacing could distort hardness trends. Mitigation: keep the upper spacing within the known stable window and halt if visual bead integrity degrades.
- Ni evaporation or composition drift could alter hardness independently of hatch spacing. Mitigation: maintain robust argon shielding and consistent parameter settings; compare all islands within a single build to minimize inter-build variability.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
- Assumes the DED machine allows region-wise parameter assignment (parameter islands) within one build file; if not, fabricate five separate coupons under identical overall conditions.
- Assumes the microhardness tester can place and measure a grid of indents without external microscopy (most microhardness systems include in-situ optics; if not, manual stage positioning will be used).

- Source Papers Used:
- paper_005_chunk_01

task.goal: Design one feasible experiment to study how hatch spacing affects microhardness in a DED NiTi process.

evidence.text: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

response_text:
- Candidate Experiment:
  Build three replicate NiTi DED pads at fixed power/speed/layer thickness while sweeping hatch spacing, then prepare polished cross-sections and perform Vickers microhardness mapping across and through the build to quantify how hatch spacing affects hardness distributions.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  The DED machine can vary hatch spacing within a stable processing window for NiTi. After fabrication, the precision saw, mounting press with mounting resin, and grinding/polishing kit enable cross-section preparation. The microhardness tester enables indentation mapping on the polished sections. Argon provides shielding during deposition.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Systematic, side-by-side microhardness mapping across multiple depths and across hatch overlap regions for three hatch spacings while holding all other process parameters constant, using a consistent indentation grid oriented both parallel and perpendicular to scan vectors to probe local overlap effects.
  - Include replicate pads per condition to separate process-induced variability from spacing effects using only hardness statistics (mean, standard deviation, and spatial gradients) that do not require additional imaging tools.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit
  - Microhardness tester

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas
  - Mounting resin

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Primary variable: Hatch spacing (three levels, e.g., 0.30 mm, 0.40 mm, 0.50 mm).
  - Held constant across all builds:
    - Laser power, scan speed, and layer thickness set within the stable NiTi DED window referenced by the literature precedent.
    - Shielding gas: argon flow as per machine’s standard for NiTi.
    - Scan strategy: bidirectional raster with 90° rotation between successive layers.
    - Build geometry: pads ~12 mm × 12 mm × 5 mm tall; 3 replicates per hatch spacing.
  - Suggested sample count: 3 hatch spacings × 3 replicates = 9 pads.

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Sample preparation: Section each pad at mid-length perpendicular to scan vectors; mount and polish to a mirror finish suitable for indentation.
  - Microhardness mapping:
    - Vickers indents (e.g., HV0.2 or HV0.3) on a 2D grid.
    - Through-thickness line scans: from substrate interface (or first deposited layers) to top surface at 0.5 mm step.
    - In-plane overlap mapping: a 6 × 6 grid with 250 µm spacing centered on a representative region to capture track overlap effects.
  - Outputs:
    - Mean and standard deviation of hardness per pad and per hatch spacing.
    - Hardness gradients vs. build height.
    - Spatial hardness variation across overlap regions.
    - ANOVA across hatch spacing groups to identify statistically significant differences (if basic stats are available; otherwise report descriptive statistics).

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Composition shift during DED (e.g., Ni evaporation) could alter transformation behavior and confound hardness trends independent of hatch spacing. Also, excessive hatch spacing may reduce overlap and increase lack-of-fusion defects, leading to erratic hardness or premature cracking in polishing.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes availability of a compatible substrate plate/fixture for DED (e.g., NiTi or steel base) and standard DED fixturing/leveling—these are not listed in the BOM.
  - Assumes the microhardness tester includes the optical measurement capability required to read indent diagonals (typical of such instruments).

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01

- Candidate Experiment:
  Build a single DED NiTi multi-zone pad with three programmed hatch spacings (0.3 mm, 0.6 mm, 0.9 mm) under otherwise identical parameters, then prepare a transverse cross-section and perform Vickers microhardness mapping across and through each zone to quantify the effect of hatch spacing on hardness.

- Why This Is Feasible With Current BOM:
  All steps—DED building under argon, sectioning, mounting, grinding/polishing, and Vickers microhardness testing—are supported by the listed materials and equipment.

- Borrowed Literature Precedents:
  - From “paper_005_chunk_01”: Hatch spacing can be varied within a stable DED NiTi window, and polished cross-sections can be used for microhardness mapping after preparation with the listed metallography tools.

- New Adaptation / Novel Twist:
  - Implement three hatch spacings as adjacent zones within a single 20 mm × 20 mm × 5 mm pad to minimize build-to-build variability and enable direct, same-build comparison across hatch spacings.
  - Use a standardized microhardness grid that spans zone boundaries to detect gradients near overlap regions versus track centers within each hatch-spacing condition.

- Needed Equipment:
  - DED machine
  - Precision saw
  - Mounting press
  - Grinding and polishing kit
  - Microhardness tester

- Needed Materials / Consumables:
  - NiTi powder
  - Argon gas (shielding and carrier as needed)
  - Mounting resin
  - Standard SiC papers and polishing suspensions/cloths (assumed part of grinding/polishing kit)

- Key Process Parameters To Sweep:
  - Hatch spacing (primary variable): 0.3 mm, 0.6 mm, 0.9 mm (three adjacent zones within one pad)
  - Hold constant for all zones:
    - Laser power: e.g., 300 W (fixed)
    - Scan speed: e.g., 600 mm/min (fixed)
    - Powder feed rate: e.g., 6 g/min (fixed)
    - Layer thickness: 0.3 mm (fixed)
    - Shielding argon flow: per machine recommendation (fixed)
    - Scan strategy: unidirectional with 90° rotation between layers (fixed)
    - Substrate preheat: not used unless standard on machine; if available, keep constant
  - Build geometry: Single pad 20 × 20 × 5 mm subdivided into three 20 × ~6.5 × 5 mm zones, each with assigned hatch spacing

- Measurements / Outputs:
  - Sample prep: Section the pad perpendicular to scan direction to expose cross-sections of all three zones in one mount; grind/polish to a mirror finish.
  - Microhardness: Vickers HV0.2 (200 g load), 10 s dwell.
    - For each zone: 2D grid of indents (e.g., 5 columns × 6 rows, 0.3 mm spacing between indents) covering from near-surface to mid-thickness and capturing multiple hatch overlaps.
    - Optional line traverses across zone boundaries to check thermal cross-talk.
  - Outputs:
    - Mean and standard deviation of hardness per zone
    - Hardness maps highlighting local variations near track overlaps vs. inter-track regions
    - Trend of hardness versus hatch spacing

- Main Risk / Failure Mode:
  - Thermal cross-talk between adjacent zones could blur differences due to shared heat flow, especially in early layers. Mitigation: print zones sequentially in layer-wise interleaved order but always complete a full layer across all zones before proceeding to the next, and maintain constant interpass dwell.

- Missing Capability / Assumption:
  - Assumes the DED machine’s toolpath software can assign zone-specific hatch spacings within one build; if not supported, perform three separate small pads under identical conditions except hatch spacing.
  - No microscopy is listed; the plan relies on hardness mapping without microstructural imaging.

- Source Papers Used:
  - paper_005_chunk_01
